Now, check all the options.
Only {tex}(4 \sqrt{3}, 2 \sqrt{2}){/tex}, satisfy the above equation.

questions.question: If the vertices of a hyperbola be at (-2,0) and (2,0) and one of its foci be at (-3,0), then which one of the following points does not lie on this hyperbola?
questions.question_raw: If the vertices of a hyperbola be at (-2,0) and (2,0) and one of its foci be at (-3,0), then which one of the following points does not lie on this hyperbola?
questions.explanation:
The vertices of hyperbola are given as (± 2,0) and one of
its foci is at (- 3,0).
(a, 0) = (2,0) and (- ae, 0), = (- 3,0)
On comparing x-coordinates both sides, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2 and -ae = -3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
Also, {tex}\frac{9}{4}=1+\frac{b^{2}}{4} \Rightarrow b^{2}=5{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}e^{2}=1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}]
So, equation of the hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}-\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1{/tex} ....(i)
The point (6,5{tex}\sqrt2{/tex}) from the given options does not satisfy the above equation of hyperbola.

questions.question: If the circles $x^2+y^{2}+5 K x+2 y+K=0$ and $2\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)+2 K x+3 y-1=0, \;(K \in R)$ intersect at the points P and Q, then the line 4x + 5 y - K = 0 passes through P and Q, for
questions.question_raw: If the circles {tex}x^2+y^{2}+5 K x+2 y+K=0{/tex} and {tex}2\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)+2 K x+3 y-1=0, \;(K \in R){/tex} intersect at the points P and Q, then the line 4x + 5 y - K = 0 passes through P and Q, for
questions.explanation:
Equation of given circles
x2 + y2 + 5.Kx+ 2y + K = 0 ......(i)
and 2(x2 + y2) + 2.Kx+ 3y - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + Kx + {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}y - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0
On subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
4Kx + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} y + K + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8Kx +  y + (2K + 1) = 0 .......(iii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} if S1 = 0 and S2 = 0 be two circles, then their common chord is giyen by S1 -S2 = 0]
Eq. (iii) represents equation of common chord as it is given that circles (i) and (ii) intersects each other at points P and Q
Since, line 4x + 5 y - K = 0 passes through point P and Q.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{8 K}{4}=\frac{1}{5}=\frac{2 K+1}{-K}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} [equating first and second terms] 
and -K = 10K +5 [equating second and third terms]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11K + 5 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} K = - {tex}\frac{5}{11}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10} \neq-\frac{5}{11}{/tex}, so there is no such value of K, for which line
4x + 5y - K = 0 passes through points P and Q.

questions.question: The equation of the circle passing through the foci of $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ and having centre at (0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle passing through the foci of {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex} and having centre at (0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of ellipes is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}

Here, a = 4, b = 3, e = {tex}\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{16}} \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Foci = {tex}(\pm a e, 0)=\left(\pm 4 \times \frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}, 0\right)=(\pm \sqrt{7}, 0){/tex}
Radius of the circle, {tex}r=\sqrt{(a e)^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{7+9}=\sqrt{16}=4{/tex}
Now, equation of circle is (x - 0)2 + (y - 3)2 = 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 + y2 - 6y - 7 = 0

questions.question: If a hyperbola has length of its conjugate axis equal to 5 and the distance between its foci is 13, then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
questions.question_raw: If a hyperbola has length of its conjugate axis equal to 5 and the distance between its foci is 13, then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
questions.explanation:
We know that in {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, where b2= a2(e2- 1), the 
length of conjugate axis is 2b and distance between the foci is 2ae
According the problem, 2b = 5 and 2ae =13
Now, b2 - a2(e2 - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}=a^{2} e^{2}-a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{25}{4}=\frac{(2 a e)^{2}}{4}-a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a^{2}=\frac{169-25}{4}=\frac{144}{4}=36{/tex}  ( {tex}\because{/tex} 2ae=165)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 6
Now, 2ae = 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 \times 6 \times e=13{/tex}
{tex}e=\frac{13}{12}{/tex}

questions.question: If a tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 1 intersects the coordinate axes at distinct points P and Q, then the locus of the mid-point of PQ is
questions.question_raw: If a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = 1 intersects the coordinate axes at distinct points P and Q, then the locus of the mid-point of PQ is
questions.explanation:
Equation of given circle is x2 + y2 = 1, then equation of tangent at the point (cos{tex}\theta{/tex},sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) on the given circle is
x cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + y sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 ...(i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Equation of tangent at the point P (cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) to the circle x2 + y2 = r2 is x cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + y sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = r]
Now, the point of intersection with coordinate axes are P (sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, 0) and Q (0, cosec {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\because{/tex} Mid-point of line joining points P and Q is {tex}M\left(\frac{\sec \theta}{2}, \frac{\cos \operatorname{ec} \theta}{2}\right){/tex} = (h. k) (let)
So, cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 h}{/tex} and sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 k}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} sin2{tex}\theta{/tex} + cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{4 h ^2}+\frac{1}{4 k^{2}}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{h ^2}+\frac{1}{k ^2}=4 {/tex}
Now, locus of mid-point M is {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{y^{2}}{/tex} = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 4x2y2 = 0

questions.question: The ellipse $E_{1}: \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ is inscribed in a rectangle R whose sides are parallel to the coordinate axes. Another ellipse E_2 passing through the point (0, 4) circumscribes the rectangle R. The eccentricity of the ellipse E_2 is
questions.question_raw: The ellipse {tex}E_{1}: \frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} is inscribed in a rectangle R whose sides are parallel to the coordinate axes. Another ellipse E2 passing through the point (0, 4) circumscribes the rectangle R. The eccentricity of the ellipse E2 is
questions.explanation:
Plan: Equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} {tex}[\because a>b]{/tex}
Eocentricity {tex}e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}[\because a>b]{/tex}

Description of Situation: As ellipse circumscribes the rectangle, then it must pass through all four vertices.
Let the equation of an ellipse E2 be
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, where a < b and b = 4

Also, it passes through (3, 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{9}{a^{2}}+\frac{4}{b^{2}}=1 \quad[\because b=4]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{9}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{4}=1{/tex} or a2 = 12
Eccentricity of E2, {tex}e^{2}=1-\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}=1-\frac{12}{16}=\frac{1}{4} \quad[\because a<b]{/tex}
{tex}e =\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Let S = $\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2}: \frac{y^{2}}{1+r}-\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}=1\right\}$
where r $\ne \pm$ 1. Then, S represents
questions.question_raw:
Let S = {tex}\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2}: \frac{y^{2}}{1+r}-\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}=1\right\}{/tex}
where r {tex}\ne \pm{/tex} 1. Then, S represents

options.text: a hyperbola whose eccentricity is $\frac{2}{\sqrt{r+1}}$, when 0 < r < 1
a hyperbola whose eccentricity is $\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-r}}$, when 0 < r < 1
an ellipse whose eccentricity is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{r+1}}$, when r > 1
an ellipse whose eccentricity is $\sqrt{\frac{2}{r+1}}$, when r > 1

questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}S=\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2}: \frac{y^{2}}{1+r}-\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}=1\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}\left\{(x, y) \in R^{2} ; \frac{y^{2}}{1+r}+\frac{x^{2}}{r-1}=1\right\}{/tex}
For r > 1, {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}+\frac{x^{2}}{r-1}{/tex} = 1, represents a vertical ellipse. [{tex}\because{/tex} for r > 1, r - 1 < r + 1 and r - 1 > 0]
Now, eccentricity (e) = {tex}\sqrt{1-\frac{r-1}{r+1}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} For {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1, a < b, e {tex}=\sqrt{1-\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}}{/tex}]
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(r+1)-(r-1)}{r+1}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2}{r+1}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a variable line $3 x+4 y-\lambda=0$ is such that the two circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y+1=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-18 x-2 y+78=0$ are on its opposite sides, then the set of all values of $\lambda$ is the interval
questions.question_raw: If a variable line {tex}3 x+4 y-\lambda=0{/tex} is such that the two circles {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y+1=0{/tex} and {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-18 x-2 y+78=0{/tex} are on its opposite sides, then the set of all values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the interval
questions.explanation:
The given circles,
x2 + y2 - 2x - 2y + 1 = 0 ....(i)
and  x2 + y2 - 18x - 2y + 78 = 0, ......(ii)
are on the opposite sides of the variable line 3x + 4y -{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0. So, their centres also lie on the opposite sides of the variable line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}[3(1)+4(1)-\lambda][3(9)+4(1)-\lambda]<0{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} The points P(x1, y1)] and Q(x2, y2) lie on the opposite sides of the line ax+ by + c = 0, if (ax1 + by1 + c) (ax2 + by2 + c) < 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(\lambda-7)(\lambda-31)<0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} (7, 31) .....(iii)
Also, we have {tex}\left|\frac{3(1)+4(1)-\lambda}{5}\right| \geq \sqrt{1+1-1}{/tex}
({tex}\because{/tex} Distance of centre from the given line is greater than the radius i.e. {tex}\frac{a x_{1}+b y_{1}+c}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}} \geq r{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|7-\lambda| \geq 5 \Rightarrow \lambda \in(-\infty, 2] \cup[12, \infty){/tex} ....(iv)
and {tex}\left|\frac{3(9)+4(1)-\lambda}{5}\right| \geq \sqrt{81+1-78}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\lambda-31| \geq 10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda \in(-\infty, 21] \cup[41, \infty){/tex} ....(v)
From Eqs. (iii), (iv) and (v), we get
{tex}\lambda \in{/tex}[12, 21]

questions.question: If P = (x, y), F_1 = (3, 0), F_2 = (-3, 0) and 16x^2 + 25y^2 = 400, then PF_1 + PF_2 equals
questions.question_raw: If P = (x, y), F1 = (3, 0), F2 = (-3, 0) and 16x2 + 25y2 = 400, then PF1 + PF2 equals
questions.explanation:
Given, 16x2  + 25y2 = 400 [given]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{16}=1{/tex}
Now, PF1 + PF2 = Major axis = 2a [where, a = 5}
= {tex}2 \times 5{/tex} = 10

questions.question: If the normal to the ellipse 3x^2 + 4y^2 = 12 at a point P on it is parallel to the line, 2x + y = 4 and the tangent to the ellipse at P passes through Q(4, 4) then PQ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the normal to the ellipse 3x2 + 4y2 = 12 at a point P on it is parallel to the line, 2x + y = 4 and the tangent to the ellipse at P passes through Q(4, 4) then PQ is equal to
questions.explanation:
Equation of given ellipse is 3x2 + 4y2 = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex} ...(i)
Now, let point P(2 cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}), so equation of tangent to ellipse (i) at point P is
{tex}\frac{x \cos \theta}{2}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{\sqrt{3}}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
Since, tangent (ii) passes through point Q(4, 4)
{tex}\therefore 2 \cos \theta+\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \sin \theta=1{/tex} ...(iii)
and equation of normal to ellipse (i) at point P is
{tex}\frac{4 x}{2 \cos \theta}-\frac{3y}{\sqrt{3} \sin \theta}=4-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2 x \sin \theta-\sqrt{3} \cos \theta y=\sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex} ...(iv)
Since, normal (iv) is parallel to line, 2x + y = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of normal (iv) is parallel to line, 2x + y = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \tan \theta=-2 \Rightarrow \tan \theta=-\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=120^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(\sin \theta, \cos \theta)=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2},-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
Hence, point {tex}P\left(-1, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex}
Now, PQ = {tex}=\sqrt{(4+1)^{2}+\left(4-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} [given cordinates of Q {tex}\equiv{/tex} (4, 4)]
{tex}=\sqrt{25+\frac{25}{4}}=\frac{5 \sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Let C_1 and C_2 be the centers of the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y-2=0 $ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-6 y+14=0$ respectively. If P and Q are the points of intersection of these circles, then the area (in sq units) of the quadrilateral PC_1QC_2 is
questions.question_raw: Let C1 and C2 be the centers of the circles {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-2 y-2=0 {/tex} and {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-6 y+14=0{/tex} respectively. If P and Q are the points of intersection of these circles, then the area (in sq units) of the quadrilateral PC1QC2 is
questions.explanation:
Given circles,
x2 + y2 - 2x - 2y - 2 = 0 .....(i)
and x2 + y2 - 6x - 6y + 14 = 0 .....(ii)
are intersecting each other orthogonally, because
2(1) (3) + 2(1) (3) = 14 - 2 [{tex}\because{/tex} two circles are intersected orthogonally if 2g1g2 + 2f1f2 = c1 + c2]

So, the area of quadrilateral
PC1QC2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} ar ({tex}\triangle{/tex} PC1C2)
= {tex}2 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2\right){/tex} = 4 sq units

questions.question: A hyperbola has its centre at the origin, passes through the point (4, 2) and has a transverse axis of length 4 along the X-axis. Then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
questions.question_raw: A hyperbola has its centre at the origin, passes through the point (4, 2) and has a transverse axis of length 4 along the X-axis. Then the eccentricity of the hyperbola is
questions.explanation:
Equation of hyperbola is given by
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Length of transverse axis = 2a = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2
Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} is the equation of hyperbola
{tex}\because{/tex} It passes through (4, 2).
{tex}\therefore \frac{16}{4}-\frac{4}{b^{2}}=1 \Rightarrow 4-\frac{4}{b^{2}}=1 \Rightarrow b^{2}=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow b=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Now, eccentricity,
{tex}e=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\frac{4}{3}}{4}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}.

questions.question: If the circles x^2 + y^2 - 16x - 20y + 164 = r^2 and $(x-4) ^2+(y-7)^{2}=36$ intersect at two distinct points, then
questions.question_raw: If the circles x2 + y2 - 16x - 20y + 164 = r2 and {tex}(x-4) ^2+(y-7)^{2}=36{/tex} intersect at two distinct points, then
questions.explanation:
Circle I is x2 + y2 - 16x - 20y + 164 = r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 8)2 + (y - 10)2 = r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C1 (8, 10) is the centre of lst circle and r1 = r is its radius
Circle II is (x - 4)2 + (y - 7)2 = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C2(4, 7) is the centre of 2nd circle 'and r2 = 6 is its radius.
Two circles intersects if |r1 - r2| < C1C2 < r1 + r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|r-6|<\sqrt{(8-4)^{2}+(10-7)^{2}}<r+6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|r-6|<\sqrt{16+9}<r+6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|r-6|<5<r+6{/tex}
Now as, 5 < r+6 always, we have to solve only 
{tex}|r-6|<5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-5<r-6<5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 - 5 < r < 5 + 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 < r < 11

questions.question: The tangent and normal to the ellipse 3x^2 + 5y^2 = 32 at the point P(2, 2) meets the X-axis at Q and R, respectively. Then, the area (in sq units) of the $\Delta$PQR is
questions.question_raw: The tangent and normal to the ellipse 3x2 + 5y2 = 32 at the point P(2, 2) meets the X-axis at Q and R, respectively. Then, the area (in sq units) of the {tex}\Delta{/tex}PQR is
questions.explanation:
Equation of given ellipse is 3x2 + 5y2 = 32 ...(i)
Now, the slope of tangent and normal at point P(2, 2) to the ellipse (i) are respectively
{tex}m_{T}=\frac{d y}{d x}|_{2,2} \text { and } m_{N}=-\left.\frac{d x}{d y}\right|_{2,2}{/tex}
On differentiating ellipse (i), w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}6 x+10 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{3 x}{5 y}{/tex}
So, {tex}m_{T}=-\left.\frac{3x}{5 y}\right|_{(2,2)}=-\frac{3}{5} \text { and } m_{N}=\left.\frac{5 y}{3 y}\right|_{(2,2)}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
Now, equation of tangent and normal to the given
ellipse (i) at point P(2, 2) are
{tex}(y-2)=-\frac{3}{5}(x-2){/tex}
and {tex}(y-2)=\frac{5}{3}(x-2){/tex} respectively.
It is given that point of intersection of tangent and normal are Q and R at X-axis respectively.
So, {tex}Q\left(\frac{1}{36}, 0\right) \text { and } R\left(\frac{4}{5}, 0\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \text { Area of } \Delta P Q R=\frac{1}{2}(Q R) \times \text { height }{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{68}{15} \times 2=\frac{68}{15} \text { sq units }{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}Q R=\sqrt{\left(\frac{16}{3}-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{68}{15}\right)^{2}}=\frac{68}{15}{/tex} andheight = 2]

questions.question: If the line x - 2y = 12 is tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ at the point $\left(3, \frac{-9}{2}\right)$, then the length of the latusrectum of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: If the line x - 2y = 12 is tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at the point {tex}\left(3, \frac{-9}{2}\right){/tex}, then the length of the latusrectum of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
Equation of given ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} ...(i)
Now, equation of tangent at the point {tex}\left(3,-\frac{9}{2}\right){/tex} on the ellipse (i) is
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 x}{a^{2}}-\frac{9 y}{2 b^{2}}=1{/tex} ...(ii) [{tex}\because{/tex} the equation of the tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at the point (x1, y1) is {tex}\frac{xx_{1}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}]
{tex}\because{/tex} Tangent (ii) represent the line x - 2y = 12, so
{tex}\frac{1}{\frac{3}{a^{2}}}=\frac{2}{\frac{9}{2 b^{2}}}=\frac{12}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 36 and b2 = 27
Now, Length of latusrectum = {tex}\frac{2 b_{2}}{a}=\frac{2 \times 27}{6}=9 \text { units }{/tex}

questions.question: Let 0 < $\theta$ < $\frac{\pi}{2}$. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \theta}-\frac{y^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \theta}$ = 1 is greater than 2, then the length of its latus rectum lies in the interval
questions.question_raw: Let 0 < {tex}\theta{/tex} < {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}. If the eccentricity of the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \theta}-\frac{y^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \theta}{/tex} = 1 is greater than 2, then the length of its latus rectum lies in the interval
questions.explanation:
For the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex},
e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For the given hyperbola,
e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\cos ^{2} \theta}}>2{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} {tex}a^{2}=\cos ^{2} \theta{/tex} and {tex}b^{2}=\sin ^{2} \theta{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+\tan ^{2} \theta>4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{2} \theta>3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \theta \in(-\infty,-\sqrt{3}) \cup(\sqrt{3}, \infty){/tex}
[{tex}\left|x^{2}>3 \Rightarrow\right| x |>\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x \in(-\infty,-\sqrt{3}) \cup(\sqrt{3}, \infty){/tex}]
But {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan{tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} ({tex}\sqrt3{/tex}, 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta \in\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} 
Now, length of latusrectum
= {tex}\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}{/tex} = {tex}2 \frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\cos \theta}{/tex} = 2sin{tex}\theta{/tex} tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
Since, both sin{tex}\theta{/tex} and tan{tex}\theta{/tex} are increasing functions in 
{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Least value of latus rectum is
{tex}2\sin \frac{\pi }{3}.\tan \frac{\pi }{3} = 2\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}.\sqrt 3 = 3{/tex} (at {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi }{3}{/tex})
and greatest value of latusrectum is < {tex}\infty{/tex}
Hence, latus rectum length {tex}\in{/tex} (3, {tex}\infty{/tex})

questions.question: If one of the diameters of the circle, given by the equation, x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0 is a chord of a circle S, whose centre is at (-3, 2), then the radius of S is
questions.question_raw: If one of the diameters of the circle, given by the equation, x2 + y2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0 is a chord of a circle S, whose centre is at (-3, 2), then the radius of S is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of a circle is x2 + y2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0,
whose centyer is (2, -3) and radius
= {tex}\sqrt{2^{2}+(-3)^{2}+12}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+12}{/tex} = 5
Now, according to given information, we have the following figure

x2 + y2 - 4x + 6y - 12 = 0
Clearly, AO {tex}\perp{/tex} BC,  as O is mid-point of the chord
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex} AOB, we have
OA = {tex}\sqrt {{{(-3 - 2)}^2}\, + \,{{(2+3)}^2}} {/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{25+25}=\sqrt{50}=5 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
and OB = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB {tex}\sqrt {{{OA^{2} + O}}{{\text{B}}^{\text{2}}}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{{50 + }{25}}} {/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{75}=5 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If the tangent to the parabola y^2 = x at a point $(\alpha, \beta),(\beta>0)$ is also a tangent to the ellipse, x^2 + 2y^2 = 1, then $\alpha$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the tangent to the parabola y2 = x at a point {tex}(\alpha, \beta),(\beta>0){/tex} is also a tangent to the ellipse, x2 + 2y2 = 1, then {tex}\alpha{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Since the point ({tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex}) is on the parabola y2 = x, so
{tex}\alpha = \beta^2{/tex} ... (i)
Now, equation of tangent at point ({tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex}) to the parabola
y2 = x, is T = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \beta=\frac{1}{2}(x+\alpha){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} equation of the tangent to the parabola y2 = 4ax at a point (x1 , y1) is given by yy1 = 2a(x+ x1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 y \beta=x+\beta^{2}{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{x}{2 \beta}+\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
Since, line (ii) is also a tangent of the ellipse x2 + 2y2 = 1
{tex}\therefore \quad\left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right)^{2}=(1)^{2}\left(\frac{1}{2 \beta}\right)^{2}+\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} condition of tangency of line y = mx + c to ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 \text { is } c^2=a^{2} m^2+b^2{/tex}, here {tex}m=\frac{1}{2\beta}, a=1, b=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \text { and } c=\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\beta^{2}}{4}=\frac{1}{4 \beta^{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{4}=1+2 \beta^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad B^{4}-2 B^{2}-1=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{2}=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4+4}}{2}=\frac{2 \pm 2 \sqrt{2}}{2}=1 \pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \beta^{2}=1+\sqrt{2} \quad\left[\because \beta^{2}>0\right]{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\beta^{2}=1+\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The eccentricity of the hyperbola whose length of the latusrectum is equal to 8 and the length of its conjugate axis is equal to half of the distance between its foci, is
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of the hyperbola whose length of the latusrectum is equal to 8 and the length of its conjugate axis is equal to half of the distance between its foci, is
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=8{/tex} and 2b = ae
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 4a and 2b = ae
Consider, 2b = ae
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4b2 = a2e2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}4 a^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right)=a^{2} e^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4e2- 4 = e2 [{tex}\because{/tex} a {tex}\ne{/tex} 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3e2= 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} e > 0]

questions.question: If the tangents on the ellipse 4x^2 + y^2 = 8 at the points. (1, 2) and (a, b) are perpendicular to each other, then a^2 is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the tangents on the ellipse 4x2 + y2 = 8 at the points. (1, 2) and (a, b) are perpendicular to each other, then a2 is equal to
questions.explanation:
Equation of given ellipse is 4x2 + y2 = 8 ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{y^{2}}{8}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{(\sqrt{2})^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}=1{/tex}
Now, equation of tangent at point (1, 2) is 2x + y = 4 ...(ii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} equation of tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at (x1, x1) is {tex}\frac{x x_{1}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}] and equation of another tangent at point (a, b)is
4ax + by= 8 ... (iii)
Since, lines (ii) and (iii) are perpendicular to each other.
{tex}\therefore\left(-\frac{2}{1}\right) \times\left(-\frac{4a}{b}\right)=-1{/tex}
[if lines a1x + b1y + c1 = 0 and a2x + b2y + c2 = 0 are perpendicular, then {tex}\left(-\frac{a_{1}}{b_{1}}\right)\left(-\frac{a_{2}}{b_{2}}\right)=-1{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -8a ...(iv)
Also, the point (a, b) lies on the ellipse (i), so
4a2 + b2 = 8
4a2 + 64a2 = 8 [from Eq.(iv)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 68 a^{2}=8 \Rightarrow a^{2}=\frac{8}{68}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a^{2}=\frac{2}{17}{/tex}

questions.question:
Consider a branch of the hyperbola
$x^{2}-2 y^{2}-2 \sqrt{2} x-4 \sqrt{2} y-6=0$
with vertex at point A. Let B be one of the endpoints of its latus rectum. If C is the focus of the hyperbola nearest to point A, then the area of the $\triangle$ABC is
questions.question_raw:
Consider a branch of the hyperbola
{tex}x^{2}-2 y^{2}-2 \sqrt{2} x-4 \sqrt{2} y-6=0{/tex}
with vertex at point A. Let B be one of the endpoints of its latus rectum. If C is the focus of the hyperbola nearest to point A, then the area of the {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC is
questions.explanation:
Given equation can be rewritten as focal chord
{tex}\frac{(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}}{4}-\frac{(y+\sqrt{2})^{2}}{2}=1{/tex}
For point A(x, y), e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{2}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex} = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 + {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}

For point C(x, y), x - {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = ae = {tex}\sqrt6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\sqrt6 {/tex} + {tex}\sqrt2{/tex}
Now, AC = {tex}\sqrt6{/tex} + {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} - 2 - {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt6{/tex} - 2
and BC = {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{2}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times(\sqrt{6}-2) \times 1{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt {\frac{3}{2}} - 1{/tex} sq unit.

questions.question: If tangents are drawn to the ellipse x^2 + 2y^2 = 2 at all points on the ellipse other than its four vertices, then the mid-points of the tangents intercepted between the coordinate axes lie on the curve
questions.question_raw: If tangents are drawn to the ellipse x2 + 2y2 = 2 at all points on the ellipse other than its four vertices, then the mid-points of the tangents intercepted between the coordinate axes lie on the curve
questions.explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is x2 + 2y2 = 2 , which can be written as {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1{/tex}
Let P be a point on the ellipse, other than its four vertices. Then, the parametric coordinates of P be ({tex}\sqrt2{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, sin{tex}\theta{/tex})

Now, the equation of tangent at P is
{tex}\frac{x \sqrt{2} \cos \theta}{2}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{1}=1{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} equation of tangent at (x, y) is given by T = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x x_{1}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{\sqrt{2} \sec \theta}+\frac{y}{cosec \theta}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because A(\sqrt{2} \sec \theta, 0) \text { and } B(0, cosec \theta){/tex}
Let mid-point of AB be R(h, k), then
{tex}h=\frac{\sqrt{2} \sec \theta}{2} \text { and } k=\frac{cosec \theta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}2 h=\sqrt{2} \sec \theta \text { and } 2 k=cosec \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} h} \text { and } \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2 k}{/tex}
We know that, {tex}\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2 h^2}+\frac{1}{4 k^{2}}=1{/tex}
So, locus of (h, k) is {tex}\frac{1}{2 x^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 y^{2}}=1{/tex}

questions.question: If one of the diameters of the circle $x ^2+y^{2}-2 x-6 y+6=0$ is a chord to the circle with centre (2, 1), then the radius of the circle is
questions.question_raw: If one of the diameters of the circle {tex}x ^2+y^{2}-2 x-6 y+6=0{/tex} is a chord to the circle with centre (2, 1), then the radius of the circle is
questions.explanation:
{tex}Equation ~of ~circle =x^{2}+y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c=0{/tex}
Here, radius of smaller circle, AC = {tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+3^{2}-6}{/tex} = 2
Clearly, from the figure the radius of bigger circle
r2 = 22 + [(2 - 1)2 + (1 - 3)2]
r2 = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 3

questions.question: A hyperbola, having the transverse axis of length 2 sin$\theta$, is confocal with the ellipse 3x^2 + 4y^2 = 12. Then, its equation is
questions.question_raw: A hyperbola, having the transverse axis of length 2 sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, is confocal with the ellipse 3x2 + 4y2 = 12. Then, its equation is
questions.explanation:
The given ellipse is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2, b = {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 = 4(1 - e2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e =  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
So, ae = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1
Hence, the eccentricity e1 of the hyperbola is given by 
e1 = cosec{tex}\theta{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} ae = esin{tex}\theta{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = {tex}sin^2\theta (cosec^2\theta -1) = cos^2\theta{/tex}
Hence, equation of hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \theta}-\frac{y^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \theta}=1{/tex} or {tex}x^{2} cosec ^{2} \theta-y^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}

questions.question: Equation of a common tangent to the parabola y^2 = 4x and the hyperbola xy = 2 is
questions.question_raw: Equation of a common tangent to the parabola y2 = 4x and the hyperbola xy = 2 is
questions.explanation:
We know that, {tex}y=m x+\frac{a}{m}{/tex} is the equation of tangent to the parabola y2 = 4ax.
{tex}\therefore y=m x+\frac{1}{m}{/tex} i s a tangent to the parabola
y2 = 4x [{tex}\because{/tex} a = 1]
Let, this tangent is also a tangent to the hyperbola xy=2
Now, on substituting {tex}y=m x+\frac{1}{m} \text { in } x y=2{/tex}, we get
{tex}x\left(m x+\frac{1}{m}\right)=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m^{2} x^{2}+x-2 m=0{/tex}
 Note that tangent touch the curve exactly at one point,
therefore both roots of above equations are equal
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad D=0 \Rightarrow 1-4\left(m^{2}\right)(-2 m) \Rightarrow m^{3}=\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of tangent is {tex}y=-\frac{x}{2}-2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = - x  - 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2y + 4 = 0

questions.question: The eccentricity of an ellipse whose centre is at the origin is 1/2. If one of its directrices is x = -4, then the equation of the normal to it at $\left(1, \frac{3}{2}\right)$ is
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of an ellipse whose centre is at the origin is 1/2. If one of its directrices is x = -4, then the equation of the normal to it at {tex}\left(1, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}e=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } \frac{a}{e}=4{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 2
Now, {tex}b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)=(2)^{2}\left[1-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}\right]=4\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad b=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of the ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{(2)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{(\sqrt{3})^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex}
Now, the equation of normal at {tex}\left(1, \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex} is
{tex}\frac{a^{2} x}{x_{1}}-\frac{b^{2} y}{y_{1}}=a^{2}-b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{4 x}{1}-\frac{3 y}{(3 / 2)}=4-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x - 2y = 1

questions.question: The number of common tangents to the circles x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y - 12 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 18y + 26 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The number of common tangents to the circles x2 + y2 - 4x - 6y - 12 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 6x + 18y + 26 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given equations of circles are
x2 + y2 - 4x - 6y - 12 = 0 ......(i)
x2 + y2 + 6x + 18y + 26 = 0 ......(ii)
Centre of circle (i) is C1 (2,3) and radius
= {tex}\sqrt{4+9+12}{/tex} = 5 (r1) [say]
Centre of circle (ii) is C2(-3, - 9) and radius
= {tex}\sqrt{9+81-26}{/tex} = 8 (r2) [say]
Now, C1C2 = {tex}\sqrt{(2+3) 2+(3+9) 2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C1C2 = {tex}\sqrt{5^2+12^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C1C2 = {tex}\sqrt{25+144}{/tex} = 13
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r1 + r2 = 5 + 8 = 13
Also, C1C2 = r1 + r2
Thus, both circles touch each other externally. Hence, there are three common tangents.

questions.question: The area (in sq units) of the quadrilateral formed by the tangents at the end points of the latusrectum to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1$ is
questions.question_raw: The area (in sq units) of the quadrilateral formed by the tangents at the end points of the latusrectum to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a^{2}=9, b^{2}=5 \Rightarrow a=3, b=\sqrt{5}{/tex}
Now, {tex}e=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{5}{9}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Foci = {tex}(\pm a e, 0)=(\pm 2,0) \text { and } \frac{b^{2}}{a}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Extermities of one of latusrectumk are {tex}\left(2, \frac{5}{3}\right) \text { and }\left(2, \frac{-5}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent at {tex}\left(2, \frac{5}{3}\right){/tex} is
{tex}\frac{x(2)}{9}+\frac{y(5 / 3)}{5}=1{/tex} or 2x + 3y = 9
Since, Eq. (ii) intersects X and Y-axes at {tex}\left(\frac{9}{2},0\right){/tex} and (0, 3), respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of quadrilateral = {tex}4 \times \text { Area of } \Delta P O Q{/tex}
= {tex}4\times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9}{2} \times 3\right)=27 \mathrm{sq} \text { units }{/tex}

questions.question: Let C be the circle with center at (1, 1) and radius 1. If T is the circle centered at (0, y) passing through the origin and touching the circle C externally, then the radius of T is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let C be the circle with center at (1, 1) and radius 1. If T is the circle centered at (0, y) passing through the origin and touching the circle C externally, then the radius of T is equal to
questions.explanation:
PLAN: Use the property, when two circles touch each other externally, then the distance between the center is equal to the sum of their radii, to get the required radius.
Let the coordinate of the center of T be (0, k).
Distance between their centre
k + 1 = {tex}\sqrt{1+(k-1) ^2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} C1C2 = k + 1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k + 1 = {tex}\sqrt{1+k^{2}+1-2 k}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k + 1 = {tex}\sqrt{k^{2}+2-2 k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 + 1 + 2k = k2 + 2 - 2k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
So, the radius of circle T is k, i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn from the centre of the ellipse x^2^ + 3y^2 = 6 on any tangent to it is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn from the centre of the ellipse x2 + 3y2 = 6 on any tangent to it is
questions.explanation:
Equation of ellipse is {tex}x^{2}+3 y^{2}=6 \text { or } \frac{x^{2}}{6}+\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1{/tex}
Equation of the tangent is {tex}\frac{x \cos \theta}{a}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{b}=1{/tex}
Let (h, k) be any point on the locus.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{h}{a} \cos \theta+\frac{k}{b} \sin \theta=1{/tex} ...(i)
Slope of the tangent line is {tex}\frac{-b}{a} \cot \theta{/tex}
Slope of perpendicular drawn from centre (0, 0) to (h, k) is k/h.
Since, both the lines are perpendicular.
{tex}\therefore \quad\left(\frac{k}{h}\right) \times\left(-\frac{b}{a} \cot \theta\right)=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\cos \theta}{h a}=\frac{\sin \theta}{k b}=\alpha{/tex} [say]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\alpha h a{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta=\alpha k b{/tex}
From Eq. (i), {tex}\frac{h}{a}(\alpha \ h\ a)+\frac{k}{b}(\alpha \ k \ b)=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad h_{2} \alpha+k_{2} \alpha=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{1}{\hbar^{2}+k^{2}}{/tex}
Also, {tex}\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad(\alpha k b)^{2}+(\alpha h a)^{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \alpha{^2 k^{2} b^{2}}+\alpha^2 h^{2} a^{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k^{2} b^{2}}{\left(h^{2}+k^{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{h^{2} a^{2}}{\left(h^{2}+k^{2}\right)^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 k^{2}}{\left(h^{2}+k^{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{6 h^{2}}{\left(h^{2}+k^{2}\right)^{2}}=1 \quad\left[\because a^{2}=6, b^{2}=2\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x2 + 2y2 = (x2 + y2)2 [replacing k by y and h by x]

questions.question: If e_1 is the eccentricity of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ and e_2 the eccentricity of the hyperbola passing through the foci of the ellipse and e_1e_2 = 1, then equation of the hyperbola is
questions.question_raw: If e1 is the eccentricity of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1{/tex} and e2 the eccentricity of the hyperbola passing through the foci of the ellipse and e1e2 = 1, then equation of the hyperbola is
questions.explanation:
The eccentricity of {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1{/tex} is 
e1 = {tex}=\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} e2 = {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} e1e2 = 1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Foci of ellipse (0, {tex}\pm{/tex}3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation of hyperbola is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}=-1{/tex}.

questions.question: The normal at a point P on the ellipse x^2 + 4y^2 = 16 meets the X-axis at Q. If M is the mid-point of the line segment PQ, then the locus of M intersects the latusrectum of the given ellipse at the points
questions.question_raw: The normal at a point P on the ellipse x2 + 4y2 = 16 meets the X-axis at Q. If M is the mid-point of the line segment PQ, then the locus of M intersects the latusrectum of the given ellipse at the points
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex}
Here, a = 4, b = 2
Equation of normal 4xsec {tex}\theta{/tex}-2y cosec {tex}\theta{/tex} = 12
{tex}M\left(7 \frac{\cos \theta}{2}, \sin \theta\right)=(h, k){/tex} [say]
{tex}\therefore \quad h=\frac{7 \cos \theta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 h}{7}=\cos \theta{/tex} ...(i)
and k = sin{tex}\theta{/tex} ...(ii)
On squaring and adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{4 h^{2}}{49}+k^{2}=1 \quad\left[\because \cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta=1\right]{/tex}
Hence, locus is {tex}\frac{4 x^{2}}{49}+y^{2}=1{/tex} ...(iii)

For given ellipse, {tex}e^{2}=1-\frac{4}{16}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad e=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad x=\pm 4 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\pm 2 \sqrt{3} \quad[\because x=\pm a e]{/tex} ...(iv)
On solving Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex}\frac{4}{49} \times 12+y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow y^{2}=1-\frac{48}{49}=\frac{1}{49}{/tex}
{tex}y=\pm \frac{1}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required points {tex}\left(\pm 2 \sqrt{3}, \pm \frac{1}{7}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If the circle x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 2ky + 6 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 2ky + k = 0 intersect orthogonally, then k is
questions.question_raw: If the circle x2 + y2 + 2x + 2ky + 6 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 2ky + k = 0 intersect orthogonally, then k is
questions.explanation:
Since the given circles intersect orthogonally.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2(1) (0) + 2 (k) (k) = 6 + k [{tex}\because{/tex} 2g1 g2 + 2f1 f2 = c1 + c2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2k2 - k - 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = - {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} , 2

questions.question: The line passing through the extremity A of the major axis and extremity B of the minor axis of the ellipse x^2 + 9y^2 = 9 meets its auxiliary circle at the point M. Then, the area (in sq. units) of the triangle with vertices at A, M and the origin O is
questions.question_raw: The line passing through the extremity A of the major axis and extremity B of the minor axis of the ellipse x2 + 9y2 = 9 meets its auxiliary circle at the point M. Then, the area (in sq. units) of the triangle with vertices at A, M and the origin O is
questions.explanation:
Equation of auxiliary circle is x2 + y2 = 9 ...(i)
Equation of AM is {tex}\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{1}=1{/tex} ...(ii)

On solving Eqs, (i) and (ii), we get M{tex}\left(-\frac{\mathbb{12}}{5}, \frac{9}{5}\right){/tex}
Now, area of {tex}\Delta A O M=\frac{1}{2} \cdot O A \times M N=\frac{27}{10}{/tex} sq units

questions.question: For hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{cos^2 \alpha}-\frac{y^{2}}{sin^2\alpha}=1$, which of the following remains constant with change in $\alpha$
questions.question_raw: For hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{cos^2 \alpha}-\frac{y^{2}}{sin^2\alpha}=1{/tex}, which of the following remains constant with change in {tex}\alpha{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given equation of hyperbola is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\cos ^{2} \alpha}-\frac{y^{2}}{\sin ^{2} \alpha}=1{/tex}
Here, {tex}a^{2}=\cos ^{2} \alpha{/tex} and {tex}b^{2}=\sin ^{2} \alpha{/tex}
[i.e. comparing with standard equation {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}]
We know that, foci = (± ae,0)
where, ae = {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Foci = ({tex}\pm{/tex} 1, 0)
where, vertices are ({tex}\pm{/tex}cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}, 0)
Eccentricity, ae = 1 or e = {tex}\frac{1}{\cos \alpha}{/tex}
Hence, foci remains constant with change in {tex}\alpha{/tex}.

questions.question: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse x^2 + 2y^2 = 2, then the locus of the mid-point of the intercept made by the tangents between the coordinate axes is
questions.question_raw: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse x2 + 2y2 = 2, then the locus of the mid-point of the intercept made by the tangents between the coordinate axes is
questions.explanation:
Let the point {tex}P\left(\sqrt{2} \cos \theta, \sin \theta) \text { on } \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1\right.{/tex}


Equation of tangent is, {tex}\frac{x \sqrt{2}}{2} \cos \theta+y \sin \theta=1{/tex}
whose intercept on coordinate axes are
A({tex}\sqrt2{/tex} sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, 0) and B (0, cosec {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mid-point of its intercept between axes
{tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \sec \theta, \frac{1}{2} cosec~ \theta\right)=(h, k){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 h}} \text { and } \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2 k}{/tex}
Thus, focus of mid-point M is {tex}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)=\frac{1}{2 h^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 k^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 x^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 y^{2}}=1{/tex}, is required locus.

questions.question: The $\triangle$PQR is inscribed in the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25. If Q and R have coordinates (3, 4) and (-4, 3) respectively, then $\angle$QPR is equal to
questions.question_raw: The {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR is inscribed in the circle x2 + y2 = 25. If Q and R have coordinates (3, 4) and (-4, 3) respectively, then {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR is equal to
questions.explanation:
Let O is the point at the centre and P is the point at the circumference. Therefore, angle QOR is double the angle QPR. So, it is sufficient to find the angle QOR.

Now,
slope of OQ,m1 = 4/3,
slope of OR, m2 = -3/4
Here, m1 m2 = - 1
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}QOR = {tex}\pi{/tex}/2
which implies that {tex}\angle{/tex}QPR = {tex}\pi{/tex} / 4

questions.question: The equation $\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}$ = 1, |r| < 1 represents
questions.question_raw: The equation {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}{/tex} = 1, |r| < 1 represents
questions.explanation:
Given equation is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}=1{/tex}, where |r| < 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - r is (+ve) and 1 + r is (+ve)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Given equation is of the form {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}.
Hence, it represents a hyperbola when |r| < 1.

questions.question: Tangent is drawn to ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{27}+y^{2}=1$ at $(3 \sqrt{3} \cos \theta, \sin \theta)\;(\text {where, } \theta \in(0, \pi / 2)$. Then, the value of $\theta$ such that the sum of intercepts on axes made by this tangent is minimum, is
questions.question_raw: Tangent is drawn to ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{27}+y^{2}=1{/tex} at {tex}(3 \sqrt{3} \cos \theta, \sin \theta)\;(\text {where, } \theta \in(0, \pi / 2){/tex}. Then, the value of {tex}\theta{/tex} such that the sum of intercepts on axes made by this tangent is minimum, is
questions.explanation:
Given, tangent is drawn at {tex}(3 \sqrt{3} \cos \theta, \sin \theta){/tex} to {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{27}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangents is {tex}\frac{x \cos \theta}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{1}=1{/tex}
Thus, sum of intercepts = {tex}\left(\frac{3{\sqrt{3}}}{\cos \theta}+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right)=f(\theta){/tex} [say]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(\theta)=\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \sin ^{3} \theta-\cos ^{3} \theta}{\sin ^{2} \theta \cos ^{2} \theta}, \text { put } f^{\prime}(\theta)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin ^{3} \theta=\frac{1}{3^{3 / 2}} \cos ^{3} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tan \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \text { i.e. } \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}, f^{\prime \prime}(0)>0{/tex}
Hence, tangent is minimum at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

questions.question: The number of common tangents to the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}=4$ and x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 8y = 24 is
questions.question_raw: The number of common tangents to the circles {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=4{/tex} and x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y = 24 is
questions.explanation:
Given, x2 + y2 = 4
Centre {tex}\equiv{/tex} C1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (0, 0) and R1 = 2
Again, x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y - 24 = 0, then C2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (3, 4)
and R2 = 7
Again, C1C2 = 5 = R2 - R1
Therefore, the given circles touch internally such that, they can have just one common tangent at the point of contact.

questions.question: The equation of a common tangent to the curves, y^2 = 16x and xy = -4, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a common tangent to the curves, y2 = 16x and xy = -4, is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of curves are
y2= 16x (parabola) ... (i)
and xy = - 4 (rectangular hyperbola) ... (ii)
Clearly, equation of tangent having slope 'm' to parabola
(i) is y = mx + {tex}\frac{4}{m}{/tex} ... (iii)
Now, eliminating y from Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
x{tex}\left(m x+\frac{4}{m}\right){/tex} = - 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mx2 + {tex}\frac{4}{m}x{/tex} + 4 = 0,
which will give the points of intersection of tangent and rectangular hyperbola.
Since, line y = mx + {tex}\frac{4}{m}{/tex} is also a tangent to the rectangular hyperbola.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The discriminant of quadratic equation mx2 + {tex}\frac{4}{m} x{/tex} = 0, should be zero.
[{tex}\because{/tex} there will be only one point fo intersection]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D = {tex}\left(\frac{4}{m}\right)^{2}-4(m)(4)=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m3 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 1
So, equation of required tangent is y = x + 4.

questions.question:
The angle between a pair of tangents drawn from a point P to the circle
$x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x-6 y+9 \sin ^2 \alpha+13 \cos ^{2} \alpha=0$ is 2$\alpha$. The equation of the locus of the point P is
questions.question_raw:
The angle between a pair of tangents drawn from a point P to the circle
{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x-6 y+9 \sin ^2 \alpha+13 \cos ^{2} \alpha=0{/tex} is 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}. The equation of the locus of the point P is
questions.explanation:
Centre of the circle
x2 + y2 + 4x - 6y + 9 sin2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 13 cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0
is C (-2, 3) and its radius is
{tex}=\sqrt{(-2)^2+(3)^2-9 \sin^2 2 \alpha-13 \cos^ 2 \alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{13-13 \cos 2 \alpha-9 \sin 2 \alpha}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{13 \sin ^{2} \alpha-9 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4 \sin ^{2} \alpha}{/tex} = 2 sin {tex}\alpha{/tex}

Let (h, k) be any point P and
{tex}\angle{/tex}APC = {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\angle{/tex}PAC = {tex}\pi{/tex}/2
That is, triangle APC is a right angled triangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{A C}{P C}=\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{\sqrt{(h+2)^{2}+(k-3)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (h + 2)2 + (k - 3)2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + 4 + 4h + k2 + 9 - 6k = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 + 4h - 6k + 9 = 0
Thus, required equation of the locus is
x2 + y2 + 4x - 6y + 9 = 0

questions.question: If a > 2b > 0, then positive value of m for which y = mx - $b \sqrt {1+ m^2}$ is a common tangent to x^2 + y^2 = b^2 and (x - a)^2 + y^2 = b^2 is
questions.question_raw: If a > 2b > 0, then positive value of m for which y = mx - {tex}bÂ \sqrt {1+ m^2}{/tex} is a common tangent to x2 + y2 = b2 and (x - a)2 + y2 = b2 is
questions.explanation:
Given,  y = mx - {tex}bÂ \sqrt {1+ m^2}{/tex} touches both the circles, so distance from centre = radius of both the circles.
{tex}\frac{|m a-0-b \sqrt{1+m^{2}}|}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}=b \text { and } \frac{|-b \sqrt{1+m^{2}}|}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}=b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|m a-b \sqrt{1+m^{2}}|=|-b \sqrt{1+m^{2}}|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m^{2} a^{2}-2 a b m \sqrt{1+m^{2}}+b^{2}\left(1+m^{2}\right)=b^{2}\left(1+m^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m a-2 b \sqrt{1+m^{2}}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m^{2} a^{2}=4 b^{2}\left(1+m^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad m=\frac{2 b}{\sqrt{a^{2}-4 b^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: If the line y = mx + 7$\sqrt3$ is normal to the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{24}-\frac{y^{2}}{18}=1$, then a value of m is
questions.question_raw: If the line y = mx + 7{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} is normal to the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{24}-\frac{y^{2}}{18}=1{/tex}, then a value of m is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of hyperbola, is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{24}-\frac{y^{2}}{18}=1{/tex} ...(i)
Since, the equation of the normals of slope m to the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, are given by y = {tex}m x \mp \frac{m\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2} m^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of normals of slope m, to the hyperbola (i), are
y = {tex}m x \pm \frac{m(24+18)}{\sqrt{24-m^{2}(18)}}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\because{/tex} Line y = mx {tex}\pm{/tex} 7{tex}\sqrt3{/tex} is normal to hyperbola (i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} On comparing with Eq. (ii) we get
{tex}\pm \frac{m(42)}{\sqrt{24-18 m^{2}}}=7 \sqrt{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pm \frac{6 m}{\sqrt{24-18 m^{2}}}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{36 m^{2}}{24-18 m^{2}}=3{/tex} [squaring both sides]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12m2 =24 - 18m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30m2 = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5m2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}

questions.question: The number of values of c such that the straight line y = 4x + c touches the curve $\frac{x^{2}}{4}$ + y^2 = 1 is
questions.question_raw: The number of values of c such that the straight line y = 4x + c touches the curve {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}{/tex} + y2 = 1 is
questions.explanation:
For ellipse, condition of tangency is c2 = a2m2 + b2
Given line is y = 4x + c and curve {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+y^{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad c^{2}=4 \times 4^{2}+1=65{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad c=\pm \sqrt{65}{/tex}
So, there are two different values of C.

questions.question: If the two circles $(x-1)^{2}+(y-3)^2=r^{2}$ and x^2 + y^2 - 8x + 2y + 8 = 0 intersect in two distinct points, then
questions.question_raw: If the two circles {tex}(x-1)^{2}+(y-3)^2=r^{2}{/tex} and x2 + y2 - 8x + 2y + 8 = 0 intersect in two distinct points, then
questions.explanation:
As, the two circles intersect in two distinct points.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Distance between centres lies between |r1 - r2| and |r1 + r2|.
i.e. {tex}|r-3|<\sqrt{(4-1) ^2+(-1-3)}^2<|r+3|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} | r - 3 | < 5 < | r + 3 | {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r < 8 or r > 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 < r < 8

questions.question: If the eccentricity of the standard hyperbola passing through the point (4, 6) is 2, then the equation of the tangent to the hyperbola at (4, 6) is
questions.question_raw: If the eccentricity of the standard hyperbola passing through the point (4, 6) is 2, then the equation of the tangent to the hyperbola at (4, 6) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of standard hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} ...(i)
Now, eccentricity of hyperbola is  
{tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=2{/tex} ...(given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = 4a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 3a2 ...(ii)
Since, hyperbola (i) passes through the point (4, 6)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{16}{a^{2}}-\frac{36}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} ...(iii)
On solving Eqs. (ii) and (iii) we get 
a2 = 4 and b2 = 12
Now, equation of tanget to hyperbola (i) at point (4, 6), is 
{tex}\frac{4 x}{a^{2}}-\frac{6 y}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 x}{4}-\frac{6 y}{12}=1{/tex} [From Eq. (iv)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x-\frac{y}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y - 2 = 0

questions.question: If a circle passes through the point (a, b) and cuts the circle x^2 + y^2 = k^2 orthogonally, then the equation of the locus of its centre is
questions.question_raw: If a circle passes through the point (a, b) and cuts the circle x2 + y2 = k2 orthogonally, then the equation of the locus of its centre is
questions.explanation:
Let x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, cuts x2 + y2 = k2 orthogonally.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2g1g2 + 2f1f2 = C1 + C2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2g·0 - 2f·O = c - k2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = k2 .....(i)
Also, x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + k2 = 0 passes through (a, b),
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a2 + b2 + 2ga + 2fb + k2 = 0 ....(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required equation of locus of centre is
-2ax - 2by + a2 + b2 + k2 = 0
or 2ax + 2by - (a2 + b2 + k2) = 0

questions.question: The equation of a tangent to the hyperbola 4x^2 - 5y^2 = 20 parallel to the line x - y = 2 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a tangent to the hyperbola 4x2 - 5y2 = 20 parallel to the line x - y = 2 is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of hyperbola is
4x2- 5y2 = 20
which can be rewritten as
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{5}-\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex}
The line x - y = 2 has slope, m = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of tangent parallel to this line = 1
We know equation of tangent to hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
having slope m is given by
y = {tex}m x \pm \sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex}
Here, a2= 5, b2= 4 and m = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of tangent is
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}x \pm \sqrt{5-4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y = x \pm 1 \Rightarrow x-y \pm 1=0{/tex}

questions.question: Tangents are drawn to the hyperbola 4x^2 - y^2 = 36 at the points P and Q. If these tangents intersect at the point T(0, 3), then the area (in sq units) of $\triangle$PTQ is
questions.question_raw: Tangents are drawn to the hyperbola 4x2 - y2 = 36 at the points P and Q. If these tangents intersect at the point T(0, 3), then the area (in sq units) of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PTQ is
questions.explanation:
Tangents are drawn to the hyperbola 4x2- y2 = 36 at the point P and Q.
Tangent intersects at point T(0,3)

Clearly, PQ is chord of contact.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of PQ is -3y = 36
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -12
Solving the curve 4x2 - y2 = 36 and y = -12,
we get x = {tex}\pm 3 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
Area of {tex}\triangle PQT{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times P Q \times S T{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(6 \sqrt{5} \times 15)=45 \sqrt{5}{/tex}

questions.question: If a hyperbola passes through the point P($\sqrt 2$, $\sqrt 3$) and has foci at ($\pm$2,0), then the tangent to this hyperbola at P also passes through the point
questions.question_raw: If a hyperbola passes through the point P({tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}) and has foci at ({tex}\pm{/tex}2,0), then the tangent to this hyperbola at P also passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of hyperbola be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ae = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2e2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 4 - a2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{4-a^{2}}=1{/tex} 
Since, ({tex}\sqrt2{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) lie on hyperbola.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{a^{2}}-\frac{3}{4-a^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 - 2a2 - 3a2= a2(4 - a2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 - 5a2 = 4a2 - a4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a4 - 9a2 + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a4 - 8)(a4 - 1) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a4 = 8, a4 = 1
a = 1
Now, equation of hyperbola is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1}-\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of tangent at ({tex}\sqrt2{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt3{/tex}) is given by
{tex}\sqrt{2} x-\frac{\sqrt{3} y}{3}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{2} x-\frac{y}{\sqrt{3}}=1{/tex}
which passes through the point (2{tex}\sqrt2{/tex}, 3{tex}\sqrt3{/tex}).

questions.question: If the line 2x + $\sqrt6$y = 2 touches the hyperbola x^2 - 2y^2 = 4, then the point of contact is
questions.question_raw: If the line 2x + {tex}\sqrt6{/tex}y = 2 touches the hyperbola x2 - 2y2 = 4, then the point of contact is
questions.explanation:
The equation of tangent at (x1, y1) is xx1 - 2yy1 = 4, which is same as 2x + {tex}\sqrt{6} y{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}}{2}=-\frac{2 y_{1}}{\sqrt{6}}=\frac{4}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = 4 and y1 = {tex}-\sqrt{6}{/tex}
Thus, the point of contact is (4, {tex}-\sqrt6{/tex})

questions.question: Let P(a sec$\theta$, b tan$\theta$) and Q(a sec$\phi$, b tan $\phi$), where $\theta+\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}$, be two points on the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$. If (h, k) is the point of the intersection of the normals at P and Q, then k is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let P(a sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, b tan{tex}\theta{/tex}) and Q(a sec{tex}\phi{/tex}, b tan {tex}\phi{/tex}), where {tex}\theta+\phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, be two points on the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}. If (h, k) is the point of the intersection of the normals at P and Q, then k is equal to
questions.explanation:
Firstly, we obtain the slope of normal to {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at  (a sec{tex}\theta{/tex},b tan{tex}\theta{/tex}). On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{2 x}{a^{2}}-\frac{2 y}{b^{2}} \times \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} \frac{x}{y}{/tex}
Slope for normal at the point (a sec0,b tan 0) will be
{tex}-\frac{a^{2} b \tan \theta}{b^{2} a \sec \theta}=-\frac{a}{b} \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Equation of normal at (a sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, b tan{tex}\theta{/tex}) is
y - btan{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{a}{b} \sin \theta(x-a \sec \theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) x + by = (a2+ b2) tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ax + b cosec{tex}\theta{/tex} = (a2 + b2) sec{tex}\theta{/tex} ...(i)
Similarly, equation of normal to {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at
(a sec{tex}\phi{/tex}), b tan{tex}\phi{/tex}) is ax + by cosec {tex}\phi{/tex} = (a2+ b2) sec{tex}\phi{/tex} .. .(ii)
On subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
b (cosec{tex}\theta{/tex} - cosec {tex}\phi{/tex})y = (a2+ b2) (sec{tex}\theta{/tex} - sec {tex}\phi{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{b} \cdot \frac{\sec \theta-\sec \phi}{cosec\theta-cosec \phi}{/tex}
But {tex}\frac{{\sec \theta - \sec \phi }}{{cosec \theta - cosec \phi }} = \frac{{\sec \theta - \sec (\frac{\pi }{2} - \theta )}}{{cosec \theta - cosec (\frac{\pi }{2} - \theta )}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\phi{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}]
= {tex}\frac{\sec \theta-\csc \theta}{\sec \theta-\sec \theta}=-1{/tex}
Thus, y = {tex}-\left(\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{b}\right){/tex}, i.e. k = {tex}-\left(\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{b}\right){/tex}

questions.question: If x = 9 is the chord of contact of the hyperbola x^2 - y^2 = 9, then the equation of the corresponding pair of tangents is
questions.question_raw: If x = 9 is the chord of contact of the hyperbola x2 - y2 = 9, then the equation of the corresponding pair of tangents is
questions.explanation:
Let (h,k) be a point whose chord of contact with respect to hyperbola x2- y2= 9 is x = 9.
We know that, chord of contact of (h, k) with respect to hyperbola x2 - y2 = 9 is T = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h.x  + k(-y) - 9 = 0 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} hx - ky - 9 = 0
But it is the equation fo the line x = 9.
This is possible when h = l, k=0 (by comparing both equations).
Again equation of pair of tangents is
T2 = SS1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 9)2 = (x2 - y2 - 9) (12 - 02 - 9)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 18x + 81 = (x2 - y2 - 9) (-8)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 18x + 81 = -8x2 + 8y2 + 72
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x2 - 8y2 - 18x + 9 = 0

questions.question: The common tangent to the circles x^2 + y^2 = 4 and x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 8y - 24 = 0 also passes through the point
questions.question_raw: The common tangent to the circles x2 + y2 = 4 and x2 + y2 + 6x + 8y - 24 = 0 also passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Given circles are
x2 + y2 = 4, centre c1 (0, 0) and radius r1 = 2 and x2 + y2 + 6x + 8y - 24 = 0, centre c2(-3, -4) and radius r2 = 7
{tex}\because{/tex} c1c2 = {tex}\sqrt{9+16}{/tex} = 5 and |r1 - r2| = 5
{tex}\because{/tex} c1c2 = |r1 - r2| = 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} circle x2 + y2 = 4 touches the circle x2 + y2 + 6x + By - 24 = 0 internally.
S1 - S2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 8y - 20 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 4y = 10 .....(i)
The common tangent passes through the point (6, - 2), from the given options.

questions.question: A rectangle is inscribed in a circle with a diameter lying along the line 3y = x + 7. If the two adjacent vertices of the rectangle are (-8, 5) and (6, 5), then the area of the rectangle (in sq units) is
questions.question_raw: A rectangle is inscribed in a circle with a diameter lying along the line 3y = x + 7. If the two adjacent vertices of the rectangle are (-8, 5) and (6, 5), then the area of the rectangle (in sq units) is
questions.explanation:
Given points are (-8, 5) and (6, 5) in which y-coordinate is the same, i.e. these points lie on horizontal line y = 5.

Let (-8, {tex}\beta{/tex}) and (6, {tex}\beta{/tex}) are t e coordinates of the other vertices of a rectangle as shown in the figure.
Since, the mid-point of line joining points (-8, 5) and (6, {tex}\beta{/tex}) lies on the line 3y = x + 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3 {tex}\left(\frac{5+\beta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-8+6}{2} {/tex} + 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 15 + 3{tex}\beta{/tex} = - 2 + 14
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3{tex}\beta{/tex} = - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = - 1
Now, area of rectangle= |-8 - 6| {tex}\times{/tex} |{tex}\beta{/tex} - 5|
= 14 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 84

questions.question: The tangent and the normal lines at the point $(\sqrt{3}, 1)$ to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4 and the X-axis form a triangle. The area of this triangle (in square units) is
questions.question_raw: The tangent and the normal lines at the point {tex}(\sqrt{3}, 1){/tex} to the circle x2 + y2 = 4 and the X-axis form a triangle. The area of this triangle (in square units) is
questions.explanation:
Let T = 0 and N = 0 represents the tangent and normal lines at the point p ({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 1) to the circle x2 + y2 = 4

So, equation of tangent (T = 0) is
{tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} x + y = 4 .....(i)
For point A, put y = 0, we get
x = {tex}\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of required {tex}\triangle{/tex}OPA = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (OA)(PM)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \times 1{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} PM = y-coordinate of P]
= {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}sq unit

questions.question: The straight-line x + 2y = 1 meets the coordinate axes at A and B. A circle is drawn through A, B and the origin. Then, the sum of perpendicular distances from A and B on the tangent to the circle at the origin is
questions.question_raw: The straight-line x + 2y = 1 meets the coordinate axes at A and B. A circle is drawn through A, B and the origin. Then, the sum of perpendicular distances from A and B on the tangent to the circle at the origin is
questions.explanation:
According to given information, we have the following figure.

From figure, equation of circle (diameter form) is (x - 1) (x - 0) + (y - 0) {tex}\left(y-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - x - {tex}\frac{y}{2}{/tex} = 0
Equation of tangent at (0, 0) is x + {tex}\frac{y}{2}{/tex} = 0
[{tex}\because{/tex} equation of tangent at (x1, y1) is given by T = 0
Here, T = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xx1 + yy1 - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x + x1) - {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} (y + y1) = 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + y = 0
Now, {tex}A M=\frac{|2 \cdot 1+1 \cdot 0|}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} distance of a point P(x1, y1) from a line ax + by + c = 0 is {tex}\frac{\left|a x_{1}+b y_{1}+c\right|}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}]{/tex}
and {tex}B N=\frac{\left|2 \cdot 0+1\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right|}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AM + BN {tex}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{5}}=\frac{4+1}{2 \sqrt{5}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If a circle C passing through the point (4, 0) touches the circle x^2 + y^2 + 4x - 6y = 12 externally at the point (1, -1), then the radius of C is
questions.question_raw: If a circle C passing through the point (4, 0) touches the circle x2 + y2 + 4x - 6y = 12 externally at the point (1, -1), then the radius of C is
questions.explanation:
Equation of a tangent to the circle
x2 + y2 + 4x - 6y - 12 = 0 at (1,-1) is given by xx1 + yy1 + 2 (x + x1) - 3 (y + y1) -12 = 0, where x1 = 1 and y1 = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y + 2 (x + 1) - 3(y -1) -12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x - 4y - 7 = 0
This will also a tangent to the required circle.
Now, the equation of the family of circles touching the line 3x - 4y - 7 = 0 at point (1, -1) is given by
(x - 1)2 + (y + 1)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (3x - 4y - 7) = 0
So, the equation of required circle will be
(x - 1)2 + (y + 1)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (3x - 4y - 7) = 0 for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R ......(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} The required circle passes through (4, 0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(4-1)^{2}+(0+1)^{2}+\lambda(3 \times 4-4 \times 0-7)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + 1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (5) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = - 2
Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2 in Eq.(i), we get
(x -1)2 + (y + 1)2 - 2 (3x- 4y - 7) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 8x + 10y + 16 = 0
On comparing it with
x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, we get
g = - 4, f = 5, c = 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = {tex}\sqrt{g^{2}+f^{2}-c}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{16+25-16}{/tex} = 5

questions.question: If the tangent at (1, 7) to the curve x^2 = y - 6 touches the circle x^2 + y^2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0, then the value of c is
questions.question_raw: If the tangent at (1, 7) to the curve x2 = y - 6 touches the circle x2 + y2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0, then the value of c is
questions.explanation:
Tangent to the curve x2 = y - 6 at (1, 7) is
x = {tex}\frac{y+7}{2}{/tex} - 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - y + 5 = 0 ......(i)
Equation of circle is x2 + y2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0
Centre (-8, -6)
r = {tex}\sqrt{8^{2}+6^{2}-c}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{100-c}{/tex}
Since, line 2x - y + 5 = 0 also touches the circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{100-c}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{2(-8)-(-6)+5}{\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{100-c}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-16+6+5}{\sqrt{5}}{}{}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{100-c}{/tex} = {tex}|-\sqrt{5}|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 100 - c = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 95

questions.question: Let ABCD be a quadrilateral with area 18, with side AB parallel to the side CD and AB = 2 CD. Let AD be perpendicular to AB and CD. If a circle is drawn inside the quadrilateral ABCD touching all the sides, then its radius is
questions.question_raw: Let ABCD be a quadrilateral with area 18, with side AB parallel to the side CD and AB = 2 CD. Let AD be perpendicular to AB and CD. If a circle is drawn inside the quadrilateral ABCD touching all the sides, then its radius is
questions.explanation:
18 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(3 \alpha)(2 r){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex}r = 6
Line, y = {tex}\frac{2 r}{\alpha}(x-2 \alpha){/tex} is tangent to circle
 (x - r)2 + (y - r)2 = r2
2{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 3r, {tex}\alpha{/tex}r = 6 and r = 2

Alternate Solution:
{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (x + 2x) {tex}\times{/tex} 2r = 18
xr = 6 ..... (i)

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB, tan {tex}\theta=\frac{x-r}{r}{/tex}
and in {tex}\triangle{/tex}DOC,
tan (90o - {tex}\theta{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{2 x-r}{r}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-r}{r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{r}{2 x-r}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(2x - 3r) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{3 r}{2}{/tex} ....(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
r = 2

questions.question: If the tangent at the point P on the circle x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 6y = 2 meets the straight line 5x - 2y + 6 = 0 at a point Q on the Y-axis, then the length of PQ is
questions.question_raw: If the tangent at the point P on the circle x2 + y2 + 6x + 6y = 2 meets the straight line 5x - 2y + 6 = 0 at a point Q on the Y-axis, then the length of PQ is
questions.explanation:
The line 5x - 2y + 6 = 0 meets the Y-axis at the point (0, 3) and therefore the tangent has to pass through the point (0, 3) and required length
= {tex}\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+6 x_{1}+6 y_{1}-2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{0^{2}+3^{2}+6(0)+6(3)-2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} = 5

questions.question: Let PQ and RS be tangents at the extremities of the diameter PR of a circle of radius r. If PS and RQ intersect at a point X on the circumference of the circle, then 2r equals
questions.question_raw: Let PQ and RS be tangents at the extremities of the diameter PR of a circle of radius r. If PS and RQ intersect at a point X on the circumference of the circle, then 2r equals

options.text: $\frac{2 P Q \cdot R S}{P Q+R S}$

questions.explanation:
From figure, it is clear that {tex}\triangle{/tex} PRQ and {tex}\triangle{/tex} RSP are similar.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{P R}{R S}=\frac{P Q}{R P}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR2 = PQ.RS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PR = {tex}\sqrt{P Q \cdot R S}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2r = {tex}\sqrt{\overline{P Q \cdot R S}}{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the centres of the circles, which touch the circle, x^2 + y^2 = 1 externally, also touch the Y-axis and lie in the first quadrant, is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the centres of the circles, which touch the circle, x2 + y2 = 1 externally, also touch the Y-axis and lie in the first quadrant, is
questions.explanation:
Let (h, k) be the centre of the circle and radius r = h, as circle touch the Y-axis and other circle x2 + y2 = 1 whose centre (0, 0) and radius is 1

{tex}\therefore{/tex} OC = r + 1 [{tex}\because{/tex} if circles touch each other externally, then C1C2 = r1 + r2]
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}}{/tex} = h + 1, h > 0 and k > 0, for first quadrant
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 = h2 + 2h + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2 = 2h + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = {tex}\sqrt{1+2 h}{/tex}, as k > 0
Now, on taking locus of centre (h, k), we get
y {tex}=\sqrt{1+2 x}, x \geq 0{/tex}

questions.question: The line x = y touches a circle at the point (1, 1). If the circle also passes through the point (1, - 3), then its radius is
questions.question_raw: The line x = y touches a circle at the point (1, 1). If the circle also passes through the point (1, - 3), then its radius is
questions.explanation:
Since, the equation of a family of circles touching line L = 0 at their point of contact (x1, y1) is (x - x1)2 + (y - y1)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}L = 0, where {tex}\lambda \in R{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of circle, touches the x = y at point (1, 1) is (x - 1)2 + (y - 1)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(x - y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2)x + (-{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2)y + 2 = 0 ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} Circle (i) passes through point (1, -3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 + 9 + ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2) + 3({tex}\lambda{/tex} + 2) + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4
So, equation of circle (i) at {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4, is x2 + y2 - 6x + 2y + 2 = 0
Now, radius of the circle = {tex}\sqrt{9+1-2}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: Two circles with equal radii are intersecting at the points (0, 1) and (0, -1). The tangent at the point (0, 1) to one of the circles passes through the center of the other circle. Then, the distance between the centers of these circles is
questions.question_raw: Two circles with equal radii are intersecting at the points (0, 1) and (0, -1). The tangent at the point (0, 1) to one of the circles passes through the center of the other circle. Then, the distance between the centers of these circles is
questions.explanation:
Clearly, circles are orthogonal because tangent at one point of intersection is passing through centre of the other.

Let C1 ({tex}\alpha{/tex}, 0) and C2 (-{tex}\alpha{/tex}, 0) are the centres
Then, S1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex})2 + y2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} x2 + y2 - 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex}x - 1 = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} radius, r = {tex}\sqrt{(\alpha-0) 2+(0-1)^{2}}{/tex}]
and S2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (x + {tex}\alpha{/tex})2 + y2 = {tex}\alpha{/tex}2 + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} x2 + y2 + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}x - 1 = 0
Now, 2({tex}\alpha{/tex}) (-{tex}\alpha{/tex}) + 2.0.0 = (-1) + (-1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} condition of orthogonality is 2g1g2 + 2f1f2 = c1 + c2]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} C1 (1, 0) and C2 (-1, 0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}C1C2 = 2

questions.question: Three circles of radii a, b, c (a < b < c) touch each other externally. If they have X-axis as a common tangent, then
questions.question_raw: Three circles of radii a, b, c (a < b < c) touch each other externally. If they have X-axis as a common tangent, then

options.text: a, b, c are in AP

questions.explanation:
According to given information, we have the following figure.

where A, B, C are the centres of the circles
Clearly, AB = a + b (sum of radii) and BD = b - a
{tex}\therefore A D=\sqrt{(a+b)^{2}-(b-a)^{2}}{/tex} (using Pythagoras theorem in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD)
{tex}=2 \sqrt{a b}{/tex}
Similarly, AC = a + c and CE = c - a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACE, AE = {tex}\sqrt{(a+c)^{2}-(c-a)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{a c}{/tex}
Similarly, BC = b + c and CF = c - b
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex}BCF, BF = {tex}\sqrt{(b+c)^{2}-(c-b)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{b c}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} AD + AE = BF
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 \sqrt{a b}+2 \sqrt{a c}=2 \sqrt{b c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{c}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{b}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}{/tex}

questions.question: The circle passing through (1, - 2) and touching the axis of x at (3, 0) also passes through the point
questions.question_raw: The circle passing through (1, - 2) and touching the axis of x at (3, 0) also passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of circle be (x - 3)2 + (y - 0)2 + {tex}\lambda y{/tex} = 0

As it passes through (1, -2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - 3)2 + (-2)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (-2) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 + 4 - 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of circle is (x - 3)2 + y2 + 4y = 0
By hit and trial method, we see that point (5, - 2) satisfies equation of circle.

questions.question: The circle passing through the point (-1, 0) and touching the Y-axis at (0, 2) also passes through the point
questions.question_raw: The circle passing through the point (-1, 0) and touching the Y-axis at (0, 2) also passes through the point
questions.explanation:
Equation of circle passing through a point (x1, y1) and touching the straight line L, is given by (x - x1)2 + (y - y1)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} L = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of circle passing through (0, 2) and touching x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0)2 + (y - 2)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} x = 0 ...(i)
Also, it passes through (-1, 0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + 4 - {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5
Eq. (i) becomes
x2 + y2 - 4y + 4 + 5x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + 5x - 4y + 4 = 0,
For x-intercept put y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 5x + 4 = 0,
(x + 1) (x + 4) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = -1, -4
Hence, the option is correct.

questions.question: The locus of the centre of the circle which touches (y - 1)^2 + x^2 = 1 externally and also touches X-axis, is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the centre of the circle which touches (y - 1)2 + x2 = 1 externally and also touches X-axis, is
questions.explanation:
Let the locus of centre of circle be (h, k) touching (y - 1)2 + x2 = 1 and X-axis shown as

Clearly, from figure,
Distance between C and A is always 1 + |k|,
i.e. {tex}\sqrt{(h-0)^{2}+(k-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 1 + |k|,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 - 2k + 1 = 1 + k2 + 2 |k|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 = 2 |k| + 2k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 2 |y| + 2y
where {tex}|y|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {y} & {, y \geq 0} \\ {-y} & {, y<0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 = 2y + 2y, y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0
and x2 = -2y + 2y, y < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4y, when y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0
and x2 = 0, when y < 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {(x, y) : x2 = 4y, when y {tex}\geq {/tex} 0} {tex}\cup{/tex} {(0, y) : y < 0}

questions.question: If two distinct chords, drawn from the point (p, q) on the circle x^2 + y^2 = px + qy = 0 (where, pq $\neq$ 0) are bisected by the x - axis, then
questions.question_raw: If two distinct chords, drawn from the point (p, q) on the circle x2 + y2 = px + qy = 0 (where, pq {tex}\neq{/tex} 0) are bisected by the x - axis, then
questions.explanation:
From equation of circle it is clear that circle passes through origin. Let AB is chord of the circle.

A {tex}\equiv {/tex} (p, q) {tex}\cdot{/tex} C is mid-point and coordinate of C is (h, 0)
Then, coordinates of B are (- p + 2h, - q) and B lies on the circle x2 + y2 = px + qy, we have
(-p + 2h)2 + (-q)2 = p(-p + 2h) + q(-q)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}p2 + 4h2 - 4ph + q2 = -p2 + 2ph - q22
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2p2 + 2q2 - 6ph + 4h2  = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h2 - 3ph + p2 + q2 = 0 ...(i)
There are given two distinct chords which are bisected at X-axis then, there will be two distinct values of h satisfying Eq. (i).
So, discriminant of this quadratic equation must be > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (-3p)2 - 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}2 (p2 + q2) > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9p2 - 8p2 - 8q2 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 - 8q2 > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 > 8q2

questions.question: The locus of the centre of a circle, which touches externally the circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 6y + 14 = 0 and also touches the y - axis, is given by the equation
questions.question_raw: The locus of the centre of a circle, which touches externally the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 6y + 14 = 0 and also touches the y - axis, is given by the equation
questions.explanation:
Let (h, k) be the centre of the circle which touches the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 6y + 14 = 0 and Y-axis
The centre of given circle is (3, 3) and radius is {tex}\sqrt{3^{2}+3^{2}-14}=\sqrt{9+9-14}=2{/tex}
