questions.question: The equation of a circle with radius 5 and touching both the coordinate axes is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a circle with radius 5 and touching both the coordinate axes is
questions.explanation:
Case I: If the circle lies in the first quadrant:
The equation of a circle that touches both the coordinate axes and has radius a is x2 + y2 - 2ax - 2ay + a2 = 0
The given radius of the circle is 5 units, i.e. a = 5.
Thus, the equation of the circle is x2 + y2 - 10x - 10 y + 25 = 0
Case II: If the circle lies in the second quadrant:
The equation of a circle that touches both the coordinate axes and has radius a is x2 + y2 + 2ax - 2ay + a2 = 0
The given radius of the circle is 5 units, i.e. a = 5.
Thus, the equation of the circle is x2 + y2 + 10x - 10 y + 25 = 0
Case III: If the circle lies in the third quadrant:
The equation of a circle that touches both the coordinate axes and has radius a is x2 + y2 + 2ax + 2ay + a2 = 0
The given radius of the circle is 5 units, i.e. a - 5.
Thus, the equation of the circle is x2 + y2 + 10x + 10 y + 25 = 0
Case IV: If the circle lies in the fourth quadrant:
The equation of a circle that touches both the coordinate axes and has radius a is x2 + y2 - 2ax + 2ay + a2 = 0
The given radius of the circle is 5 units, i.e. a = 5.
Thus, the equation of the circle is x2 + y2 - 10x + 10y + 25 = 0
Hence, combination of all the circle is x2 + y2 {tex}\pm{/tex}10x{tex}\pm{/tex} 10y + 25 = 0
Hence, the required equation of the circle is x2 + y2 {tex}\pm{/tex} 10x {tex}\pm{/tex} 10y + 25 = 0

questions.question: The equation of the circle concentric with x^2 + y^2 - 3x + 4y - c = 0 and passing through (-1, -2) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle concentric with x2 + y2 - 3x + 4y - c = 0 and passing through (-1, -2) is
questions.explanation:
Given that we need to find the equation of the circle which is concentric with x2 + y2 - 3x + 4y - c = 0 also passing through (-1, -2).
We know that concentric circles will have the same center.
Let us assume the concentric circle be x2 + y2 - 3x + 4y + d = 0. ...(ii)
Substituting (- 1, - 2) in equation (ii) we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (- 1)2 + (- 2)2 - 3(-1) + 4(-2) + d = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + 4 + 3 - 8 + d = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = 0
Substituting value of d in (ii) we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 3x + 4y = 0
required equation of circle is x2 + y2 - 3x + 4y = 0

questions.question: The circle x^2 + y^2 +2gx + 2fy + c = 0 does not intersect x-axis, if
questions.question_raw: The circle x2 + y2 +2gx + 2fy + c = 0 does not intersect x-axis, if
questions.explanation:
Given that the circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 does not intersect x - axis.
We know that the value of y coordinate on the x - axis is 0.
Put y = 0 in above equation, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + (0)2 + 2gx + 2f(0) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 2gx + c = 0
The quadratic equation will have no roots if the circle does not intersect the x - axis.
We know that for the quadratic equation ax2 + bx + c = 0 the condition to be satisfied for the equal roots is b2 - 4ac < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2g)2 - 4(1)(c) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4(g2 - c) < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g2 - c < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g2 < c

questions.question: If the circles x^2 + y^2 = a and x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 8y + 9 = 0, touch externally, then a =
questions.question_raw: If the circles x2 + y2 = a and x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y + 9 = 0, touch externally, then a =
questions.explanation:
x2 + y2 = a ...(1)
And, x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y + 9 = 0
Let circles (1) and (2) touch each other at point P.
The centre of the circle x2 + y2 = a, O, is (0, 0)
The centre of the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y + 9 = 0, C1, is (3, 4)
Also, the radius of the circle (1) - {tex}\sqrt{a}{/tex} - OP
Radius of circle (2) = {tex}\sqrt{9 + 16 - 9}{/tex} = 4 - C1 P 
C1O = C1P + OP
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{32 + 42}{/tex} = 4 + {tex}\sqrt{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 5 = 4 + {tex}\sqrt{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1

questions.question: If (x, 3) and (3, 5) are the extremities of a diameter of a circle with centre at (2, y), then the values of x and y are
questions.question_raw: If (x, 3) and (3, 5) are the extremities of a diameter of a circle with centre at (2, y), then the values of x and y are
questions.explanation:
The endpoints of the diameter of a circle are (x, 3) and (3, 5).
According to the question, we have:
centre is midpoint of the endpoints of diameters.
{tex}\frac{x+3}{2}{/tex} = 2, y = {tex}\frac{5+3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, y = 4

questions.question: If (- 3, 2) lies on the circle x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 which is concentric with the circle x^2 + y^2 + 6x + 8y - 5 = 0, then c =
questions.question_raw: If (- 3, 2) lies on the circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 which is concentric with the circle x2 + y2 + 6x + 8y - 5 = 0, then c =
questions.explanation:
The centre of the circle x2 + y2 + 6x + 8y - 5 = 0 is (-3, -4)
The circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2f y + c = 0 is concentric with the circle x2 + y2 + 6 x + 8 y - 5 = 0
Thus, the centre of x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 is (-3, -4)
(-3, -4) = (-g , -f)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} g = 3, f = 4
Also, it is given that (-3, 2) lies on the circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (-3)2 + 22 + 2(3)(-3) + 2(4)(2) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + 4 - 18 + 16 + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = - 11

questions.question: The equation of the circle which touches the axes of coordinates and the line $\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{4}=1$ and whose centres lie in the first quadrant is x^2 + y^2 - 2cx - 2cy + c^2 = 0, where c is equal to
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle which touches the axes of coordinates and the line {tex}\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{4}=1{/tex} and whose centres lie in the first quadrant is x2 + y2 - 2cx - 2cy + c2 = 0, where c is equal to
questions.explanation:
The equation of the circle that touches the axes of coordinates is x2 + y2 - 2cx - 2cy + c2Â = 0
Also, x2 + y2 - 2cx - 2cy + c2=0 touches the line {tex}\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{4}=1{/tex} or 4x + 3y - 12 = 0
since the circle lies in the first quadrant, it centre is (c, c).

From the figure, we have
radius = perpendicular distance from point (c,c) to line 4x + 3y -12 = 0
{tex}\left|\frac{4 c + 3 c - 12}{\sqrt{42 + 32}}\right|=c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{7 c - 12}{5}=c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 6

questions.question: The equation of the circle passing through the origin which cuts off intercept of length 6 and 8 from the axes is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle passing through the origin which cuts off intercept of length 6 and 8 from the axes is
questions.explanation:
Given that we need to find the equation of the circle passing through the origin and cuts off intercepts 6 and 8 from x and y - axes
Since the circle is having intercept a from x-axis the circle must pass through (6, 0) and (- 6, 0) as it already passes through the origin.
Since the circle is having intercept 8 from y-axis the circle must pass through (0, 8) and (0, - 8) as it already passes through the origin.
Let us assume the circle passing through the points O(0, 0), A(6, 0) and B(0, 8).
We know that the standard form of the equation of the circle is given by:
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + 2fx + 2gy + c = 0 ...(1)
Substituting O(0, 0) in (1), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 02 + 02 + 2f(0) + 2g(0) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0 ...(2)
Substituting A(6, 0) in (1), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 62 + 02 + 2f(6) + 2g(0) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 36 + 12f + c = 0 ...(3)
Substituting B(0, 8) in (1), we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 02 + 82 + 2f(0) + 2g(8) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 64 + 16g + c = 0 ...(4)
On solving (2), (3) and (4) we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f = - 3, b = - 4 and c = 0
Substituting these values in (1), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + y2 + 2(- 3)x + 2(- 4)y + 0 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y = 0
Similarly, we get the equation x2 + y2 + 6x + 8y = 0 for the circle passing through the points (0, 0), (- 6, 0), (0, - 8), 
 x2 + y2 - 6x + 8y = 0 for the circle passing through the points (0, 0), (6, 0), (0, - 8) .
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equations of the circles are x2 + y2 {tex}\pm{/tex} 6x {tex}\pm{/tex}8y = 0

questions.question: The coordinates of the focus of the parabola y^2 - x - 2 y + 2 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The coordinates of the focus of the parabola y2 - x - 2 y + 2 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
Given:
The equation of the parabola is y2 - x - 2y + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)2 - 1 = (x - 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)2 = x - 1
Let X = x - 1, Y = y - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} equation of parabola is Y2 = X
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y2 = X Comparing with Y2 = 4aX
a = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Focus = (X = a, Y = 0) = {tex}\left(X=\frac{1}{4}, Y=0\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(x=\frac{1}{4}+1, y=1\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(x=\frac{5}{4}, y=1\right){/tex}
Hence, the focus is at (5/4, 1)

questions.question: The vertex of the parabola (y + a)^2 = 8a (x - a) is
questions.question_raw: The vertex of the parabola (y + a)2 = 8a (x - a) is
questions.explanation:
(y + a)2 = 8a (x - a)
Putting X = x - a, Y = y + a
Y2 = 8aX
Vertex = (X = 0, Y = 0) = (x - a = 0, y + a = 0) = (x = a, y = - a)
Hence, the vertex is at (a, - a)

questions.question: If the focus of a parabola is (-2, 1) and the directrix has the equation x + y = 3, then its vertex is
questions.question_raw: If the focus of a parabola is (-2, 1) and the directrix has the equation x + y = 3, then its vertex is
questions.explanation:
Given:
The Focus S is at (-2, 1) and the directrix is the line x + y - 3 = 0  
The slope of the line perpendicular to x + y - 3 = 0 is 1
The axis of the parabola is perpendicular to the directrix and passes through the focus.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of the axis of the parabola = y - 1 = 1 (x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x - y + 3 = 0
Point of intersection of x - y + 3 = 0 and x + y - 3 = 0 is (0, 3)
Let (h, k) be the coordinates of the vertex, which is the mid-point of the segment joining K and the focus.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}h=\frac{0-2}{2}{/tex}, {tex}k=\frac{3+1}{2}{/tex}
h = - 1, k = 2
Hence, the coordinates of the vertex are (-1, 2)

questions.question: The equation of the parabola whose vertex is (a, 0) and the directrix has the equation x + y = 3a, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the parabola whose vertex is (a, 0) and the directrix has the equation x + y = 3a, is
questions.explanation:
Given:
The vertex is at (a, 0) and the directrix is the line x + y = 3a
The slope of the line perpendicular to x + y = 3a is 1
The axis of the parabola is perpendicular to the directrix and passes through the vertex.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of the axis of the parabola = y - 0 = 1 (x - a)  {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x - y - a  = 0
point of intersection of x - y - a  = 0 and x + y = 3a is (2a, a)
The vertex is the mid-point of the segment joining K and the focus (h, k)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a=\frac{2 a+h}{2}{/tex}, {tex}0=\frac{a+k}{2}{/tex}
h = 0, k = - a
Let P (x, y) be any point on the parabola whose focus is S (h, k) and the directrix is x + y = 3a

Draw PM perpendicular to x + y = 3a
Then, we have: SP = PM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SP2 = PM2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0)2 + (y + a)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{x+y-3 a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + (y + a)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{x+y-3 a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 + 2y2 + 2a2 + 4ay = x2 + y2 + 9a2 + 2xy - 6ax - 6ay
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 7a2 + 10ay + 6ax - 2xy = 0

questions.question: The parametric equations of a parabola are x = t^2 + 1, y = 2t + 1. The cartesian equation of its directrix is
questions.question_raw: The parametric equations of a parabola are x = t2 + 1, y = 2t + 1. The cartesian equation of its directrix is
questions.explanation:
Given:
x = t2 + 1    .......(i)
y = 2t + 1 
t = {tex}\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex}  .....(ii)
From (i) and (ii):
{tex}x=\left(\frac{y-1}{2}\right)^{2}+1{/tex}
(y - 1)2 = 4 (x - 1)
Let Y = y - 1 and X = x - 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y2 = 4X
Comparing it with y2 = 4ax
a = 1
Therefore, the equation of the directrix is X = - a, i.e.
x - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0

questions.question: If the coordinates of the vertex and the focus of a parabola are (-1, 1) and (2, 3) respectively, then the equation of its directrix is
questions.question_raw: If the coordinates of the vertex and the focus of a parabola are (-1, 1) and (2, 3) respectively, then the equation of its directrix is
questions.explanation:
The vertex and the focus of a parabola are (-1, 1) and (2, 3), respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of the axis of the parabola = {tex}\frac{3-1}{2+1}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
then slope of the directrix = {tex}\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} {directrix and the axis are perpendicular to each other}
Let the directrix intersect the axis at K (r, s)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{r+2}{2}=-1{/tex}, {tex}\frac{s+3}{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = - 4, s = - 1
Equation of the directrix is
(y + 1) = {tex}\frac{-3}{2}(x+4){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 2y + 14 = 0

questions.question: The equation of the directrix of the parabola whose vertex and focus are (1, 4) and (2, 6) respectively is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the directrix of the parabola whose vertex and focus are (1, 4) and (2, 6) respectively is
questions.explanation:
Given:
The vertex and the focus of a parabola are (1, 4) and (2, 6), respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of the axis of the parabola = {tex}\frac{6-4}{2-1}=2{/tex}
Slope of the directrix = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} [Directrix and axis are perpendicular to eachother]
Let the directrix intersect the axis at K (r, s)
{tex}\frac{r+2}{2}=1{/tex}, {tex}\frac{s+6}{2}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 0, s = 2
Equation of the directrix:
(y - 2) = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}(x-0){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2y = 4

questions.question: If V and S are respectively the vertex and focus of the parabola y^2 + 6y + 2x + 5 = 0, then SV =
questions.question_raw: If V and S are respectively the vertex and focus of the parabola y2 + 6y + 2x + 5 = 0, then SV =
questions.explanation:
Given:
The vertex and the focus of a parabola are V and S, respectively.
The given equation of parabola can be rewritten as follows:
(y + 3)2 - 9 + 5 + 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)2 + 2x = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)2 = 4 - 2x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y + 3)2 = - 2 (x - 2)
Let
Y = y + 3, X = x - 2
Rewriting the equation in terms of X and Y
Y2 = - 2X
Vertex = (X = 0, Y = 0) = (x - 2 = 0, y + 3 = 0) = (x = 2, y = - 3)
Comparing with y2 = 4ax: 4a = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Focus = {tex}\left(X=\frac{-1}{2}, Y=0\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(x-2=\frac{-1}{2}, y+3=0\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(x=\frac{3}{2}, y=-3\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SV = {tex}\sqrt{\left(2-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+(-3+3)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The directrix of the parabola x^2 - 4x - 8y + 12 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The directrix of the parabola x2 - 4x - 8y + 12 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given:
x2 - 4x - 8y + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 - 4 - 8y + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = 8y - 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = 8 (y - 1)
Putting X = x - 2, Y = y - 1
Rewriting the equation in terms of X and Y.
X2 = 8Y
Comparing with X2 = 4aY
we get, a = 2
Equation of the directrix: Y = - a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Y = - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 1 = - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 2 + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = - 1

questions.question: The line 2x - y + 4 = 0 cuts the parabola y^2 = 8x in P and Q. The mid-point of PQ is
questions.question_raw: The line 2x - y + 4 = 0 cuts the parabola y2 = 8x in P and Q. The mid-point of PQ is
questions.explanation:
Let the coordinates of P and Q be {tex}\left(a t_{1}^{2}, 2 a t_{1}\right){/tex} and {tex}\left(a t_{2}^{2}, 2 a t_{2}\right){/tex} respectively.
Slope of PQ = {tex}\frac{2 a t_{2}-2 a t_{1}}{a t_{2}^{2}-a t_{1}^{2}}{/tex}    ....... (i)
But, the slope of PQ is equal to the slope of 2x - y + 4 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of PQ = {tex}\frac{-2}{-1}=2{/tex}
From (i), {tex}\frac{2 a t_{2}-2 a t_{1}}{a t_{2}^{2}-a t_{1}^{2}}=2{/tex}    ......... (ii)
Putting 4a = 8
so, a = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Focus of the parabola = (a, 0) = (2, 0)
Using equation (ii)
{tex}\frac{4\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}{2\left(t_{2}^{2}-t_{1}^{2}\right)}=2{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}{\left(t_{2}^{2}-t_{1}^{2}\right)}=1{/tex}
As, points P and Q lie on 2x - y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}P\left(a t_{1}^{2}, 2 a t_{1}\right) = P\left(2 t_{1}^{2}, 4 t_{1}\right){/tex} lie on line 2x - y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\left(2 t_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} - (4t1) + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}t_{1}^{2}{/tex} - t1 + 1 = 0   ......... (iii)
Also, {tex}Q\left(a t_{2}^{2}, 2 a t_{2}\right) o r P\left(2 t_{2}^{2}, 4 t_{2}\right){/tex} lie on line 2x - y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\left(2 t_{2}^{2}\right){/tex} -(4t2) + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}t_{2}^{2}{/tex} - t2 + 1 = 0   ............ (iv)
Adding (iii) and (iv), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}t_{1}^{2}{/tex} - t1 + 1 + {tex}t_{2}^{2}{/tex} - t2 + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}\right){/tex} - (t1 + t2) + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}\right){/tex} - 1 + 2 = 0    [t1+ t2 = 1, proved above]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}\right){/tex} = - 1   ......(v)
Let (x1, y1) be the mid-point of PQ
Then, we have: y1 = {tex}\frac{2 a t_{2}+2 a t_{1}}{2}{/tex} = 2 (t1 + t2) = 2
x1 = {tex}\frac{a t_{1}^{2}+a t_{2}^{2}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}{/tex} = - 1  [ from equation (v)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1, y1) = (-1, 2)

questions.question: In the parabola y^2 = 4ax, the length of the chord passing through the vertex and inclined to the axis at $\pi$/4 is
questions.question_raw: In the parabola y2 = 4ax, the length of the chord passing through the vertex and inclined to the axis at {tex}\pi{/tex}/4 is
questions.explanation:
Let OP be the chord.
Let the coordinates of P be (x1, y1)
From the figure, we have: O is a right-angled triangle 
OP2 = {tex}x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}{/tex}     (i)
And,
tan{tex}\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{y_{1}}{x_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = y1     (ii)
Also,
(x1, y1) lies on the parabola.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}{/tex} = 4ax1    (iii)
Using (ii) and (iii):
{tex}x_{1}^{2}{/tex} = 4ax1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = 4a    (iv)
x1 = y1 = 4a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From (iv), (i) and (ii), we have:
OP2 = (4a)2 + (4a)2 = 32a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OP = 4{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} a
Therefore, the length of the chord is 4{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} a units

questions.question: The equation 16x^2 + y^2 + 8xy - 74x - 78y + 212 = 0 represents
questions.question_raw: The equation 16x2 + y2 + 8xy - 74x - 78y + 212 = 0 represents
questions.explanation:
Comparing the given equation with ax2 + by2 + 2hxy + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, we get:
a = 16, b = 1, h = 4
We have: h2 = 16
now, ab = 16 
h2 = ab
Thus, the given equation represents a parabola

questions.question: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola y^2 + 8x - 2y + 17 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola y2 + 8x - 2y + 17 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given:
y2 + 8x − 2y + 17 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − 1)2 − 1 + 8x + 17 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − 1)2 + 8x + 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − 1)2 = − 8 (x + 2)
Let X = x + 2, Y = y − 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y2 = − 8 X
Comparing above equation with y2 = 4ax:
a = 2
Length of the latus rectum = 4a = 8 units

questions.question: Equation of the diameter of the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 4y = 0 which passes through the origin is
questions.question_raw: Equation of the diameter of the circle x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y = 0 which passes through the origin is
questions.explanation:
Let the diameter of the circle be y = mx. since the diameter of the circle passes through its centre, (1, -2) satisfies the equation of the diameter.
-2 = m(1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m = -2
Substituting the value of m in the equation of diameter. y = -2x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + y = 0
Hence, the required equation of the diameter is 2x + y = 0

questions.question: Equation of the circle through origin which cuts intercepts of length a and b on axes is
questions.question_raw: Equation of the circle through origin which cuts intercepts of length a and b on axes is
questions.explanation:
Centre of the circle is {tex}\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2}\right){/tex} and its radius is {tex}\sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
Equation of circle:
{tex}\left(x-\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2}-\frac{1}{4}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x - a)2 + (2y - b)2 = (a2 + b2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x2 + a2 - 4ax + 4y2 + b2 - 4by = a2 + b2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - ax + y2 - by = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 -ax - by = 0

questions.question: The vertex of the parabola x^2 + 8x +12y + 4 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The vertex of the parabola x2 + 8x +12y + 4 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given:
x2 + 8x + 12y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 4)2 − 16 + 12y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 4)2 + 12y − 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 4)2 = − 12 (y − 1)
Let X = x + 4, Y = y − 1
Rewriting the equation in terms of X and Y.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X2 = − 12 Y
Vertex = (X = 0, Y = 0) = (x + 4 = 0, y − 1 = 0) = (x = − 4, y = 1)
Hence, the vertex is at (−4, 1)

questions.question: If the circles x^2 + y^2 + 2ax + c = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 2by + c = 0 touch each other, then
questions.question_raw: If the circles x2 + y2 + 2ax + c = 0 and x2 + y2 + 2by + c = 0 touch each other, then
questions.explanation:
x2 + y2 + 2ax + c = 0......(1)
And, x2 + y2 + 2by + c = 0.....(2)
For circle (1), we have:
Centre =(-a, 0) = C1
For circle (2), we have:
Centre =(0, -b) = C2
Let the circles intersect at point P
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Coordinates of P = Mid point of C1C2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Coordinates of {tex}P=\left(\frac{-a + 0}{2}, \frac{0-b}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{-a}{2}, \frac{-b}{2}\right){/tex}
Now, we have:
P C1 = radius of circle(1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{\left(-a + \frac{a}{2}\right)^{2} + \left(0-\frac{b}{2}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}-c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}}{4} + \frac{b^{2}}{4}=a^{2}-c{/tex}   ......(3)
Also, radius of circle(1) = radius of circle (2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{a^{2}-c}=\sqrt{b^{2}-c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2}=b^{2}{/tex}.....(4)
From (3) and (4), we have:
{tex}\frac{a^{2}}{2}=a^{2}-c{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{a^2}{2}{/tex} - a2 = - c
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}}{2}=c{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{a^{2}}=\frac{1}{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{a^{2}}=\frac{1}{c}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{c}{/tex}

questions.question: The vertex of the parabola (y - 2)^2 = 16 (x - 1) is
questions.question_raw: The vertex of the parabola (y - 2)2 = 16 (x - 1) is
questions.explanation:
Given:
(y − 2)2 = 16 (x − 1)
Let X = x − 1, Y = y − 2
Rewriting the equation in terms of X and Y.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y2 = 16 X
Vertex = (X = 0, Y = 0) = (x − 1 = 0, y − 2 = 0) = (x = 1, y = 2)
Hence, the vertex is at (1, 2)

questions.question: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola 4y^2 + 2x - 20y +17 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola 4y2 + 2x - 20y +17 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given:
4y2 + 2x − 20y + 17 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 + {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} − 5y + {tex}\frac{17}{4}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex})2 + {tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} − 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex})2 = − 1 ({tex}\frac{x}{2}{/tex} − 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y − {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex})2 = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} (x − 4)
Let X = x − 4, Y = y − {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Rewriting the above equation in X and Y form.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y2 = {tex}\frac{-X}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the latus rectum = 4a = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} units

questions.question: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola x^2 - 4x - 8y +12 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The length of the latus-rectum of the parabola x2 - 4x - 8y +12 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
x2 − 4x − 8y + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x − 2)2 − 8y + 8 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x − 2)2 = 8y − 8 = 8 (y − 1)
Let X = x − 2, Y = y − 1
Rewriting the equation in X and Y form.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X2 = 8Y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the latus rectum = 4a = 8 units

questions.question: Which of the following points lie on the parabola x^2 = 4ay ?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following points lie on the parabola x2 = 4ay ?
questions.explanation:
Substituting x = 2at, y = at2 in the given equation:
(2at)2 = 4a (at2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a2t2 = 4a2t2 so, L.H.S.  = R.H.S.
Hence, (2at, at2) lies on the parabola x2 = 4ay

questions.question: The equation of the parabola whose focus is (1, -1) and the directrix is x + y + 7 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the parabola whose focus is (1, -1) and the directrix is x + y + 7 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Let P (x, y) be any point on the parabola whose focus is S (1, −1) and the directrix is x + y + 7 = 0

Draw PM perpendicular to x + y + 7 = 0
Then, By definition,
SP = PM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SP2 = PM2 {squaring on both the sides}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x − 1)2 + (y + 1)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{x+y+7}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x − 1)2 + (y + 1)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{x+y+7}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(x2 + 1 − 2x + y2 + 1 + 2y) = x2 + y2 + 49 + 2xy + 14y + 14x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x2 + 2 − 4x + 2y2 + 2 + 4y) = x2 + y2 + 49 + 2xy + 14y + 14x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 − 45 − 10y − 2xy − 18x = 0
Hence, the required equation is x2 + y2 − 2xy − 18x − 10y − 45 = 0

questions.question: The locus of the points of trisection of the double ordinates of a parabola is a
questions.question_raw: The locus of the points of trisection of the double ordinates of a parabola is a
questions.explanation:
Suppose PQÂ is a double ordinate of the parabola y2 = 4ax
Let RÂ and SÂ be the points of trisection of the double ordinates.
Let (h, k)Â be the coordinates of R
Then,Â 
OL = h and RL = k
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Â RS = RL + LS = k + k = 2k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â PR = RS = SQ = 2k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â LP = LR + RP = k + 2k = 3k
Thus, the coordinates of P are (h, 3k)Â 
Point P lies on the parabolaÂ y2 = 4ax so,
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Â 9k2 = 4ah
Hence, the locus of the point (h, k)Â is 9y2 = 4axÂ i.e. y2 =Â {tex}\left(\frac{4 a}{9}\right){/tex}Â xÂ which represents a parabola.

questions.question: The equation of the parabola with focus (0, 0) and directrix x + y = 4 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the parabola with focus (0, 0) and directrix x + y = 4 is
questions.explanation:
Let P (x, y)Â be any point on the parabola whose focus is S (0, 0)Â and the directrix is x + y = 4

Draw PMÂ perpendicular to x + y = 4
Then, we have: SP = PM
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â SP2 = PM2Â Â {Squaring on both the sides}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â (x - 0)2 + (y - 0)2 =Â {tex}\left(\frac{x+y-4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â x2 + y2 =Â {tex}\left(\frac{x+y-4}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â 2x2 + 2y2 = x2 + y2 + 16 + 2xy - 8x - 8y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â x2 + y2 - 2xy + 8x + 8y - 16 = 0

questions.question: The area of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 8y - 25 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The area of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y - 25 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
We need to find the area of the equilateral triangle that is inscribed in the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y - 25 = 0.

We know that for a circle x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre = (- g, - f)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius = {tex}\sqrt{g^{2}+f^{2}-c}{/tex}
For x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y - 25 = 0
g = 3 and f = 4 and c = - 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius(r1) = {tex}\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}-(-25)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r1 = {tex}\sqrt{9+16+25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r1 = {tex}\sqrt{50}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r1 = {tex}5 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
From the figure we can see that,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos 30o = {tex}\frac{\frac{a}{2}}{r_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a}{2(5 \sqrt{2})}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}5 \sqrt{6}{/tex}
We know that area of the equilateral triangle with side length ‘a’ is {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A =  {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}(5 \sqrt{6})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 150
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{75 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{75 \sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{225 \sqrt{3}}{6}{/tex}

a = 5, b = 2{tex}\sqrt 5{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of the hyperbola whose conjugate axis is 5 and the distance between the foci is 13, is:

Conjugate axis is 5 and distance between foci = 13
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b = 5 and 2ae = 13
Now, also we know for hyperbola
b2 = a2(e2 - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{25}{4}=\frac{(13)^{2}}{4 e^{2}}{/tex} (e2 - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{25}{4}=\frac{169}{4}-\frac{169}{4 e^{2}}{/tex} or e2 = {tex}\frac{169}{144}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 25x2 - 144y2 = 900

questions.question: If the latus rectum of an ellipse be equal to half of its minor axis, then its eccentricity is:

questions.question: If the eccentricity of an ellipse be $\frac 58$ and the distance between its foci be 10, then its latus rectum is:
questions.question_raw: If the eccentricity of an ellipse be {tex}\frac 58{/tex} and the distance between its foci be 10, then its latus rectum is:
questions.explanation:

We know that eccentricity = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{a}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{5}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 8
Also, {tex}c^{2}=a^{2}-b^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} b2 = a2 - c2 = 82 - 52 = 64 - 25 = 39
Length of the latus-rectum = {tex}\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=\frac{2 \times 39}{8}=\frac{39}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If the foci and vertices of an ellipse be ($\pm$1, 0) and ($\pm$2â€‹â€‹â€‹â€‹, 0), then the minor axis of the ellips is:

ae = 1, a = 2, e = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Hence minor axis = {tex}2\sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The eccentricity of an ellipse is $\frac 23$, latus rectum is 5 and centre is (0, 0). The equation of the ellipse is:

questions.question: The latus rectum of an ellipse is 10 and the minor axis is equal to the distance between the foci. The equation of the ellipse is:
questions.question_raw: The latus rectum of an ellipse is 10 and the minor axis is equal to the distance between the foci. The equation of the ellipse is:
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}{/tex} = 10 and 2b = 2ae
Also b2 = a2(1 - e2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2 = (1 - e2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} or b = 5{tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}, a = 10
Hence equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{(10)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{(5 \sqrt{2})^{2}}{/tex} = 1
i.e., x2 + 2y2 = 100

questions.question: If a double ordinate of the parabola y^2 = 4ax be of length 8a, then the angle between the lines joining the vertex of the parabola to the ends of this double ordinate is
questions.question_raw: If a double ordinate of the parabola y2 = 4ax be of length 8a, then the angle between the lines joining the vertex of the parabola to the ends of this double ordinate is
questions.explanation:
Let us assume that the ends of double ordinate are P(at2, 2at) and P′ = (at2, -2at)
Now, we also have PP′ = 8a 
{tex}\sqrt{(at^2Â âˆ’ at^2)^2Â + (2at + 2at)^2}Â = 8a{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = 2
Hence P = (at2, 2at) = (4a, 4a) and P′ = (at2, -2at) = (4a, -4a)
Now, the vertex is O(0, 0) for the parabola.
Slope of OP = m1 = {tex}\frac{4 a-0}{4 a-0}{/tex} = 1
Slope of OP' = m2 = {tex}\frac{4 a-0}{-4 a-0}{/tex} = -1
Angle between the lines OP′ and OP = tan-1{tex}\frac{\mathbf{m}_{1}-\mathbf{m}_{2}}{\mathbf{1}+\mathbf{m}_{1} \mathbf{m}_{2}}{/tex} = tan-1{tex}\frac{2}{0}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence, the line joining the origin to ends of the double ordinate will be perpendicular to each other.

questions.question: The distance between the directrices of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{20}$ = 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The distance between the directrices of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{20}{/tex} = 1 is:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 6, {tex} b=2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20 = 36(1 - e2)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}e=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Now, directrices are: {tex}x=\pm \frac{a}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance between directrix
{tex}=\frac{2 a}{e}=\frac{2 \times 6}{2 / 3}=18{/tex}

questions.question: The focal distance of a point on the parabola y^2 = 16x whose ordinate is twice the abscissa, is

Points are (0, 0), (4, 8). Hence focal distances are respectively 0 + a = 4, 4 + 4 = 8. ({tex}\because{/tex} a = 4)

questions.question: The eccentric angle of the point where the line, 5x - 3y = $8 \sqrt{2}$ is a normal to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}$ = 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The eccentric angle of the point where the line, 5x - 3y = {tex}8 \sqrt{2}{/tex} is a normal to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 is:
questions.explanation:
The equation of the normal to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 at the point P(a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) is
ax sec {tex}\theta{/tex} - by cosec {tex}\theta{/tex} = a2 - b2
Given, ellipse equation as {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{5^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{3^{2}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Length of major axis, a = 5a = 5 and length of minor axis, b = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The required equation of normal is 5x sec {tex}\theta{/tex} - 3y cosec {tex}\theta{/tex} = 52 - 32
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x sec {tex}\theta{/tex} - 3y cosec {tex}\theta{/tex} = 16 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A
Given normal equation 5x - 3y = {tex}8 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Multiplying both side with {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 \sqrt{2} x-3 \sqrt{2} y{/tex} = 16 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B
Comparing equation A and B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sec{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation to the locus of the middle point of the portion of the tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}$ = 1 included between the co-ordinate axes is the curve:

questions.explanation:
Tangent to ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 is
{tex}\frac{x}{4} \cos \theta+\frac{y}{3} \sin \theta{/tex} = 1
Let P be point of intersection of x-axis & Q be Let Q be the point on tangent and y-axis
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P = {tex}\left(\frac{4}{\cos \theta}, 0\right){/tex} & Q = {tex}\left(0, \frac{3}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex}
Let M = (x, y)
x = {tex}\frac{2}{\cos \theta}{/tex} & y = {tex}\frac{3}{2 \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{4}{x^{2}}+\frac{9}{4 y^{2}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 16x2 + 9y2 = 4x2y2

questions.question: If F_1 and F_2 are the feet of the perpendiculars from the foci S_1 and S_2 of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{5}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}$ = 1 on the tangent at any point P on the ellipse, then (S_1F_1) (S_2F_2) is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If F1 and F2 are the feet of the perpendiculars from the foci S1 and S2 of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{5}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}{/tex} = 1 on the tangent at any point P on the ellipse, then (S1F1) (S2F2) is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Any tangent to the ellipse has equation y = mx + {tex}\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
And the foci have the coordinate as S1(ae, 0) and  S2(-ae, 0)
Let the distance from S1 to the tangent be f1 and S2 to the tangent be f2
So S1f1 = {tex}\frac{a e m+\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}{/tex}
And S2f2 = {tex}\frac{-a e m+\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}{/tex}
So the product of (S1f1) {tex}\times{/tex} (S2f2) = {tex}\frac{a e m+\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}} \times \frac{-a e m+\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}-a^{2} e^{2} m^{2}}{m^{2}+1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{a^{2} m^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)+b^{2}}{m^{2}+1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{b^{2}\left(m^{2}+1\right)}{m^{2}+1}{/tex} [as a2(1 - e2) = b2]
= b2
Hence b2 = 3

questions.question: The eccentricity of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1 whose latus rectum is half of its major axis is:
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 whose latus rectum is half of its major axis is:
questions.explanation:
Latus rectum = {tex}2 \frac{b^{2}}{a}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (2a) (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2b2 = a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a2(1 - e2) = a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(1 - e2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - e2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a circle passes through the point (1, 2) and cuts the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4 orthogonally, then the locus of its centre is:
questions.question_raw: If a circle passes through the point (1, 2) and cuts the circle x2 + y2 = 4 orthogonally, then the locus of its centre is:
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of circle be
x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0
Since, this passes through (1, 2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 12 + 22 + 2g(1) + 2f(2) + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + 2g + 4f + c = 0 ...(i)
Also, the circle x2 + y2 = 4 intersects the circle
x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 orthogonally
{tex}\therefore 2(g \cdot 0+f \cdot 0)=c-4 \Rightarrow{/tex} c = 4
On putting the value of c in Eq. (i), we get
2g + 4f + 9 = 0
Hence, the locus of centre (-g, -f) is
- 2x + 4y - 9 = 0
or 2x + 4y - 9 = 0

questions.question: The tangent drawn from the origin to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2ax - 2by + a^2 = 0 are perpendicular if:
questions.question_raw: The tangent drawn from the origin to the circle x2 + y2 - 2ax - 2by + a2 = 0 are perpendicular if:
questions.explanation:
Given circle equation x2 + y2 - 2ax - 2by + a2 = 0
Center: (a, b) and Radius = {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex}
Both tangents are drawn from origin and perpendicular to each other. So, two tangent are x and y axis.
Hence, {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}-b^{2}}{/tex} = a = b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = b2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - b2 = 0

questions.question: Locus of the point of intersection of the pair of perpendicular tangents to the circles x^2 + y^2 = 1 and x^2 + y^2 = 7 is the director circle of the circle with radius.
questions.question_raw: Locus of the point of intersection of the pair of perpendicular tangents to the circles x2 + y2 = 1 and x2 + y2 = 7 is the director circle of the circle with radius.
questions.explanation:
We know that,
It's a circle with center O,
So, we need a point to find the radius.
x = {tex}\sqrt 7{/tex}â€‹, the intersection (1, {tex}\sqrt 7{/tex}â€‹) is on the circle
The radius is 
= {tex}\sqrt{1^{2}+\sqrt{7}^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt 8{/tex}
= {tex}2\sqrt 2{/tex}

questions.question: Suppose two perpendicular tangents can be drawn from the origin to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 2cy + 17 = 0, for some real c, then |c| is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Suppose two perpendicular tangents can be drawn from the origin to the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 2cy + 17 = 0, for some real c, then |c| is equal to:
questions.explanation:
To a circle (x − h)2 + (y − k)2 = r2,
perpendicular tangents can be drawn from a point on the circle which satisfies the equation (x − h)2 + (y − k)2 = 2r2
Here, the circle's equation can be written as (x − 3)2 + (y − p)2 = p2 − 8
Thus, the locus of points from where perpendicular tangents can be drawn to this circle is given by (x − 3)2 + (y − p)2 = 2(p2 − 8)
Since origin lies on this locus, (0 − 3)2 + (0 − p)2 = 2(p2 − 8)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 9 + p2 = 2p2 − 16
{tex}\therefore{/tex} p2 = 25
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |p| = 5

questions.question: If the eccentricities of the hyperbolas $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1 and $\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}$ = 1 be e and e_1, then $\frac{1}{e^{2}}+\frac{1}{e_{1}^{2}}$ =
questions.question_raw: If the eccentricities of the hyperbolas {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 and {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} = 1 be e and e1, then {tex}\frac{1}{e^{2}}+\frac{1}{e_{1}^{2}}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}{/tex}
e2 = {tex}\frac{a^{2} \ + \ b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
e1 = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}e_{1}^{2}=\frac{b^{2} \ + \ a^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{e_{1}^{2}}+\frac{1}{e^{2}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: If the chord of the hyperbola x^2 - y^2 = a^2 touches the parabola y^2 = 4ax, then the locus of the middle points of this chord is:
questions.question_raw: If the chord of the hyperbola x2 - y2 = a2 touches the parabola y2 = 4ax, then the locus of the middle points of this chord is:
questions.explanation:
Equation of chord of hyperbola x2 - y2 = a2 with mid-point as (h, k) is given by
xh - yk = h2 - k2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{h}}{\mathbf{k}} \mathbf{x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\left(\mathbf{h}^{2}-\mathbf{k}^{2}\right)}{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
This will touch the parabola y2 = 4ax if
{tex}-\frac{\left(\mathrm{h}^{2}-\mathrm{k}^{2}\right)}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{h} / \mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k2(h - a) = h3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The locus is y2(x - a) = x3

questions.question: A common tangent to the conics x^2 = 6 and 2x^2 - 4y^2 = 9, is:
questions.question_raw: A common tangent to the conics x2 = 6 and 2x2 - 4y2 = 9, is:
questions.explanation:
x2 - 6y = 4ay
4a = 6
a = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Tangent is x = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{y}}{\mathbf{t}}{/tex} + at
tx = y + {tex}\frac{3}{2} t^{2}{/tex}
For 2x2 - 4y2 = 9
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\frac{9}{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{\frac{9}{4}}{/tex} = 1
y = mx + {tex}\sqrt{\left(\mathbf{a}^{2} \mathbf{m}^{2}-\mathbf{b}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
y = mx + {tex}\sqrt{\frac{9 m^{2}}{2}-\frac{9}{4}}{/tex}
Both the equations represent the same line
m4 - 2m2 + 1 = 0
m2 = 1
y = tx - {tex}\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{t}^{2}{/tex}
t = 1
y = x - {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
x - y = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: For the circle x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 8y + 9 = 0, which of the following statement is true?
questions.question_raw: For the circle x2 + y2 + 6x - 8y + 9 = 0, which of the following statement is true?
questions.explanation:
{tex}2 \sqrt{g^{2}-c}=2 \sqrt{9-9}{/tex} = 0
Intercept cut on x-axis is zero. Hence circle touches x-axis.

questions.question: The eccentricity of a hyperbola passing through the points (3, 0), (3$\sqrt 2$, 2) will be:
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of a hyperbola passing through the points (3, 0), (3{tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}, 2) will be:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{9}{a^{2}}{/tex} = 1
a = 3 and {tex}\frac{18}{a^{2}}-\frac{4}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1
b2 = 4
Therefore, e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: Two circles neither intersect nor touch each other. Then the locus of the centre of a variable circle which touches both the circles externally is:
questions.question_raw: Two circles neither intersect nor touch each other. Then the locus of the centre of a variable circle which touches both the circles externally is:
questions.explanation:
Let C1, C2 be the centres of two given circles of radii r1 and r2 respectively. Let C be the centre of a circle of radius r which touches the two circles externally. Then, CC1 = r + r1 and CC2 = r + r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CC1 − CC2 = r1 − r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C lies on a hyperbola having two foci at C1 and C2.
Hence, the locus of C is a hyperbola.

questions.question: The locus of the mid points of the chords of the circle x^2 + y^2 = 16, which are tangent to the hyperbola 9x^2 - 16y^2 = 144 is:
questions.question_raw: The locus of the mid points of the chords of the circle x2 + y2 = 16, which are tangent to the hyperbola 9x2 - 16y2 = 144 is:
questions.explanation:
Let mid point of the chord is P(h, k)
Thus equation of chord with P as mid point to the circle x2 + y2 = 16 is given, T = S1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} hx + ky = h2 + k2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}-\frac{\mathbf{h}}{\mathbf{k}} \mathbf{x}+\frac{\mathbf{h}^{2}+\mathbf{k}^{2}}{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} ...(i)
Given hyperbola may be written as, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1
Since line (i) is tangent to the hyperbola, so using condition of tangency,
c2 = a2m2 - b2
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathbf{h}^{2}+\mathbf{k}^{2}}{\mathbf{k}}\right)^{2}=16\left(\frac{\mathbf{h}^{2}}{\mathbf{k}^{2}}-\mathbf{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (h2 + k2)2 = 16h2 - 9k2
Hence locus of p(h, k) is, (x2 + y2)2 = 16x2 - 9y2

questions.question: Let a and b be any two non-zero real numbers satisfying $\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}$. Then, the foot of perpendicular drawn from origin on the variable line $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}$ = 1, lies on:
questions.question_raw: Let a and b be any two non-zero real numbers satisfying {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}. Then, the foot of perpendicular drawn from origin on the variable line {tex}\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}{/tex} = 1, lies on:

options.text: ellipse with length of semi-major axis 2
hyperbola with length of semi-transverse axis $\sqrt{2}$

questions.explanation:
Given equation of line is
{tex}\frac{\mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{a}}+\frac{\mathbf{y}}{\mathbf{b}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} bx + ay - ab = 0 ...(i)
Slope of the line is {tex}-\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
Slope of perpendicular line is {tex}\frac{\mathbf{a}}{\mathbf{b}}{/tex}
Equation of line passing through origin having slope {tex}\frac{\mathbf{a}}{\mathbf{b}}{/tex} is
y = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{a}}{\mathbf{b}}{/tex}x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} by - ax = 0 ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get the foot of perpendicular.
x = {tex}\frac{a b^{2}}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex} ...(iii)
y = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{a}^{2} \mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^{2}+\mathbf{b}^{2}}{/tex} ...(iv)
Squaring and adding (iii) and (iv), we get
x2 + y2 = {tex}\frac{a^{2} b^{2}}{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 4 (Given, {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{a^{2} b^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex})
Which is a circle of radius 2.

questions.question: If PQ is a double originate of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1 such that OPQ is an equilateral triangle, O being the centre of the hyperbola, then the eccentricity e of the hyperbola satisfies:
questions.question_raw: If PQ is a double originate of the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 such that OPQ is an equilateral triangle, O being the centre of the hyperbola, then the eccentricity e of the hyperbola satisfies:
questions.explanation:
Let the hyperbola be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 and any double originate PQ be (a sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, b tan {tex}\theta{/tex}), (a sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, -b tan {tex}\theta{/tex}) and O is centre (0, 0).
{tex}\triangle{/tex}OPQ being equilateral
Thus tan30o = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{b \ tan} \theta}{\operatorname{a \ sec} \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \cdot \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} = cosec2{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3(e2 - 1) = cosec2{tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, cosec2{tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\geq{/tex} 1
Therefore, 3(e2 - 1) {tex}\geq{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2 {tex}\geq{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e > {tex}\frac {2}{\sqrt 3}{/tex}

questions.question: If the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1 and the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{144}-\frac{y^{2}}{81}=\frac{1}{25}$ coincide, then the value of b^2 is:
questions.question_raw: If the foci of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 and the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{144}-\frac{y^{2}}{81}=\frac{1}{25}{/tex} coincide, then the value of b2 is:
questions.explanation:
Eccentricity for {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 is b2 = a2(1 - e2)
and eccentricity for {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{ \frac {144} {25}}-\frac{y^{2}}{\frac {81}{ 25}}{/tex} = 1 is
e1 = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{a}_{1}^{2}+\mathbf{b}_{1}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
e1 = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{81}{144}}=\frac{15}{12}{/tex}
Foci = a1e1 = {tex}\frac{12}{5} \times \frac{15}{12}{/tex} = 3
Focus of hyperbola is (3, 0) = (ae, 0)
So focus of ellipse is (ae, 0) = (4e, 0)
As their foci are same
4e = 3
e = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
e2 = 1 - {tex}\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}^{2}}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{b}^{2}}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{1 6}}{/tex} = 1 - e2 = 1 - {tex}\frac{9}{16}{/tex}
b2 = 7

questions.question: The equation 16x^2 - 3y^2 - 32x + 12 y - 44 = 0 represents a hyperbola:
questions.question_raw: The equation 16x2 - 3y2 - 32x + 12 y - 44 = 0 represents a hyperbola:

options.text: the length of whose conjugate axis is 4
the length of whose transverse axis is $4 \sqrt{3}$

questions.explanation:
16(x2 - 2x)-3(y2 - 4y) = 44
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16(x - 1)2 −3(y - 2)2 = 44 + 16 − 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16(x - 1)2 - 3(y - 2)2 = 48
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{x}-1)^{2}}{3}-\frac{(\mathrm{y}-2)^{2}}{16}{/tex} = 1
Here, centre (1, −2), length of transverse axis
= 2a = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Length of conjugate axis = 2b = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 8
and eccentricity, e = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+\frac{16}{3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\frac{19}{3}}{/tex}

questions.question: If the tangents are drawn from any point on the line x + y = 3 to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 9, then the chord of contact passes through the point:
questions.question_raw: If the tangents are drawn from any point on the line x + y = 3 to the circle x2 + y2 = 9, then the chord of contact passes through the point:
questions.explanation:
Let ({tex}\alpha{/tex}, 3 − {tex}\alpha{/tex}) be any point on x + y = 3.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of chord of contact is {tex}\alpha{/tex}x + (3 − {tex}\alpha{/tex})y = 9
i.e., {tex}\alpha{/tex}(x − y) + 3y − 9 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The chord passes through the point (3, 3) for all values of {tex}\alpha{/tex}.

questions.question: The equation of the transverse and conjugate axes of a hyperbola are respectively x + 2y - 3 = 0, 2x - y + 4 = 0 and their respective lengths are $\sqrt{2}$ and $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$. The equation of the hyperbola is:
questions.question_raw: The equation of the transverse and conjugate axes of a hyperbola are respectively x + 2y - 3 = 0, 2x - y + 4 = 0 and their respective lengths are {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}. The equation of the hyperbola is:
questions.explanation:
Given, 2a = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Also, 2b = {tex}\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
If we take the two axes as the new coordinate system, and the point of intersection of the axes as the new origin, then in the new coordinate system, equation of the hyperbola will be:
{tex}\frac{\mathbf{X}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}^{2}}-\frac{\mathbf{Y}^{2}}{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2X2 - 3Y2 = 1
Let P(x, y) be the coordinates of a point on the hyperbola in original x-y system, then
X = {tex}\frac{|2 x-y+4|}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}, Y = {tex}\frac{|\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{2 y}-\mathbf{3}|}{\sqrt{\mathbf{5}}}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} X is the distance of a point on hyperbola from 2x - y + 4 = 0 and Y is the distance of a point on hyperbola from x + 2y - 3 = 0)
So, the required equation is
{tex}\frac{2(2 x-y+4)^{2}}{5}-\frac{3(x+2 y-3)^{2}}{5}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The equation of common tangent to the parabola y^2 = 8x and the hyperbola 3x^2 - y^2 = 3 is:
questions.question_raw: The equation of common tangent to the parabola y2 = 8x and the hyperbola 3x2 - y2 = 3 is:
questions.explanation:
The equation of tangents to y2 = 8x is y = mx + {tex}\frac{2}{\mathbf{m}}{/tex}
Also, the equation of tangent to {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1}-\frac{y^{2}}{3}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = mx {tex}\pm{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{m^{2}-3}{/tex}
On comparing, we get m = {tex}\pm{/tex}2
or tangent as 2x {tex}\pm{/tex} y + 1 = 0

questions.question: If the latus rectum of a hyperbola subtends right angle at its centre, then its eccentricity is:
questions.question_raw: If the latus rectum of a hyperbola subtends right angle at its centre, then its eccentricity is:
questions.explanation:
Consider the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1,
Point F is the focus on the positive X-axis and AB is the latus rectum.
The coordinates of F are (ae, 0) and the coordinates of the ends of the latus rectum, AF are BF are (ae, {tex}\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}}{/tex}) and (ae, {tex}-\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}}{/tex}) respectively.
It is given that the latus rectum subtends an angle of 90o at the centre.
{tex}\angle{/tex}AOF = 45o
since {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BOF
{tex}\angle{/tex}OAF = 45o since {tex}\angle{/tex}OFA = 90o
OF = AF
But OF = ae
AF = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}}{/tex} = ae
e = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{b}^{2}}{\mathbf{a}^{2}}{/tex}
e = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the middle points of the focal chords of the parabola, y^2 = 4x is:
questions.question_raw: The locus of the middle points of the focal chords of the parabola, y2 = 4x is:
questions.explanation:
We have 
y2 = 4x
We know that ends of focal chords are (at2, 2at) and {tex}\left(\frac{a}{t^{2}},-\frac{2 a}{t}\right){/tex}
here a = 1
Let (h, k) be the mid point of the chord.
{tex}\Rightarrow {k}=\frac{2 {t}+\left(-\frac{2}{t}\right)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 {k}=2 {t}-\frac{2}{{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {k}={t}-\frac{{1}}{{t}}{/tex} ...(i)
h = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{t}^{2}+\frac{{1}}{{t}^{2}}}{{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 h=\left(t-\frac{1}{t}\right)^{2}{/tex} + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h = k2 + 2
To get equation of locus replace
h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} x and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} y
2x = y2 + 2
y2 = 2(x − 1)

questions.question: The set of points (x, y) whose distance from the line y = 2x + 2 is the same as the distance from (2, 0) is a parabola. This parabola is congruent to the parabola in standard form y = Kx^2 for some K which is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The set of points (x, y) whose distance from the line y = 2x + 2 is the same as the distance from (2, 0) is a parabola. This parabola is congruent to the parabola in standard form y = Kx2 for some K which is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Distance from (2, 0) to 2x − y + 2 = 0 is equal to {tex}\frac{\sqrt{6}}{5}{/tex} = semi latus rectum
{tex}\therefore{/tex} length of latus rectum is {tex}\frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{x}^{2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}} y \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}=\frac{12}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
K = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{12}{/tex}

questions.explanation:

{tex}\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}=\frac{4}{c}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{4}{c}{/tex}
c = {tex}\frac{24}5{/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the foot of the perpendiculars drawn from the vertex on a variable tangent to the parabola y^2 = 4ax is:
questions.question_raw: The locus of the foot of the perpendiculars drawn from the vertex on a variable tangent to the parabola y2 = 4ax is:
questions.explanation:
Tangent at (at2, 2at) is given by ty = x + at2
Slope of OP {tex}\times{/tex} Slope of tangent = −1
{tex}\frac{{b}}{{h}} \times \frac{{1}}{{t}}{/tex} = -1
k = -ht
{tex}\Rightarrow t = \frac {-k}h{/tex}
(h, k) is a tangent,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} kt = h + at2
{tex}\Rightarrow {k}\left(-\frac{{k}}{{h}}\right)={h}+{a}\left(\frac{{k}^{2}}{{h}^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -k2h = h3 + ab2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x3 + ay2 + xy2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x2 + y2) + ay2 = 0

questions.question: Two mutually perpendicular tangents of the parabola y^2 = 4ax meet the axis in P_1 and P_2. If S is the focus of the parabola then $\frac{1}{\left(S P_{1}\right)}+\frac{1}{\left(S P_{2}\right)}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Two mutually perpendicular tangents of the parabola y2 = 4ax meet the axis in P1 and P2. If S is the focus of the parabola then {tex}\frac{1}{\left(S P_{1}\right)}+\frac{1}{\left(S P_{2}\right)}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let ({tex}{a t}_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹, 2at1â€‹) and ({tex}{a t}_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹, 2at2â€‹) be any two points on the given parabola
Equation of tangent to the parabola y2 = 4ax at ({tex}{a t}_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹, 2at1â€‹) is
t1â€‹y = x + {tex}{a t}_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex}
Since this tangent meets x-axis in P1â€‹.
Coordinates of P1â€‹ are (−{tex}{a t}_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹,0)
Equation of tangent to the parabola y2 = 4ax at ({tex}{a t}_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹, 2at2â€‹) is
t2â€‹y = x + {tex}{a t}_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex}
Since, this tangent meets x-axis in P2â€‹.
Coordinates of P2â€‹ are (−{tex}{a t}_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹, 0)
SP1 â€‹= a(1 + {tex}t_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹); SP2 â€‹= a(1 + {tex}t_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex}â€‹)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t1â€‹t1 â€‹= −1
{tex}\frac{1}{{S P}_{1}}=\frac{1}{{a}\left({1} \ + \ {t}_{{1}}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{{S P}_{2}}=\frac{1}{{a}\left({1} \ + \ {t}_{{2}}^{2}\right)} = \frac{{t}_{1}^{2}}{{a}\left({t}_{1}^{2} \ + \ {1}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{{S P}_{1}}+\frac{{1}}{{S P}_{2}}=\frac{{1}}{{a}}{/tex}

questions.question: Tangent to the curve y = x^2 + 6 at a point P (1, 7) touches the circle x^2 + y^2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0 at a point Q. Then the coordinates of Q are:
questions.question_raw: Tangent to the curve y = x2 + 6 at a point P (1, 7) touches the circle x2 + y2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0 at a point Q. Then the coordinates of Q are:
questions.explanation:
Curve, y = x2 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = y - 6 ...(i)
Vertex: (0, 6), a = {tex}\frac 14{/tex}
T at P(1, 7) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {y \ + \ 7}2{/tex} - 6
2x = y - 5 ...(ii) (m = 2)
({tex}c^\prime{/tex} = 5)
Circle: x2 + y2 + 16x + 12y + c = 0 ..(iii) (radius = {tex}\sqrt 8^2{/tex} + 62 − c)
If (ii) is tangent to (ii),
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (radius)({tex}\sqrt 1{/tex} â€‹+ m2) = {tex}c^\prime{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (5)2 = [1 + (2)2][100 − c]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 = 100 − c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 95 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 5â€‹
PO contact: {tex}\left(\pm \frac{{a m}}{\sqrt{{1 \ + \ m}^{2}}}, \pm \frac{{a}}{\sqrt{{1 \ + \ m}^{2}}}\right){/tex}
(-6, -7)

questions.question: Length of normal chord of the parabola y^2 = 4x, which makes an angle of $\frac {\pi}4$ with x-axis, is:
questions.question_raw: Length of normal chord of the parabola y2 = 4x, which makes an angle of {tex}\frac {\pi}4{/tex} with x-axis, is:
questions.explanation:
Given the equation of parabola is y2 = 4x
Here, a = 1
Let P({tex}t_1^2{/tex}â€‹, 2t1â€‹) and Q({tex}t_2^2{/tex}â€‹, 2t2â€‹) be the endpoints of the normal chord of the parabola.
Equation of normal at point ({tex}t_1^2{/tex}â€‹, 2t1â€‹) is y = −t1â€‹x + 2t1 â€‹+ t1â€‹3
The slope of the normal chord is 1
Hence, -t = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Coordinates of P are (1, -2)
Hence, parameter at Q is t2 = -t1 - {tex}\frac{2}{\mathrm{t}_{1}}{/tex} = 1 + 2 = 3
Therefore, coordinates at Q are (9, 6)
Length of PQ = {tex}\sqrt{8^{2}+8^{2}}=8 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.explanation:
Let L : x cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}Â + yÂ sinÂ {tex}\alpha{/tex}Â - pÂ = 0
Centre of the circle is C(0, 0) and its radius is a
And distance of chord AB from the centre of the circle is, MC =Â {tex}\left|\frac{(0) \sin \alpha \ + \ (0) \cos \alpha \ - \ p}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} \alpha \ + \ \cos ^{2} \alpha}}\right|{/tex}Â = |p|
Now for the chord AB to subtends right angle at C,Â {tex}\angle{/tex}MCB = {tex}\angle{/tex}MCA = 45o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â MC = AC cosÂ 45oÂ {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â |p| =Â {tex}\frac{{a}}{\sqrt{{2}}} \Rightarrow{/tex}Â a2Â =Â 2p2

questions.question: In acute triangle ABC equation of side BC is x - y = 0. Circumcentre and orthocentre of the triangle are (2, 3) and (5, 8) respectively. Equation of circumcircle of the triangle is:
questions.question_raw: In acute triangle ABC equation of side BC is x - y = 0. Circumcentre and orthocentre of the triangle are (2, 3) and (5, 8) respectively. Equation of circumcircle of the triangle is:
questions.explanation:
Reflection of orthocenterÂ P(5, 8)Â inÂ BC(x âˆ’ y = 0)Â will lie on circumcircle.
ClearlyÂ P1Â {tex}\equiv{/tex}Â (8, 5). Thus, the Â equation of circumcircle with centerÂ O(2, 3)Â and passing throughÂ P(8, 5)Â is
(x âˆ’ 2)2+(y âˆ’ 3)2Â = (8 âˆ’ 2)2Â + (5 âˆ’ 3)2
i.e,Â x2 + y2 - 4x - 6y - 27Â = 0

questions.question: A line L is common tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 1 and the parabola y^2 = 4x. If $\theta$ is the angle which it makes with the positive x-axis, then tan^2 $\theta$ is. equal to:

questions.explanation:
Tangent to the parabola
y2 = 4x is y = mx +Â {tex}\frac 1m{/tex}Â ...(i)
m2x - my + 1 = 0
As, it touches the circle x2 + y2 = 1, so
{tex}\left|\frac{{1}}{\sqrt{{m}^{4}+{m}^{2}}}\right|={1} \Rightarrow{/tex}Â m4 + m2 - 1 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Â m2Â = tan2Â {tex}\theta=\frac{-1 \ \pm \ \sqrt{1 \ + \ 4}}{2}{/tex}
=Â {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5} \ - \ 1}{2}=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{5} \ - \ 1}{4}\right){/tex}Â = 2 sin 18Â°

questions.question: A circle touches the parabola y^2 = 4x at M (1, 2) and also touches its directrix. The y-coordinate of the point of contact of the circle and the directrix is:
questions.question_raw: A circle touches the parabola y2 = 4x at M (1, 2) and also touches its directrix. The y-coordinate of the point of contact of the circle and the directrix is:
questions.explanation:
y2Â = 4x
2yÂ {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}Â = 4
{tex}m_T=\frac{2}{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{2}{2}{/tex}Â = 1
CircleÂ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}Â S +Â {tex}\lambda{/tex}L = 0
(x + 1)2 +Â (y + {tex}\alpha{/tex})2Â +Â {tex}\lambda{/tex}(x + 1) = 0 ...(i)
differentiate,
2(x + 1) + 2(y - {tex}\alpha{/tex})Â {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}+\lambda{/tex}Â = 0
x = 1, y = 2
4 +Â 2(2Â - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) mTÂ +Â {tex}\lambda{/tex}Â = 0
{tex}\lambda{/tex}Â = 2{tex}\alpha{/tex}Â - 8 ...(ii)
(1, 2) satisfies eq. (i)
2 =Â (2 -Â {tex}\alpha{/tex})2 + 2{tex}\lambda{/tex}Â = 0
{tex}\alpha{/tex}2Â - 4{tex}\alpha{/tex}Â + 8 + 2(2{tex}\alpha{/tex}Â - 8) = 0
{tex}\alpha{/tex}2Â = 8
{tex}\alpha{/tex}Â = 2Â {tex}\sqrt 2{/tex}

questions.question: Chord of the curve 4x^2 + y^2 - x + 4y = 0 which subtend a right angle at the origin pass through a fixed point whose co-ordinate are :
questions.question_raw: Chord of the curve 4x2 + y2 - x + 4y = 0 which subtend a right angle at the origin pass through a fixed point whose co-ordinate are :
questions.explanation:
Let chord be ax + by = 1
homogenising cube with the help of line
4x2 + y2 - x (ax + by) + 4y (ax + by) = 0
Subtending right angle
4 - a + 1 + 4b = 0
a - 4b = 5
{tex}\frac{a}{5}-\frac{4}{5}{/tex} b = 1
passing through {tex}\left(\frac{1}{5}, \frac{-4}{5}\right){/tex}

questions.question: The locus of the mid-point of the chord of contact of tangents drawn from points lying on the straight line 4x - 5y = 20 to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 9 is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the mid-point of the chord of contact of tangents drawn from points lying on the straight line 4x - 5y = 20 to the circle x2 + y2 = 9 is
questions.explanation:
Description of Situation As equation of chord of contact is T = 0

Here, equation of chord of contact w .r. t. P is
{tex}x \lambda+y \cdot\left(\frac{4 \lambda-{20}}{{5}}\right)=9{/tex}
5{tex}\lambda{/tex}x+ (4{tex}\lambda{/tex}Â - 20)y = 45 ...(i)

and equation of chord bisected at the point Q (h, k) is
xh+ yk - 9 = h2 + k2 - 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â xh + ky = h2 + k2Â ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{5 \lambda}{h}=\frac{4 \lambda-20}{k}=\frac{45}{h^{2}+k^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Â {tex}\lambda=\frac{20 h}{4 h-5 k} \text { and } \lambda=\frac{9 h}{h^{2}+k^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{20 h}{4 h-5 k}=\frac{9 h}{h^{2}+k^{2}}{/tex}
orÂ 20 (h2 + k2) = 9 (4h - 5k)
orÂ 20 (x2 + y2) = 36x - 45y

inside circle
Circle has equation {tex}x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y-36=0{/tex}
{tex}6^2+2^2-2 * 6-4 * 2-36=36+4-12-8-36=-16<0{/tex} so, point is inside the circle.

questions.question: The equation of the conic with focus at (1, -1), directrix along x - y + 1 = 0 and with eccentricity $\sqrt{2}$ , is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the conic with focus at (1, -1), directrix along x - y + 1 = 0 and with eccentricity {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} , is
questions.explanation:
Let S(1, -1) be the focus and P {tex}\equiv{/tex} (x, y) be any point on conic.
Then, PS = e PM
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{(x-1)^{2}+(y+1)^{2}}=\sqrt{2}\left|\frac{x-y+1}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}-2 x+1+y^{2}+2 y+1}{/tex} = |x - y + 1|
Squaring on both sides, we get
4x - 4y - 2xy - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2xy - 4x + 4y + 1 = 0

questions.question: An ellipse has OB as a semi-minor axis, F, F' as its foci and the angle $\angle$FBF' is a right angle. Then, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: An ellipse has OB as a semi-minor axis, F, F' as its foci and the angle {tex}\angle{/tex}FBF' is a right angle. Then, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\angle \mathrm{FBF}^{\prime}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} FF'2 = FB2 + F'B2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2ae)2 = a2 + a2 ...{tex}\because{/tex} x - coordinate of B is zero, {tex}\therefore{/tex} FB = a - e {tex}\times{/tex} 0 and F'B = a + e {tex}\times{/tex} 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4e2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of hyperbola whose coordinates of the foci are ($\pm$8, 0) and the length of latus rectum is 24 units, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of hyperbola whose coordinates of the foci are ({tex}\pm{/tex}8, 0) and the length of latus rectum is 24 units, is
questions.explanation:
Given, ae = 8 and {tex}\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}{/tex} = 24
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 12a
Now, b2 = a2(e2 - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12a = a2e2 - a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12a = 64 - a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + 12a - 64 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4 ...[{tex}\because{/tex} a > 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 12(4) = 48
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equation of hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{48}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - y2 = 48

questions.question: A man running around a race-course note that the sum of the distances of two flag-posts from him is always 10 metres and the distance between the flag-posts is 8 metres. The area of the path he encloses in square metres is
questions.question_raw: A man running around a race-course note that the sum of the distances of two flag-posts from him is always 10 metres and the distance between the flag-posts is 8 metres. The area of the path he encloses in square metres is
questions.explanation:
We have, 2a = 10m and 2ae = 8m
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Now, b2 = a2(1 - e2) = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 3
Thus, required area = {tex}\pi{/tex}ab =15{tex}\pi{/tex} sq. metre.

questions.question: The equation of hyperbola whose foci are (2, 4) and (-2, 4) and eccentricity is $\frac{4}{3}$, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of hyperbola whose foci are (2, 4) and (-2, 4) and eccentricity is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
Distance between foci is 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ae = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow a^{2}=\frac{9}{4} \quad \ldots\left[\because e=\frac{4}{3}\right]{/tex}
Also, e2 = 1 + {tex}\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{16}{9}-1=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b^{2}=\frac{7}{4}{/tex}
Centre is (0, 4)
Hence, equation of hyperbola is {tex}\frac{4 x^{2}}{9}-\frac{4(y-4)^{2}}{7}=1{/tex}

questions.question: Let a and b be non-zero real numbers. Then, the equation (ax^2 + by^2 + c)(x^2 - 5xy + by^2) = 0 represents
questions.question_raw: Let a and b be non-zero real numbers. Then, the equation (ax2 + by2 + c)(x2 - 5xy + by2) = 0 represents

options.text: two straight lines and a circle, when a = b, and c is of sign opposite to that of a
two straight lines and a hyperbola, when a and b are of the same sign and c is of sign opposite to that of a
four straight lines, when c = 0 and a, b are of the same sign
a circle and an ellipse, when a and b are of the same sign and c is of sign opposite to that of a

questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} x2 - 5xy + by2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 3y)(x - 2y) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 - 5xy + by2 = 0 represents a pair of straight lines given by x - 3y = 0 and x - 2y = 0.
Also ax2 + by2 + c = 0 will represent a circle if a = b and c is of sign opposite to that of a.

questions.question: If the common tangent to the parabolas, y^2 = 4x and x^2 = 4y also touches the circle, x^2 + y^2 = c^2, then c is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the common tangent to the parabolas, y2 = 4x and x2 = 4y also touches the circle, x2 + y2 = c2, then c is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Equation tangent to parabola y2 = 4x with slope m be:
{tex}y=m x+\frac{1}{m}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} Equation of tangent to x2 = 4y with slope m be :
y = mx - am2 ...(ii)
From eq. (i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{1}{m}=-m^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = -1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation tangent :x + y + 1 = 0
It is tangent to circle x2 + y2 = c2
{tex}\Rightarrow c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of the common tangent to the curves y^2 = 8x and xy = -1 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the common tangent to the curves y2 = 8x and xy = -1 is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of curves are
y2 = 8x ...(i)
and xy = -1 ...(ii)
If m is the slope of the tangent to (i), then the equation of tangent is
y = mx + {tex}\frac 2m{/tex}
If this tangent is also a tangent to (ii), then putting the value of y in the curve (ii)
{tex}x\left(m x+\frac{2}{m}\right)=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow m x^{2}+\frac{2}{m} x+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2x2 + 2x + m = 0
We should get repeated roots for the equation (condition of tangency)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D = 0, {tex}\therefore{/tex} (2)2 - 4m2. m = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m3 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 1
Hence equation of required tangent is y = x + 2

questions.question: The point at which the line y = mx + c touches the parabola y^2 = 4ax is
questions.question_raw: The point at which the line y = mx + c touches the parabola y2 = 4ax is
questions.explanation:
The line y = mx + c is tangent to the parabola
y2 = 4ar
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2x2 + (2mc - 4a)x + c2 = 0 ...(i)
has equal roots
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} discriminant = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = mc ...(ii)
Substitute in (i) to get (mx - c)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{c}{m}=\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex} ...[From (ii)]
Substitute in y2 = 4ax to get y = {tex}\frac{2 a}{m}{/tex}
