questions.question: The locus of the point of intersection of the lines $\sqrt{3} x$ - y - $4 \sqrt{3} k$ = 0 and $\sqrt{3} x$ - ky - $4 \sqrt{3} $ = 0 for different value of k is a/an
questions.question_raw: The locus of the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\sqrt{3} x{/tex} - y - {tex}4 \sqrt{3} k{/tex} = 0 and {tex}\sqrt{3} x{/tex} - ky - {tex}4 \sqrt{3} {/tex} = 0 for different value of k is a/an
questions.explanation:
Given equation of lines are
{tex}\sqrt{3} x-y-4 \sqrt{3} k=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x-y=4 \sqrt{3} k{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}\sqrt{3} k x+k y-4 \sqrt{3}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k(\sqrt{3} x+y)=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} x+y=\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{k}{/tex} ...(ii)
Multiplying equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}(\sqrt{3} x-y)(\sqrt{3} x+y)=4 \sqrt{3} k \times \frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - y2 = 48
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{(48 / 3)}-\frac{y^{2}}{48}=1{/tex}, which is a hyperbola.

questions.question: The curve described parametrically by x = t^2 + t + 1, y = t^2 - t + 1 represents
questions.question_raw: The curve described parametrically by x = t2 + t + 1, y = t2 - t + 1 represents
questions.explanation:
The equation ax2 + 2hxy + by2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0
represents a parabola if {tex}\Delta \neq 0{/tex} and h2 = ab
where {tex}\Delta{/tex} = abc + 2fgh - af2 - bg2 - ch2
Now we have x = t2 + t + 1 and y = t2 - t + 1
{tex}\therefore \frac{x+y}{2}{/tex} = t2 + 1 and {tex}\frac{x-y}{2}=t{/tex}
On eliminating t, we get 2(x + y) = (x - y)2 + 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 - 2xy + y2 - 2x - 2y + 4 = 0
Here, a = 1, h = -1, b = 1, g = -1, f = -1, c = 4
{tex}\therefore \Delta \neq 0{/tex} and h2 = ab
Hence the given curve represents a parabola.

questions.question: The equation of the tangent to the parabola y^2 = 16x, which is perpendicular to the line y = 3x + 7 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the tangent to the parabola y2 = 16x, which is perpendicular to the line y = 3x + 7 is
questions.explanation:
Line perpendicular to given line is, 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} + x = {tex}\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\frac{-1}{3} x+\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}
Here, {tex}m=\frac{-1}{3}, c=\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}
If we compare y2 = 16 x with y2 = 4ax then a = 4
Condition for tangency is,
{tex}c=\frac{a}{m} \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{4}{(-1 / 3)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda{/tex} = -36
Required equation is x + 3y + 36 = 0.

questions.question: A hyperbola passes through the points (3, 2) and (-17, 12) and has its centre at origin and transverse axis is along x-axis. The length of its transverse axis is
questions.question_raw: A hyperbola passes through the points (3, 2) and (-17, 12) and has its centre at origin and transverse axis is along x-axis. The length of its transverse axis is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of hyperbola be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
This hyperbola passes through (3, 2).
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9}{a^{2}}-\frac{4}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}  ...(i)
Also, it passes through (-17, 12).
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(-17)^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{(12)^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get a = 1 and {tex}b=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Hence, length of transverse axis = 2a = 2

questions.question: The equation 2x^2 + 3y^2 - 8x - 18y + 35 = k represents
questions.question_raw: The equation 2x2 + 3y2 - 8x - 18y + 35 = k represents
questions.explanation:
2x2 + 3y2 - 8x - 18y + 35 = k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 (x - 2)2 + 3(y - 3)2 = k
For k = 0, we get 2(x - 2)2 + 3(y - 3)2 = 0, which represents the point (2, 3).

questions.question: The locus of a point which divides the line segment joining the point (0, -1) and a point on the parabola, x^2 = 4y, internally in the ratio 1 : 2, is:
questions.question_raw: The locus of a point which divides the line segment joining the point (0, -1) and a point on the parabola, x2 = 4y, internally in the ratio 1 : 2, is:
questions.explanation:
Let point P be (2t, t2) and Q be (h, k)
Using section formula,
{tex}h=\frac{2 t}{3}, k=\frac{-2+t^{2}}{3}{/tex}
Hence, locus is 3k + 2 {tex}=\left(\frac{3 h}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x2 - 12y + 8

questions.question: Consider the ellipse E: $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{v^{2}}{9}=1$, with the major axis AA'. P is a point of E and Q is the corresponding point on the major auxiliary circle. M is the midpoint of PQ. The eccentricity of locus of M:
questions.question_raw: Consider the ellipse E: {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{v^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}, with the major axis AA'. P is a point of E and Q is the corresponding point on the major auxiliary circle. M is the midpoint of PQ. The eccentricity of locus of M:
questions.explanation:
P = (4 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, 3 sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) and Q = (4 cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, 4 sin{tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M = {tex}\left(4 \cos \theta, \frac{7}{2} \sin \theta\right){/tex} = (h, k), say
Then {tex}\left(\frac{h}{4}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{k}{7 / 2}\right)^{2}=1{/tex} (an ellipse)
Let e be the eccentricity.
Then {tex}\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (4)2 (1 - e2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2 {tex}=1-\frac{49}{4 \times 16}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow e^{2}=\frac{15}{64} \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{8}{/tex}

questions.question: Tangents are drawn from the point (2, 5) to y^2 = 4x. The slopes of the tangents are the roots of the quadratic equation ax^2 - bx + c = 0, where a, b, c are positive integers with no common factor except 1. Then which of the following is not correct?
questions.question_raw: Tangents are drawn from the point (2, 5) to y2 = 4x. The slopes of the tangents are the roots of the quadratic equation ax2 - bx + c = 0, where a, b, c are positive integers with no common factor except 1. Then which of the following is not correct?
questions.explanation:
Equation of a tangent in slope form is
y = {tex}m x+\frac{1}{m}{/tex}
The tangents pass through (2, 5).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 = 2m + {tex}\frac{1}{m}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2m2 - 5m + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2, b = 5, c = 1, a + b + c = 8

questions.question: If y = mx + 4 is a tangent to both the parabolas, y^2 = 4x and x^2 = 2by, then b is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If y = mx + 4 is a tangent to both the parabolas, y2 = 4x and x2 = 2by, then b is equal to:
questions.explanation:
y = mx + 4 ...(i)
Tangent of y2 = 4x is
{tex}\Rightarrow y=m x+\frac{1}{m}{/tex} ...(ii) [{tex}\because{/tex} Equation of tangent of y2 = 4 ax is {tex}y=m x+\frac{a}{m}{/tex}]
From (i) and (ii)
{tex}4=\frac{1}{m} \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
So, the line {tex}y=\frac{1}{4} x+4{/tex} is also tangent to the parabola
x2 = 2by, so solve both equations.
{tex}x^{2}=2 b\left(\frac{x+16}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - bx - 16b = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} D = 0 [For tangent]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (-16b) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 + 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 4b = 0
b = -128, b = 0 (not possible)

questions.question: The value of m for which y = mx + 6 is a tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{100}-\frac{v^{2}}{49}=1$, is
questions.question_raw: The value of m for which y = mx + 6 is a tangent to the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{100}-\frac{v^{2}}{49}=1{/tex}, is
questions.explanation:
If y = mx + c touches {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{v^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, then c2 = a2m2 - b2.
Here, c = 6, a2 = 100, b2 = 49
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 36 = 100m2 - 49
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 100m2 = 85
{tex}\Rightarrow m=\sqrt{\frac{17}{20}}{/tex}

questions.question: If P, P_1 and Q, Q_1 are corresponding points of an ellipse and its auxiliary circle, then $\frac{\text { Area of }(\Delta \mathrm{OPQ})}{\text { Area of }\left(\Delta \mathrm{OP}_{1} \mathrm{Q}_{1}\right)}$ =
questions.question_raw: If P, P1 and Q, Q1 are corresponding points of an ellipse and its auxiliary circle, then {tex}\frac{\text { Area of }(\Delta \mathrm{OPQ})}{\text { Area of }\left(\Delta \mathrm{OP}_{1} \mathrm{Q}_{1}\right)}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of ellipse be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}.
P {tex}\equiv{/tex} (a cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, b sin{tex}\theta{/tex}), P1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (a cos{tex}\theta{/tex}, a sin{tex}\theta{/tex})
Q {tex}\equiv{/tex} (a cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}, b sin{tex}\alpha{/tex}), Q1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} (a cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}, a sin{tex}\alpha{/tex})
Now, area of ({tex}\triangle{/tex}OPQ)
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a \cos \theta & b \sin \theta & 1 \\ a \cos \alpha & b \sin \alpha & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}ab(sin{tex}\alpha{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} - cos{tex}\alpha{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}ab sin {tex}(\alpha-\theta){/tex}
and area of ({tex}\triangle{/tex}OP1Q1) {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ a \cos \theta & a \sin \theta & 1 \\ a \cos \alpha & a \sin \alpha & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}a2sin{tex}(\alpha-\theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\text { area of }(\Delta \mathrm{OPQ})}{\text { area of }\left(\Delta \mathrm{OP}_{1} \mathrm{Q}_{1}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a b}{a^{2}}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}

questions.question: If one end of a focal chord of the parabola, y^2 = 16x is at (1, 4), then the length of this focal chord is:
questions.question_raw: If one end of a focal chord of the parabola, y2 = 16x is at (1, 4), then the length of this focal chord is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} y2 = 16x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4
One end of the focal chord of the parabola is at (1, 4)
y - coordinate of a focal chord is 2 at
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2 at = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Hence, the required length of the focal chord
{tex}=a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{2}=4 \times\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} = 25

questions.question: The equation of a tangent to the parabola y^2 = 8x is y = x + 2. The point on this line from which the other tangent to the parabola is perpendicular to the given tangent is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a tangent to the parabola y2 = 8x is y = x + 2. The point on this line from which the other tangent to the parabola is perpendicular to the given tangent is
questions.explanation:
The locus of the point of intersection of perpendicular tangents to a parabola is its directrix.
So, required point is the point of intersection of y = x + 2 and directrix x = -2. Hence, its coordinates are (-2, 0).

questions.question: The tangent to the parabola y^2 = 4x at the point where it intersects the circle x^2 + y^2 = 5 in the first quadrant, passes through the point:
questions.question_raw: The tangent to the parabola y2 = 4x at the point where it intersects the circle x2 + y2 = 5 in the first quadrant, passes through the point:
questions.explanation:
To find intersection point of x2 + y2 = 5 and y2 = 4x,
substitute y2 = 4x in x2 + y2 = 5, we get,
x2 + 4x - 5 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 5x - x - 5 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x + 5) - 1 (x + 5) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1, -5
Intersection point in 1st quadrant be (1, 2)
Now, equation of tangent to y2 = 4x at (1, 2) is y {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 2(x + 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - y + 1 = 0 ...(i)
Hence, {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \frac{7}{4}\right){/tex} lies on (i)

questions.question: The equation $\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}=1$, r > 1 represents
questions.question_raw: The equation {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{y^{2}}{1+r}=1{/tex}, r > 1 represents
questions.explanation:
Given equation {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{1-r}-\frac{v^{2}}{1+r}=1{/tex}, r > 1
As r > 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 - r < 0 and 1 + r > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Let 1 - r = -a2, 1 + r = b2
{tex}\therefore \frac{x^{2}}{-a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = -1
which is not possible for any real values of x and y.

questions.question: The tangent drawn at any point P to the parabola y^2 = 4ax meets the directrix at the point K, then the angle which KP subtends at its focus is

Here, P(at2, 2at) and S(a, 0).
If the tangent at P, ty = x + at2, meets the directrix
x = -a at K, then {tex}\mathrm{K}=\left(-a, \frac{a t^{2}-a}{t}\right){/tex}
Now, m1 = slope of {tex}\mathrm{SP}=\frac{2 a t}{a\left(t^{2}-1\right)}{/tex}
and m2 = slope of {tex}\mathrm{SK}=\frac{a\left(t^{2}-1\right)}{-2 a t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m1m2 = -1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PSK = 90

questions.question: The equation of the tangents drawn at the ends of the major axis of the ellipse 9x^2 + 5y^2 - 30y = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The equation of the tangents drawn at the ends of the major axis of the ellipse 9x2 + 5y2 - 30y = 0 are
questions.explanation:
9x2 + 5y2 - 30y = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x2 + 5(y2 - 6y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9x2 + 5(y2 - 6y + 9) = 45
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{5}+\frac{(y-3)^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} a2 < b2, so the axis of the ellipse is on the y-axis.
Put x = 0, to obtain vertices.
5y2 - 30y = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y = 0, y = 6
Therefore, equation of tangents are y = 0, y = 6.

questions.question: The tangents from a point P to y^2 = 4ax have the inclinations $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$, and cot $\theta_{1}$ + cot $\theta_{2}$ = 2, then point P lies on
questions.question_raw: The tangents from a point P to y2 = 4ax have the inclinations {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex}, and cot {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} + cot {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex} = 2, then point P lies on
questions.explanation:
Let the tangents from point P meet the curve at Q and R.
Let Q = (at12, 2at1) and R = (at22, 2at2).
Then P = (at1t2, a(t1 + t2))
The slopes of the tangents at Q and R are {tex}\frac{1}{t_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{t_{2}}{/tex} respectively.
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \theta_{1}=\frac{1}{t_{1}}, \tan \theta_{2}=\frac{1}{t_{2}}{/tex}
Given cot cot {tex}\theta_1{/tex} + cot{tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t1 + t2 = 2
Ordinate of point P is a(t1 + t2) = 2a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point P lies on the line y = 2a.

questions.question: Equation of a common tangent to the parabola y^2 = 4x and the hyperbola xy = 2 is:
questions.question_raw: Equation of a common tangent to the parabola y2 = 4x and the hyperbola xy = 2 is:
questions.explanation:
Equation of a tangent to parabola y2 = 4x is:
{tex}y=m x+\frac{1}{m}{/tex}
This line is a tangent to xy = 2
{tex}\therefore x\left(m x+\frac{1}{m}\right)=2 \Rightarrow m x^{2}+\frac{1}{m} {/tex}x - 2 = 0
{tex}\because{/tex} Tangent is common for parabola and hyperbola.
{tex}\therefore D=\left(\frac{1}{m}\right)^{2}{/tex} - 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}m{tex}\cdot{/tex}(-2) = 0
{tex}\frac{1}{m^{2}}+8 m{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + 8 m3 = 0
m3 = {tex}-\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow m=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of common tangent: y = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = -x - 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 2y + 4 = 0

questions.question: Tangents are drawn from a point P to a parabola y^2 = 4ax. The area enclosed by the tangents and the corresponding chord of contact is 4a^2. Then point P satisfies
questions.question_raw: Tangents are drawn from a point P to a parabola y2 = 4ax. The area enclosed by the tangents and the corresponding chord of contact is 4a2. Then point P satisfies
questions.explanation:
Let P = (x1, y1) , A = (a12, 2at1), B = (at22, 2at2)
Then (x1, y1) = (at1t2, a(t1 + t2))
Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}PAB {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a t_{1}^{2} & 2 a t_{1} & 1 \\ a t_{1} t_{2} & a\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right) & 1 \\ a t_{2}^{2} & 2 a t_{2} & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow 8 a^{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a t_{1}\left(t_{1}-t_{2}\right) & a\left(t_{1}-t_{2}\right) & 0 \\ a t_{1} t_{2} & a\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right) & 1 \\ a t_{2}\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right) & a\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right) & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (t1 - t2)3 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t1 - t2 = 2
{tex}y_{1}^{2}{/tex} = a2(t1 + t2)2
= a2[(t1 - t2)2 + 4t1t2]
{tex}=a^{2}\left(4+\frac{4 x_{1}}{a}\right){/tex}
{tex}y_{\mathrm{I}}^{2}{/tex} = 4a2 + 4x1a
= 4a(x1 + a)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point P satisfies y2 4a(x +a)

questions.explanation:
We have y2 = 8x = 4ax, say, then
4a = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2
Any tangent of parabola is,
y = {tex}m x+\frac{a}{m}{/tex} or mx - y + {tex}\frac{2}{m}{/tex} = 0
If this line is a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = 2, then
{tex}\left|\frac{\frac{2}{m}}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}\right|=\sqrt{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{4}{m^{2}}{/tex} = 2(m2 + 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m4 + m2 - 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (m2 + 2)(m2 - 1) = 0 or m = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1
Hence, the common tangents are y = {tex}\pm{/tex} (x + 2)

questions.question: The maximum number of normals that can be drawn from a point to a parabola is
questions.question_raw: The maximum number of normals that can be drawn from a point to a parabola is
questions.explanation:
The equation of a normal to the parabola
y2 = 8x is
y = mx - 4m - 2m3 (as a = 2)

{tex}\because{/tex} This normal passes through (0, -6).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -6 = -4m - 2m3
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m3 + 2m - 3 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (m - 1) (m2 + m + 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point P(2m2, -4m) = (2, -4)
Radius of required circle = CP = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The polar of P(x_1, y_1) with respect to the parabola y^2 = 4ax is normal to the curve, then point P describes a curve given by
questions.question_raw: The polar of P(x1, y1) with respect to the parabola y2 = 4ax is normal to the curve, then point P describes a curve given by
questions.explanation:
The polar of point P(x1, y1) with respect to the parabola y2 = 4ax is
yy1 = 2a(x + x1) {tex}\Leftrightarrow y=\frac{2 a}{y_{1}} x+\frac{2 a x_{1}}{y_{1}}{/tex} ...(i)
The equation of normal in slope form is
y = mx - 2am - am3 ...(ii)
...(m is slope of the normal)
Comparing (i) and (ii), and eliminating m, we get
{tex}\frac{2 a x_{1}}{y_{1}}=-2 a\left(\frac{2 a}{y_{1}}\right)-a\left(\frac{2 a}{y_{1}}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x_{1}=\frac{-2 a y_{1}^{2}-4 a^{3}}{y_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} Point P describes xy2 + 2ay2 + 4a3 = 0

questions.question: The centroid of the triangle formed by joining the feet of the normals drawn from any point to the parabola y^2 = 4ax, lies on the
questions.question_raw: The centroid of the triangle formed by joining the feet of the normals drawn from any point to the parabola y2 = 4ax, lies on the
questions.explanation:
Normal to parabola is given by
y = mx - 2am - am3 which is a cubic equation in m.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Three normals can be drawn on parabola
y2 = 4ax. Since coefficient of m2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m1 + m2 + m3 = 0, If (x1, y1), (x2, y2), (x3, y3)
are feet of the normals then {tex}m_{1}=-\frac{y_{1}}{2 a}{/tex}, {tex}m_{2}=-\frac{y_{2}}{2 a}, m_{3}=-\frac{y_{3}}{2 a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 + y2 + y3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y coordinate of the centroid of the triangle, with feet of the normals as vertices, is zero.
So centroid lies on the axis of the parabola.

P = ({tex}a t_{1}^{2}{/tex}, 2a t1) and Q = ({tex}a t_{2}^{2}{/tex}, 2at2)
Then {tex}h=\frac{a\left(t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}\right)}{2}{/tex} and k = a (t1 + t2). Also OP {tex}\perp{/tex} OQ.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{t_{1}} \cdot \frac{2}{t_{2}}=-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t1t2 = -4
{tex}k^{2}=a^{2}\left(t_{1}^{2}+t_{2}^{2}+2 t_{1} t_{2}\right)=a^{2}\left(\frac{2 h}{a}-8\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The locus of M is given by y2 = 2ax - 8a2

questions.question: If foci of an ellipse are (0, Â±3) and length of semimajor axis is 5 units, then find the equation of ellipse.
questions.question_raw: If foci of an ellipse are (0, ±3) and length of semimajor axis is 5 units, then find the equation of ellipse.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{5}\right)^2=1{/tex}
Given, {tex}a=5{/tex} and {tex}c=3=>b^2=a^2-c^2=5^2-3^2=4^2=>b=4{/tex}.
Equation of ellipse with major axis along {tex}y{/tex}-axis is {tex}\left(\frac{x}{b}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{a}\right)^2=1{/tex}.
So, equation of given ellipse is {tex}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{5}\right)^2=1{/tex}.

questions.question: The equation of the normal at the point (a sec$\theta$, b tan$\theta$) of the curve b^2x^2 - a^2y^2 = a^2b^2 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the normal at the point (a sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, b tan{tex}\theta{/tex}) of the curve b2x2 - a2y2 = a2b2 is
questions.explanation:
The equation of the normal to the hyperbola
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at the point (x1, y1) is
{tex}\frac{a^{2} x}{x_{1}}+\frac{b^{2} y}{y_{1}}{/tex} = a2 + b2.
Equation of normal to the hyperbola
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at (a sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, b tan{tex}\theta{/tex}) is
{tex}\frac{a^{2} x}{a \sec \theta}+\frac{b^{2} y}{b \tan \theta}{/tex} = a2 + b2
i.e., {tex}\frac{a x}{\sec \theta}+\frac{b y}{\tan \theta}{/tex} = a2 + b2

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 = 12, 0
Hence, the points are (0, 0) and (4, 12).

questions.question: If length of major axis is 10 and minor axis is 8 and major axis is along $x$-axis then find the equation of ellipse.
questions.question_raw: If length of major axis is 10 and minor axis is 8 and major axis is along {tex}x{/tex}-axis then find the equation of ellipse.
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{4}\right)^2=1{/tex}
Given, {tex}2 a=10=>a=5{/tex} and {tex}2 b=8=>b=4{/tex}.
Equation of ellipse with major axis along x -axis is {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)^2=1{/tex}.
So, equation of given ellipse is {tex}\left(\frac{x}{5}\right)^2+\left(\frac{y}{4}\right)^2=1{/tex}.

questions.question: The value of m, for which the line y = mx + $\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3}$ is a normal to the conic $\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}$ = 1, is
questions.question_raw: The value of m, for which the line y = mx + {tex}\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3}{/tex} is a normal to the conic {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1, is
questions.explanation:
We have, y = {tex}m x+\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}
Here, {tex}c=\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3}{/tex}, a2 = 16 and b2 = 9
{tex}c^{2}=\frac{m^{2}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}{a^{2}-b^{2} m^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{m^{2}(16+9)^{2}}{16-9 m^{2}}{/tex}
After solving, we get m = {tex}\pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}

questions.question: If the parabola y^2 = 4ax passes through (-3, 2), then the length of its lams rectum is
questions.question_raw: If the parabola y2 = 4ax passes through (-3, 2), then the length of its lams rectum is

Parabola y2 = 4ax has vertex (0, 0) and given that the curve passes through (-3, 2) (which is a point of 2nd quadrant)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Parabola opens on left of y-axis {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a < 0.
Take a = -a' where a' > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equation of parabola is y2 = -4a'x
The point (-3, 2) will satisfy the equation y2 = -4a'x
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 a^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The length of its latus rectum is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}.

Equation of director circle is x2 + y2 = 13.

i.e., {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{2}-\frac{2}{3}=k \Rightarrow k=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow{/tex} Normal is given by

is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x} \sec \theta}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{y} \tan \theta}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 1 at any parametric point {tex}\theta{/tex}
P is ({tex}\frac{2}{\sec \theta}{/tex}, 0)
Q is (0, {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{\tan \theta}{/tex})
R will be x coordinate of r is taken and y-coordinate Q is taken
R{tex}\left(\frac{2}{\sec \theta}, \frac{-\sqrt{2}}{\tan \theta}\right){/tex} h = {tex}\frac{2}{\sec \theta}{/tex} K = {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{\tan \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{4}{\mathrm{~h}^{2}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{k}^{2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4}{x^{2}}-\frac{2}{y^{2}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The distance between the directrices of a rectangular hyperbola is 10 units, then distance between its foci is

Given that, {tex}\frac{2 a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 10

The distance between foci = 2ae
{tex}=(10 \sqrt{2})(\sqrt{2}){/tex}
= 20

questions.question: The circle passing through the intersection of the circles, x^2 + y^2 - 6x = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 4y = 0, having its centre on the line, 2x - 3y +12 = 0, also passes through the point:
questions.question_raw: The circle passing through the intersection of the circles, x2 + y2 - 6x = 0 and x2 + y2 - 4y = 0, having its centre on the line, 2x - 3y +12 = 0, also passes through the point:

We know family of circle be S1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}S2 = 0
x2 + y2 - 6x + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(x2 + y2 - 4y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex})x2 + (1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex})y2 - 6x - 4{tex}\lambda{/tex}y = 0 ...(i)
Centre (-g, -f) {tex}=\left(\frac{3}{1+\lambda}, \frac{2 \lambda}{\lambda+1}\right){/tex}
Centre lies on 2x - 3y + 12 = 0, then
{tex}\frac{6}{\lambda+1}-\frac{6 \lambda}{\lambda+1}+12=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-3{/tex}
Equation of circle (i),
-2x2 - 2y2 - 6x + 12y = 0

Only (-3, 6) satisfy equation (ii)

questions.question: Locus of the point of intersection of the tangents at the ends of the normal chords of the parabola y^2 = 4ax is:
questions.question_raw: Locus of the point of intersection of the tangents at the ends of the normal chords of the parabola y2 = 4ax is:
questions.explanation:
Let (h, k) be the point of intersection of tangents.
Then equation of chord of contact is ky − 2a(h + x) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ax − ky + 2ah = 0 ...(i)
Equation of normal in parametric form:
y + xt − 2at − at3 = 0 ...(ii)
Since, (i) and (ii) are same
By comparing them {tex}\frac{2 {a}}{{t}}=\frac{-{k}}{{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 {a h}}{-2 {a t} \ - \ {a t}^{3}}{/tex}
By eliminating t, we get (2a + h)k2 + 4a3 = 0

questions.question: Which of the following points lies on the locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn upon any tangent to the ellipse, $\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1$ from any of its foci?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following points lies on the locus of the foot of perpendicular drawn upon any tangent to the ellipse, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1{/tex} from any of its foci?
questions.explanation:
We know that the locus of the feet of the perpendicular drawn from foci to any tangent of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} is

questions.question: The length of the chord of the hyperbola x^2 - y^2 = 4a^2, if (3a, a) is the midpoint of the chord, is
questions.question_raw: The length of the chord of the hyperbola x2 - y2 = 4a2, if (3a, a) is the midpoint of the chord, is
questions.explanation:
Equation of the chord having (3a, a) as the midpoint is
{tex}\frac{3 a x-a y}{4 a^{2}}=\frac{9 a^{2}-a^{2}}{4 a^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3x - y = 8a ....(i)
Given hyperbola is
x2 - y2 = 4a2 ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
x2 - (3x - 8a)2 = 4a2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 8x2 - 48 ax + 68 a2 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 12 ax + 17 a2 = 0
Let A = (x1, y1) and B = (x2, y2) be the ends of the chord. Then,
x1 + x2 = 6a and x1x2 = {tex}\frac{17 a^{2}}{2}{/tex} ...(iii)
Also A and B satisfy (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x1 - y1 = 8a and 3x2 - y2 = 8a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 - y2 = 3(x1 - x2)
|AB| {tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}+9\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10} \sqrt{\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right)^{2}-4 x_{1} x_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{10} \sqrt{36 a^{2}-34 a^{2}}{/tex} ...[From (iii)]
{tex}=\sqrt{20} a=2 a \sqrt{5}{/tex}
