questions.question: If a line, y = mx + c is a tangent to the circle, (x - 3)^2 + y^2 = 1 and it is perpendicular to a line L_1, where L_1 is the tangent to the circle, x^2 + y^2 = 1 at the point $\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$; then:
questions.question_raw: If a line, y = mx + c is a tangent to the circle, (x - 3)2 + y2 = 1 and it is perpendicular to a line L1, where L1 is the tangent to the circle, x2 + y2 = 1 at the point {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}; then:
questions.explanation:
Slope of tangent of x2 + y2 = 1 at {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} y-1=0{/tex}
x + {tex}y \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 0, which is perpendicular to x - y + c = 0
At {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} which is tangent of (x - 3)2 + y2 = 1
So, m = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x + c
Now, distance of (3, 0) from y = x + c is
{tex}\left|\frac{c+3}{\sqrt{2}}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow c=-3 \pm \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} (c + 3)2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} c2 + 6c + 9 = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} c2 + 6c + 7 = 0

questions.question: If the vertex of the parabola y = x^2 - 8x + c lies on x-axis, then the value of c is
questions.question_raw: If the vertex of the parabola y = x2 - 8x + c lies on x-axis, then the value of c is
questions.explanation:
The given equation can be written as (x - 4)2 = y - (c - 16). Therefore the vertex of the parabola is (4, c - 16). Since, the point lies on x-axis.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c - 16 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 16

questions.question: P is any point on the ellipse 9x^2 + 36y^2 = 324, whose foci are S and S'. Then SP + S'P equals
questions.question_raw: P is any point on the ellipse 9x2 + 36y2 = 324, whose foci are S and S'. Then SP + S'P equals
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{36}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}
Here, a = 6
SP + S'P = 2a = 2(6) = 12

questions.question: If the normal at an end of a latus rectum of an ellipse passes through an extremity of the minor axis, then the eccentricity e of the ellipse satisfies:
questions.question_raw: If the normal at an end of a latus rectum of an ellipse passes through an extremity of the minor axis, then the eccentricity e of the ellipse satisfies:
questions.explanation:
Normal to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at {tex}\left(a e, \frac{b^{2}}{a}\right){/tex} is
{tex}\frac{a^{2} x}{a e}-\frac{b^{2} y}{b^{2} / a}=a^{2}-b^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x-e y=\frac{e\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)}{a}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} (0, -b) lies on equation (i), then
{tex}b e=\frac{e\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ab = a2e2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = ae2 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} = e4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 - e2 = e4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e4 + e2 - 1 = 0

questions.question: The equation of latus rectum of a parabola is x + y = 8 and the equation of the tangent at the vertex is x + y = 12, then length of the latus rectum is
questions.question_raw: The equation of latus rectum of a parabola is x + y = 8 and the equation of the tangent at the vertex is x + y = 12, then length of the latus rectum is
questions.explanation:
Since, a = distance between tangent at vertex and latus rectum
{tex}\Leftrightarrow a=\left|\frac{-8-(-12)}{\sqrt{1+1}}\right|=\frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Length of latus rectum = 4a {tex}=4 \times \frac{4}{\sqrt{2}} = 8 \sqrt {2}{/tex}

questions.question: If the coordinates of two points A and B are $(\sqrt{7}, 0)$ and $(-\sqrt{7}, 0)$ respectively and P is any point on the conic, 9x^2 + 16y^2 = 144, then PA + PB is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the coordinates of two points A and B are {tex}(\sqrt{7}, 0){/tex} and {tex}(-\sqrt{7}, 0){/tex} respectively and P is any point on the conic, 9x2 + 16y2 = 144, then PA + PB is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Ellipse: {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex},
a = 4, b = 3, {tex}c=\sqrt{16-9}=\sqrt{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore(\pm \sqrt{7}, 0){/tex} are the foci of given ellipse. So for any point P on it; PA + PB = 2a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PA + PB = 2(4) = 8

questions.question: Let the tangents drawn from the origin to the circle, x^2 + y^2 - 8x - 4y + 16 = 0 touch it at points A and B. The (AB)^2 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let the tangents drawn from the origin to the circle, x2 + y2 - 8x - 4y + 16 = 0 touch it at points A and B. The (AB)2 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}L=\sqrt{S_{1}}=\sqrt{16}=4{/tex}
{tex}R=\sqrt{16+4-16}=2 {/tex}
Length of chord of contact
{tex}=\frac{2 L R}{\sqrt{L^{2}+R^{2}}}=\frac{2 \times 4 \times 2}{\sqrt{16+4}}=\frac{16}{\sqrt{20}}{/tex}
Square of length of chord of contact {tex}=\frac{64}{5}{/tex}

questions.question: If the point P on the curve, 4x^2 + 5y^2 = 20 is farthest from the point Q(0, -4), then PQ^2 is equals to:
questions.question_raw: If the point P on the curve, 4x2 + 5y2 = 20 is farthest from the point Q(0, -4), then PQ2 is equals to:
questions.explanation:
Ellipse {tex}\equiv \frac{x^{2}}{5}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex}
Let a point on ellipse be {tex}(\sqrt{5} \cos \theta, 2 \sin \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ2 = ({tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex})2 + (-4 - 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex})2
= 5cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + 4sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} + 16 + 16sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
= 21 + 16 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex}
= 21 + 64 - (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - 8)2 = 85 - (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - 8)2
PQ2 to be maximum when sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore P Q_{\max }^{2}{/tex} = 85 - 49 = 36

questions.question: The axis of the parabola 9y^2 - 16x - 12y - 57 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The axis of the parabola 9y2 - 16x - 12y - 57 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
9y2 - 16x - 12y - 57 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(y-\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2}=\frac{16}{9}\left(x+\frac{61}{16}\right){/tex}
Put y - {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} = Y and x + {tex}\frac{61}{16}{/tex} = X
{tex}\Rightarrow Y^{2}=4\left(\frac{4}{9}\right) X{/tex}
Axis of this parabola is Y = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3y = 2

questions.question: If the length of the major axis of an ellipse is three times the length of its minor axis, then its eccentricity is
questions.question_raw: If the length of the major axis of an ellipse is three times the length of its minor axis, then its eccentricity is
questions.explanation:
Given, Major axis = 3(Minor axis) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2a = 3(2b)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a = 3b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 9b2 = 9a2(1 - e2)
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The slope of a common tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ and a concentric circle of radius r is
questions.question_raw: The slope of a common tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} and a concentric circle of radius r is
questions.explanation:
The equation of any tangent to {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} is y = mx + {tex}\sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}, where m is the slope.If it touches x2 + y2 = r2, then
a2m2 + b2 = r2(1 + m2) ...[{tex}\because{/tex} c2 = a2(1 + m2)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2m2 - r2m2 = r2 - b2
{tex}\Rightarrow m^{2}=\frac{r^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}-r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow m=\sqrt{\frac{r^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}-r^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: The focus of the parabola y = 2x^2 + x is
questions.question_raw: The focus of the parabola y = 2x2 + x is

options.text:
The given equation of parabola is
y = 2x^2 + x $\Rightarrow x^{2}+\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{2}$
$\Rightarrow\left(x+\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}=\frac{y}{2}+\frac{1}{16}$
$\Rightarrow\left(x+\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(y+\frac{1}{8}\right)$
Let X^2 = ^$\frac{1}{2}Y$ = $4\left(\frac{1}{8}\right) Y$ ...(i)
Here $a=\frac{1}{8}$, focus of (i) is $\left(0, \frac{1}{8}\right)$
i.e., X = 0, $Y=\frac{1}{8}$
$\Rightarrow x+\frac{1}{4}=0, y+\frac{1}{8}=\frac{1}{8}$
$\Rightarrow x=-\frac{1}{4}$, y = 0
$\Rightarrow$ The focus of given parabola is $\left(-\frac{1}{4}, 0\right)$.

questions.explanation: {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{4}, 0\right){/tex}

questions.question: If the line x cos$\alpha$ + y sin$\alpha$ = p is normal to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, then
questions.question_raw: If the line x cos{tex}\alpha{/tex} + y sin{tex}\alpha{/tex} = p is normal to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, then

options.text: p^2(a^2 cos^2 $\alpha$ + b^2 sin^2 $\alpha$) = a^2 - b^2
p^2(a^2 sec^2 $\alpha$ + b^2 cosec^2 $\alpha$) = a^2 - b^2

questions.explanation:
We have y = -x cot {tex}\alpha{/tex} + p cosec{tex}\alpha{/tex} ...(i)
Now, line (i) is normal to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}=1{/tex}, if
{tex}c^{2}=\frac{\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)^{2} m^{2}}{a^{2}+b^{2} m^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore p^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \alpha=\frac{\left(a^{2}-b^{2}\right)^{2} \cot ^{2} \alpha}{a^{2}+b^{2} \cot ^{2} \alpha}{/tex}
After solving, we get
p2(a2 sec2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + b2 cosec2 {tex}\alpha{/tex}) = (a2 - b2)2

questions.question: If the eccentricities of the two ellipses $\frac{x^{2}}{169}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ and $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ are equal, then the value of $\frac{a}{b}$ is
questions.question_raw: If the eccentricities of the two ellipses {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{169}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} are equal, then the value of {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}\sqrt{1-\frac{25}{169}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{144}{169}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}} \Leftrightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{25}{169}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{13}{5}{/tex}

questions.question: Let $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$(a > b) be a given ellipse, length of whose latus rectum is 10. If its eccentricity is the maximum value of the function, $\phi(t)=\frac{5}{12}$ + t - t^2, then a^2 + b^2 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}(a > b) be a given ellipse, length of whose latus rectum is 10. If its eccentricity is the maximum value of the function, {tex}\phi(t)=\frac{5}{12}{/tex} + t - t2, then a2 + b2 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
The given ellipse is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, (a > b)
Length of latus rectum {tex}=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 5a ...(i)
Now {tex}\phi(t)=\frac{5}{12}+t-t^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\phi^{\prime}(t){/tex} = 1 - 2t = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\phi^{\prime \prime}(t){/tex} = -2 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} maximum
{tex}\Rightarrow \phi(t)_{\max }=\frac{5}{12}+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{8}{12}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Since, {tex}\phi(t)_{\max}{/tex}.= eccentricity {tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Now, b2 = a2(1 - e2)
5a = {tex}a^{2}\left(1-\frac{4}{9}\right) \Rightarrow 5 a=\frac{5 a^{2}}{9}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 - 9a = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 81 and b2 = 45
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a2 + b2 = 81 + 45 = 126

questions.question: All the points in the set $\mathrm{S}=\left\{\frac{\alpha+\mathrm{i}}{\alpha-1}: \alpha \in \mathrm{R}\right\}(\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1})$ lie on a:
questions.question_raw: All the points in the set {tex}\mathrm{S}=\left\{\frac{\alpha+\mathrm{i}}{\alpha-1}: \alpha \in \mathrm{R}\right\}(\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1}){/tex} lie on a:
questions.explanation:
Let z {tex}\in{/tex} S then {tex}z=\frac{\alpha+i}{\alpha-i}{/tex}
Since, z is a complex number and let z = x + iy
Then, {tex}x+i y=\frac{(\alpha+i)^{2}}{\alpha^{2}+1}{/tex} (by rationalisation)
{tex}\Rightarrow x+i y=\frac{\left(\alpha^{2}-1\right)}{\alpha^{2}+1}+\frac{i(2 \alpha)}{\alpha^{2}+1}{/tex}
Then compare both sides
{tex}x=\frac{\alpha^{2}-1}{\alpha^{2}+1}{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}y=\frac{2 \alpha}{\alpha^{2}+1}{/tex} ...(ii)
Now squaring and adding equations (i) and (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 {tex}=\frac{\left(\alpha^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{\left(\alpha^{2}+1\right)^{2}}+\frac{4 \alpha^{2}}{\left(\alpha^{2}+1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The normal at one of the ends of a latus rectum of the ellipse b^2x^2 + a^2y^2 = a^2b^2 passes through the end of the minor axis. Then e satisfies
questions.question_raw: The normal at one of the ends of a latus rectum of the ellipse b2x2 + a2y2 = a2b2 passes through the end of the minor axis. Then e satisfies
questions.explanation:
Let P be the end of a latus rectum.
Equation of the tangent at P is {tex}\frac{x(a e)}{a^{2}}+\frac{y\left(b^{2} / a\right)}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{x e}{a}+\frac{y}{a}=1{/tex} {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y = -xe + a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The slope of the tangent at P = - e
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The slope of the normal at P = {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex}
Equation of normal (PB') at P is
{tex}y-\frac{b^{2}}{a}=\frac{1}{e}(x-a e){/tex}
The normal PB' passes through (0, -b).
{tex}\Rightarrow-\left(b+\frac{b^{2}}{a}\right)=\frac{-1}{e}(a e){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ab + b2 = a2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ab + a2 - a2e2 = a2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ae2 = b {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b}{a}=e^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} b2 = a2(1 - e2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (e2)2 = 1 - e2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e4 + e2 - 1 = 0

questions.question: Consider the feet of the normals from (3, 5) to y^2 = 8x. The arithmetic mean of the ordinates of these points is
questions.question_raw: Consider the feet of the normals from (3, 5) to y2 = 8x. The arithmetic mean of the ordinates of these points is
questions.explanation:
Three feet of the normals say A(t1), B(t2), C(t3) are conormal points.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t1 + t2 + t3 = 0.
A.M. of the ordinates {tex}=\frac{2 a t_{1}+2 a t_{2}+2 a t_{3}}{3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 a(0)}{3}{/tex} = 0.

questions.question: The distance between the vertex and the focus of the parabola x^2 - 2x + 3y - 2 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The distance between the vertex and the focus of the parabola x2 - 2x + 3y - 2 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of parabola
x2 - 2x + 3y - 2 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 1)2 = -3(y - 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} vertex = (1, 1), focus {tex}=\left(1, \frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Both the points lie on x = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The distance between focus and vertex
{tex}=\left|1-\frac{1}{4}\right|=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: A hyperbola having the transverse axis of length $\sqrt{2}$ has the same foci as that of the ellipse 3x^2 + 4y^2 = 12, then this hyperbola does not pass through which of the following points?
questions.question_raw: A hyperbola having the transverse axis of length {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} has the same foci as that of the ellipse 3x2 + 4y2 = 12, then this hyperbola does not pass through which of the following points?
questions.explanation:
The given ellipse:
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because c=\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}=\sqrt{4-3}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Foci = (± 1, 0)
Now for hyperbola:
Given: 2a {tex}=\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} c2 = a2 + b2 {tex}\Rightarrow 1=\frac{1}{2}+b^{2} \Rightarrow b=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
So, equation of hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{\frac{1}{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 2y2 = 1
So, option {tex}\left(\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} does not satisfy it.

questions.question: The centre of the ellipse $\frac{(x+y-3)^{2}}{9}+\frac{(x-y+1)^{2}}{16}=1$ is
questions.question_raw: The centre of the ellipse {tex}\frac{(x+y-3)^{2}}{9}+\frac{(x-y+1)^{2}}{16}=1{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Solving x + y - 3 = 0 and x - y + 1 = 0, we get (x, y) = (1, 2)

questions.question: If y = x and 3y + 2x = 0 are the equations of a pair of conjugate diameters of an ellipse, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: If y = x and 3y + 2x = 0 are the equations of a pair of conjugate diameters of an ellipse, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the ellipse be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
Now, slopes of the given diameters are m1 = 1, m2 = {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m1m2 {tex}=\frac{-2}{3}=\frac{-b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3b2 = 2a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a2(1 - e2) = 2a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - e2 = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{2}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}

questions.question: If a focal chord of y^2 = 16x is a tangent to (x - 6)^2 + y^2 = 2, then the values of the slope of this chord are
questions.question_raw: If a focal chord of y2 = 16x is a tangent to (x - 6)2 + y2 = 2, then the values of the slope of this chord are
questions.explanation:
The co-ordinates of the focus of the parabola
y2 = 16x are (4, 0).
The equations of tangents of slope m to the circle
(x - 6)2 + (y - 0)2 = {tex}(\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
are y = m (x - 6) {tex}\pm \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1+m^{2}}{/tex}
If these tangents pass through the focus
i.e., (4, 0), then
0 = -2m {tex}\pm \sqrt{2} \sqrt{1+m^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4m2 = 2(1 + m2) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 2m2 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}\pm{/tex}1

questions.question: The distance between the vertex of a parabola y = x^2 - 4x + 3 and the centre of the circle x^2 + (y - 3)^2 = 9 is
questions.question_raw: The distance between the vertex of a parabola y = x2 - 4x + 3 and the centre of the circle x2 + (y - 3)2 = 9 is
questions.explanation:
Centre of the circle x2 + (y - 3)2 = 9 is (0, 3).
y = x2 - 4x + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 = y + 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The vertex is (2, -1).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The distance between vertex and centre of the given circle is
{tex}=\sqrt{(2-0)^{2}+(-1-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{20}=2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}

questions.question: The eccentricity of the curve represented by the equation x^2 + 2y^2 - 2x + 3y + 2 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of the curve represented by the equation x2 + 2y2 - 2x + 3y + 2 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is
x2 + 2y2 - 2x + 3y + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(x-1)^{2}}{2}+\left(y+\frac{3}{4}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(x-1)^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)}+\frac{\left(y+\frac{3}{4}\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)}=1{/tex}
which is an ellipse with {tex}a^{2}=\frac{1}{8}{/tex} and {tex}b^{2}=\frac{1}{16}{/tex}
Since, a > b
{tex}e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=1-\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

questions.question: The eccentric angle of a point on the ellipse x^2 + 3y^2 = 6 at a distance 2 units from the centre of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: The eccentric angle of a point on the ellipse x2 + 3y2 = 6 at a distance 2 units from the centre of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
Let the eccentric angle of the point be {tex}\theta{/tex}. Then, its co-ordinates are{tex}(\sqrt{6} \cos \theta, \sqrt{2} \sin \theta){/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 6 cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} + 2sin2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos2{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If e_1 and e_2 are the eccentricities of the ellipse, $\frac{x^{2}}{18}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ and the hyperbola, $\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ respectively and (e_1, e_2) is a point on the ellipse, 15x^2 + 3y^2 = k, then k is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If e1 and e2 are the eccentricities of the ellipse, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{18}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} and the hyperbola, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} respectively and (e1, e2) is a point on the ellipse, 15x2 + 3y2 = k, then k is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Eccentricity of ellipse
{tex}e_{1}=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{18}}=\sqrt{\frac{7}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}{/tex}
Eccentricity of hyperbola
{tex}e_{2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{9}}=\sqrt{\frac{13}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{13}}{3}{/tex}
Since, the point (e1; e2) is on the ellipse
15x2 + 3y2 = k
Then, {tex}15 e_{1}^{2}+3 e_{2}^{2}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k=15\left(\frac{7}{9}\right)+3\left(\frac{13}{9}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 16

questions.question: For each point (x, y) on an ellipse, the sum of the distances from (x, y) to the points (2, 0) and (-2, 0) is 8. Then the positive value of x so that (x, 3) lies on the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: For each point (x, y) on an ellipse, the sum of the distances from (x, y) to the points (2, 0) and (-2, 0) is 8. Then the positive value of x so that (x, 3) lies on the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
2a = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4
Let F = (2, 0) and F' = (-2, 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ae = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
b2 = a2(1 - e2) = 16{tex}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} = 12
Equation of the ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{12}=1{/tex}
(x, 3) lies on it {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}2

questions.question: The length of the minor axis (along the y-axis) of an ellipse in the standard form is $\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}$. If this ellipse touches the line, x + 6y = 8; then its eccentricity is:
questions.question_raw: The length of the minor axis (along the y-axis) of an ellipse in the standard form is {tex}\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}. If this ellipse touches the line, x + 6y = 8; then its eccentricity is:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 ; a>b{/tex}
{tex}2 b=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow b=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Equation of tangent {tex}\equiv y=m x \pm \sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
Comparing with {tex}\equiv y=\frac{-x}{6}+\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}m=\frac{-1}{6}{/tex} and a2m2 + b2 {tex}=\frac{16}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}}{36}+\frac{4}{3}=\frac{16}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}}{36}=\frac{16}{9}-\frac{4}{3}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\pm{/tex}4
Now, the eccentricity of the ellipse {tex}(e)=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{3 \times 16}}=\sqrt{\frac{11}{12}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{11}{3}}{/tex}

questions.question: Let the line y = mx and the ellipse 2x^2 + y^2 = 1 intersect at a point P in the first quadrant. If the normal to this ellipse at P meets the co-ordinate axes at $\left(-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{2}}, 0\right)$ and $(0, \beta)$, then $\beta$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let the line y = mx and the ellipse 2x2 + y2 = 1 intersect at a point P in the first quadrant. If the normal to this ellipse at P meets the co-ordinate axes at {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{2}}, 0\right){/tex} and {tex}(0, \beta){/tex}, then {tex}\beta{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let P be (x1, y1).
So, the equation of normal at P is
{tex}\frac{x}{2 x_{1}}-\frac{y}{y_{1}}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
It passes through {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{2}}, 0\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-1}{6 \sqrt{2} x_{1}}=-\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x_{1}=\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
So, {tex}y_{1}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex} (as P lies in 1st quadrant)
So, {tex}\beta=\frac{y_{1}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If the chords of contact of tangents from two points (x_1, y_1) and (x_2, y_2) to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{v^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ are at right angles, then $\frac{x_{1} x_{2}}{y_{1} y_{2}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If the chords of contact of tangents from two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{v^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} are at right angles, then {tex}\frac{x_{1} x_{2}}{y_{1} y_{2}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Chords of contact are
{tex}\frac{x x_{1}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x x_{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
product of the slopes = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\frac{x_{1}}{a^{2}}}{\frac{y_{1}}{b^{2}}} \cdot\left(-\frac{\frac{x_{2}}{a^{2}}}{\frac{y_{2}}{b^{2}}}\right)=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1} x_{2}}{a^{4}} \cdot \frac{b^{4}}{y_{1} y_{2}}=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1} x_{2}}{y_{1} y_{2}}=-\frac{a^{4}}{b^{4}}{/tex}

questions.question: The foci of the ellipse 25x^2 + 50x + 9y^2 + 36y - 164 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The foci of the ellipse 25x2 + 50x + 9y2 + 36y - 164 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}\frac{(x+1)^{2}}{\frac{225}{25}}+\frac{(y+2)^{2}}{\frac{225}{9}}=1{/tex}
Here, {tex}a=\sqrt{\frac{225}{25}}=3, b=\sqrt{\frac{225}{9}}=5, e^{2}=1-\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
Focus = (-1, -2 {tex}\pm{/tex} 4)
= (-1, 2) and (-1, -6)

questions.question: The radius of the circle passing through the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ and having its centre (0, 3) is
questions.question_raw: The radius of the circle passing through the foci of the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex} and having its centre (0, 3) is
questions.explanation:
Here a2 = 16, b2 = 9
But b2 = a2(1 - e2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 9 = 16(1 - e2)
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{2}=1-\frac{9}{16}=\frac{7}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}{/tex}
Now, foci are (ae, 0), (-ae, 0)
i.e., {tex}(\sqrt{7}, 0),(-\sqrt{7}, 0){/tex}
Centre is (0, 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The radius of the circle
{tex}=\sqrt{(\sqrt{7}-0)^{2}+(0-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{7+9}{/tex} = 4

questions.question: The equation of the parabola whose vertex is at (2, -1) and focus at (2, -3) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the parabola whose vertex is at (2, -1) and focus at (2, -3) is

options.text:
a = |VS|
$=\sqrt{(2-2)^{2}+(-3+1)^{2}}$
= 2
The required equation is
(x - 2)^2 = - 4 $\times$ 2 (y + 1)
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 + 4 - 4x = -8y - 8
$\Rightarrow$ x^2 - 4x + 8y + 12 = 0

questions.question: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is 6 and the distance between its directrices is 12, then the length of its latus rectum is:
questions.question_raw: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is 6 and the distance between its directrices is 12, then the length of its latus rectum is:
questions.explanation:
2ae = 6 and {tex}\frac{2 a}{e}{/tex} = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ae = 3 ...(i)
and {tex}\frac{a}{e}=6 \Rightarrow e=\frac{a}{6}{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 18 [From (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = a2 - a2e2 = 18 - 9 = 9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I atus recmm {tex}=\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=\frac{2 \times 9}{3 \sqrt{2}}=3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The circles x^2 + y^2 - 10x + 16 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 = r^2 intersect each other in two distinct points if:
questions.question_raw: The circles x2 + y2 - 10x + 16 = 0 and x2 + y2 = r2 intersect each other in two distinct points if:
questions.explanation:
Centres and radii of two circles are C1(5, 0); 3 (= r1) and C2 (0, 0); r (= r2)
As the circles intersect each other in two distinct points,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} |r1 - r2| < C1C2 < r1 + r2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x - 3| < 5 < r + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 < r < 8

questions.question: If 3x + 4y = $12 \sqrt{2}$ is a tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ for some a $\in$ R, then the distance between the foci of the ellipse is:
questions.question_raw: If 3x + 4y = {tex}12 \sqrt{2}{/tex} is a tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex} for some a {tex}\in{/tex} R, then the distance between the foci of the ellipse is:
questions.explanation:
3x + 4y = {tex}12 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4y = -3x + {tex}12 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=-\frac{3}{4} x+3 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Now, condition of tangency, c2 = a2m2 + b2
{tex}\therefore 18=a^{2} \cdot \frac{\overline{9}}{16}+9 \Rightarrow a^{2} \cdot \frac{9}{16}=9{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4
Eccentricity {tex}e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{16}}=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a e=\frac{\sqrt{7}}{4} .4=\sqrt{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Focus are {tex}(\pm \sqrt{7}, 0){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance between foci of ellipse {tex}=2 \sqrt{7}{/tex}

questions.question: The equations of the latus rectum of the ellipse 9x^2 + 25y^2 - 36x + 50y - 164 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The equations of the latus rectum of the ellipse 9x2 + 25y2 - 36x + 50y - 164 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
9x2 + 25y2 - 36x + 50y- 164 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(x-2)^{2}}{25}+\frac{(y+1)^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}, which is of the form
{tex}\frac{X^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{Y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equations of latera recta are
X = {tex}\pm{/tex} ae {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2 = {tex}\pm 5\left(\frac{4}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 {tex}\pm{/tex} 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 6 = 0, x + 2 = 0

questions.question: The area of the triangle formed inside the parabola y^2 = 4x and whose ordinates of vertices are 1, 2 and 4 will be
questions.question_raw: The area of the triangle formed inside the parabola y2 = 4x and whose ordinates of vertices are 1, 2 and 4 will be
questions.explanation: Area = {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}(1 - 2)(2 - 4)(4 - 1) = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ are the parameters of end points of a focal chord of ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, then $\tan \left(\frac{\theta_{1}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\theta_{2}}{2}\right)$ =
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\theta_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_{2}{/tex} are the parameters of end points of a focal chord of ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, then {tex}\tan \left(\frac{\theta_{1}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\theta_{2}}{2}\right){/tex} =
questions.explanation:
P({tex}\theta_1{/tex}) {tex}\equiv{/tex} (acos{tex}\theta_1{/tex}, bsin{tex}\theta_1{/tex}), Q({tex}\theta_2{/tex}) {tex}\equiv{/tex}(acos{tex}\theta_2{/tex}, bsin{tex}\theta_2{/tex}) be the extremities of a focal chord of ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} through a focus S(ae, 0)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equation of focal chord is
{tex}\frac{x}{a} \cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)+\frac{y}{b} \sin \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{2}\right){/tex}
Since, it passes through (ae, 0)
{tex}\therefore \frac{a e}{a} \cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)+0=\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{e}{1}=\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{e-1}{e+1}=\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{2}\right)-\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{2}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{\theta_{1}}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta_{1}}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta_{2}}{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\tan \left(\frac{\theta_{1}}{2}\right) \tan \left(\frac{\theta_{2}}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question: The equation of parabola whose focus is (5, 3) and directrix is 3x - 4y + 1 = 0, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of parabola whose focus is (5, 3) and directrix is 3x - 4y + 1 = 0, is
questions.explanation:
Let P(x, y) be any point on the parabola.
Focus S = (5, 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 5)2 + (y - 3)2 = {tex}\left(\frac{3 x-4 y+1}{\sqrt{9+16}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 25(x2 + 25 - 10x + y2 + 9 - 6y)
= 9x2 + 16y2 + 1 - 12xy + 6x - 8y - 12xy
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x2 + 9y2 - 256x - 142y + 24xy + 849 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (4x + 3y)2 - 256x - 142y + 849 = 0

questions.question: The position of the point (1, 3) with respect to the ellipse 4x^2 + 9y^2 - 16x - 54y + 61 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The position of the point (1, 3) with respect to the ellipse 4x2 + 9y2 - 16x - 54y + 61 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Here, 4 + 9(3)2 - 16(1) - 54(3) + 61 < 0
Therefore, the point is inside the ellipse
{tex}\frac{4(x-2)^{2}}{36}+\frac{9(y-3)^{2}}{36}=1{/tex}
Now, equation of major axis is y - 3 = 0 and point (1, 3) lies on it.

questions.question: If the tangent to the parabola y^2 = x at a point $(\alpha, \beta),(\beta>0)$ is also a tangent to the ellipse, x^2 + 2y^2 = 1, then $\alpha$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the tangent to the parabola y2 = x at a point {tex}(\alpha, \beta),(\beta>0){/tex} is also a tangent to the ellipse, x2 + 2y2 = 1, then {tex}\alpha{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let tangent to parabola at point {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4 m^{2}},-\frac{1}{2 m}\right){/tex} is {tex}y=m x+\frac{1}{4 m}{/tex}
and tangent to ellipse is, y = mx {tex}\pm \sqrt{m^{2}+\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
Now, condition for common tangency,
{tex}\frac{1}{4 m}=\pm \sqrt{m^{2}+\frac{1}{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{16 m^{2}}=m^{2}+\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16m4 + 8m2 - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow m^{2}=\frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{64+64}}{2(16)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-8 \pm 8 \sqrt{2}}{2(16)}=\frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{4 m^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \frac{\sqrt{2}-1}{4}}=\sqrt{2}+1{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of the circle drawn with the two foci of $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ as the end-points of a diameter is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle drawn with the two foci of {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} as the end-points of a diameter is
questions.explanation:
The coordinates of two foci of the given ellipse are S(ae, 0) and S'(-ae, 0).
So, equation of the circle with SS' as diameter is
(x - ae) (x + ae) + (y - 0) (y - 0) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = a2e2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = a2 - b2 ...[{tex}\because{/tex} b2 = a2 (1 - e2)]

questions.question: The equation of a circle passing through the vertex and the extremities of the latus rectum of the parabola y^2 = 8x is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a circle passing through the vertex and the extremities of the latus rectum of the parabola y2 = 8x is
questions.explanation:
For parabola, y2 = 8x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2
vertex of y2 = 8x is O {tex}\equiv{/tex} (0, 0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The circle passes through the points (0,0)
L(2, 4) and L'(2, -4) where LL' is latus rectum.
Let the centre be (h, k). The
(h - 0)2 + (k - 0)2 = {h - 2)2 + (k + 4)2 and (h - 0)2 + (k - 0)2 = {h - 2)2 + (k - 4)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h - 2k = 5 and h + 2k = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 5, k = 0
Required equation is (x - 5)2 + y2 = 52
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 10x = 0

questions.question: If the tangent at a point on the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{27}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1$ meets the coordinate axes at A and B, and O is the origin, then the minimum area (in sq. units) of the triangle OAB is:
questions.question_raw: If the tangent at a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{27}+\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1{/tex} meets the coordinate axes at A and B, and O is the origin, then the minimum area (in sq. units) of the triangle OAB is:
questions.explanation:
Equation of a tangent to the ellipse
Ì£{tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{27}} \cos \theta+\frac{y}{\sqrt{3}} \sin \theta=1{/tex}
The area bounded by line and co-ordinate axis
{tex}\Delta=\frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{27}}{\cos \theta} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sin \theta}=\frac{9}{\sin 2 \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}= will be minimum when sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Delta_{\min }{/tex} = 9

questions.question: If the foci of an ellipse are (0, $\pm$4) and the equation of the directrices are y = $\pm$9, then the equation of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: If the foci of an ellipse are (0, {tex}\pm{/tex}4) and the equation of the directrices are y = {tex}\pm{/tex}9, then the equation of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
Foci of an ellipse are (0, {tex}\pm{/tex}4).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Distance between foci = 8 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2be = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} be = 4 ...(i)
Also, equation of directrices are
y = {tex}\pm{/tex}9 {tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{b}{e}{/tex} = 9 ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get
b = 6 and {tex}e=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
a2 = b2(1 - e2) = {tex}36\left(1-\frac{4}{9}\right){/tex} = 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required equation of the ellipse is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{20}+\frac{y^{2}}{36}=1{/tex} {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 9x2 + 5y2 = 180

questions.question: Let E be the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ and C be the circle x^2 + y^2 = 9. Let P and Q be the points (1, 2) and (2, 1) respectively. Then
questions.question_raw: Let E be the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} and C be the circle x2 + y2 = 9. Let P and Q be the points (1, 2) and (2, 1) respectively. Then

options.text: P lies inside both C and E
Q lies inside C but outside E
Q lies outside both C and E
P lies inside C but outside E

questions.explanation: The given ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex}. The value of the expression {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} is positive for x = 1, y = 2 and negative for x = 2, y = 1. Therefore P lies outside E and Q lies inside E. The value of the expression x2 + y2 - 9 is negative for both the points P and Q. Therefore P and Q both lie inside C. Hence P lies inside C but outside E.

questions.question: The equation of the ellipse whose centre is at origin and which passes through the points (-3, 1) and (2, -2) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the ellipse whose centre is at origin and which passes through the points (-3, 1) and (2, -2) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the ellipse be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}.
Since it passes through (-3, 1) and (2, -2)
{tex}\therefore \frac{9}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
By solving both equations, we get
{tex}a^{2}=\frac{32}{3}, b^{2}=\frac{32}{5}{/tex}
Hence, the required equation of the ellipse is 3x2 + 5y2 = 32.

questions.question: If P is a point on the parabola y^2 = 8x and A is the point (1, 0), then the locus of the midpoint of the line segment AP is
questions.question_raw: If P is a point on the parabola y2 = 8x and A is the point (1, 0), then the locus of the midpoint of the line segment AP is
questions.explanation:
Equation of parabola is y2 = 8x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2
Let P = (2t2, 4t)
Mid point = (x, y) {tex}=\left(\frac{2 t^{2}+1}{2}, \frac{4 t+0}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 x-1}{2}=t^{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{4}=t^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y^{2}}{4}=\frac{2 x-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^{2}=4\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question: A variable circle touches the y-axis and cuts off an intercept of length 2 units on the x-axis. The curve described by its centre is
questions.question_raw: A variable circle touches the y-axis and cuts off an intercept of length 2 units on the x-axis. The curve described by its centre is
questions.explanation:
Let r be the radius of the circle

QC2 = CM2 + QM2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r2 = h2 + 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} r2 - h2 = 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (r, h) satisfies x2 - y2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (r, h) describes a hyperbola.

questions.question: If the line y = mx + c is a common tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{100}-\frac{y^{2}}{64}=1$ and the circle x^2 + y^2 = 36, then which one of the following is true?
questions.question_raw: If the line y = mx + c is a common tangent to the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{100}-\frac{y^{2}}{64}=1{/tex} and the circle x2 + y2 = 36, then which one of the following is true?
questions.explanation:
General tangent to hyperbola in slope form is
{tex}y=m x \pm \sqrt{100 m^{2}-64}{/tex}
and the general tangent to the circle in slope form is
{tex}y=m x \pm 6 \sqrt{1+m^{2}}{/tex}
For common tangent,
36(1 + m2) = 100m2 - 64
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 100 = 64m2 {tex}\Rightarrow m^{2}=\frac{100}{64}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore c^{2}=36\left(1+\frac{100}{64}\right)=\frac{164 \times 36}{64}=\frac{369}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4c2 = 369

questions.question: The straight lines y = $\pm$x intersect the parabola y^2 = 8x at points P and Q, then length of PQ is
questions.question_raw: The straight lines y = {tex}\pm{/tex}x intersect the parabola y2 = 8x at points P and Q, then length of PQ is
questions.explanation:
Given, y = {tex}\pm{/tex}x ...(i)
and y2 = 8x ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii),
the point of intersection are P(8, 8) and Q(8, -8)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of PQ {tex}=\sqrt{(8-8)^{2}+(8+8)^{2}}{/tex}
= 16

questions.question: Let P(3, 3) be a point on the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$. If the normal to it at P intersects the x-axis at (9, 0) and e is its eccentricity, then the ordered pair (a^2, e^2) is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let P(3, 3) be a point on the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}. If the normal to it at P intersects the x-axis at (9, 0) and e is its eccentricity, then the ordered pair (a2, e2) is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} The equation of the hyperbola is
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Equation of hyperbola passes through (3, 3)
{tex}\frac{1}{a^{2}}-\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex} ...(i)
Equation of normal at point (3, 3) is:
{tex}\frac{x-3}{\frac{1}{a^{2}} \cdot 3}=\frac{y-3}{-\frac{1}{b^{2}} \cdot 3}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} It passes through (9, 0)
{tex}\frac{6}{\frac{1}{a^{2}}}=\frac{-3}{-\frac{1}{b^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a^{2}}{/tex} ...(ii)
From equations (i) and (ii),
{tex}a^{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex}, b2 = 9
{tex}\because{/tex} Eccentricity = e, then {tex}e^{2}=1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left(a^{2}, e^{2}\right)=\left(\frac{9}{2}, 3\right){/tex}

questions.question: If a circle cuts a rectangular hyperbola xy = c^2 in A, B, C, D and the parameters of these four points be t_1, t_2, t_3 and t_4 respectively, then
questions.question_raw: If a circle cuts a rectangular hyperbola xy = c2 in A, B, C, D and the parameters of these four points be t1, t2, t3 and t4 respectively, then
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of circle be x2 + y2 = a2.
Parametric form of xy = c2 are x = ct, y = {tex}\frac{c}{t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c2t2 + {tex}\frac{c^{2}}{t^{2}}{/tex} = a2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c2t4 - a2t2 + c2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}The product of the roots is t1t2t3t4 = {tex}\frac{c^{2}}{c^{2}}{/tex} = 1 ...[product of roots = {tex}\frac{\text { constant }}{\text { coeff.of } t^{4}}{/tex}]

questions.question: If (4, 0) and (-4, 0) be the vertices and (6, 0) and (-6, 0) be the foci of a hyperbola, then its eccentricity is
questions.question_raw: If (4, 0) and (-4, 0) be the vertices and (6, 0) and (-6, 0) be the foci of a hyperbola, then its eccentricity is
questions.explanation: We have vertices ({tex}\pm{/tex}4, 0) {tex}\equiv{/tex} ({tex}\pm{/tex}a, 0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4 and foci ({tex}\pm{/tex}6, 0) {tex}\equiv{/tex} ({tex}\pm{/tex}ae, 0) {tex}\Rightarrow e=\frac{6}{4}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The eccentricity of an ellipse, with its centre at the origin, is $\frac{1}{2}$. If one of the directrices is x = 4, then the equation of the ellipse is
questions.question_raw: The eccentricity of an ellipse, with its centre at the origin, is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. If one of the directrices is x = 4, then the equation of the ellipse is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the ellipse be {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}, (a > b)
Now, e2 {tex}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=1-e^{2}=1-\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, one of the directrices is x = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{a}{e}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4e {tex}=4 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow b^{2}=\frac{3}{4} a^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \times 4=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required equation of the ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{v^{2}}{3}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3x2 + 4y2 = 12

questions.question: Let e_1 and e_2 be the eccentricities of the ellipse, $\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1(b < 5) and the hyperbola, $\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ respectively satisfying e_1e_2 = 1. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the distances between the foci of the ellipse and the foci of the hyperbola respectively, then the ordered pair $(\alpha, \beta)$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let e1 and e2 be the eccentricities of the ellipse, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1(b < 5) and the hyperbola, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} respectively satisfying e1e2 = 1. If {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are the distances between the foci of the ellipse and the foci of the hyperbola respectively, then the ordered pair {tex}(\alpha, \beta){/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Equation of ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
Then, {tex}e_{1}=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^{2}}{25}}{/tex}
The equation of hyperbola, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{v^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
Then, {tex}e_{2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{16}}{/tex}
e1e2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (e1e2)2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-\frac{b^{2}}{25}\right)\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{16}\right)=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+\frac{b^{2}}{16}-\frac{b^{2}}{25}-\frac{b^{4}}{25 \times 16}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9}{16 \cdot 25} b^{2}-\frac{b^{4}}{25 \cdot 16}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 9
{tex}\therefore e_{1}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
And, {tex}e_{2}=\sqrt{1+\frac{9}{16}}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
Distance between focii of ellipse
= {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 2ae1= 2(5)(e1) = 8
Distance between focii of hyperbola
= {tex}\beta{/tex} = 2ae2 = 2(4)(e2) = 10
{tex}\therefore(\alpha, \beta){/tex} = (8, 10)

questions.question: The foci of the hyperbola 2x^2 - 3y^2 = 5 are
questions.question_raw: The foci of the hyperbola 2x2 - 3y2 = 5 are
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(\pm \frac{5}{\sqrt{6}}, 0\right){/tex}
The given equation of hyperbola is 2x2 - 3y2 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{\frac{5}{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{\frac{5}{3}}=1{/tex}
Here, {tex}a^{2}=\frac{5}{2}, b^{2}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
{tex}e=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{\frac{5}{3}}{\frac{5}{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The foci of hyperbola are ({tex}\pm{/tex}ae, 0)
{tex}=\left(\pm \sqrt{\frac{5}{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{5}{3}}, 0\right)=\left(\pm \frac{5}{\sqrt{6}}, 0\right){/tex}

questions.question: A line parallel to the straight line 2x - y = 0 is tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{4}-\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1$ at the point (x_1, y_1). Then $x_{1}^{2}+5 y_{1}^{2}$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: A line parallel to the straight line 2x - y = 0 is tangent to the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}-\frac{y^{2}}{2}=1{/tex} at the point (x1, y1). Then {tex}x_{1}^{2}+5 y_{1}^{2}{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
The tangent to the hyperbola at the point (x1, y1) is,
xx1 - 2yy1 - 4 = 0
The given equation of tangent is
2x - y = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}}{2 y_{1}}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = 4y1 ...(i)
Since, point (x1, y1) lie on hyperbola.
{tex}\therefore \frac{x_{1}^{2}}{4}-\frac{y_{1}^{2}}{2}-1=0{/tex} ...(ii)
On solving eqs. (i) and (ii)
{tex}y_{1}^{2}=\frac{2}{7}, x_{1}^{2}=\frac{32}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x_{1}^{2}+5 y_{1}^{2}=\frac{32}{7}+5 \times \frac{2}{7}{/tex} = 6

questions.question: The locus of a variable point whose distance from (-2, 0) is $\frac{2}{3}$ times its distance from the line $x=-\frac{9}{2}$ is
questions.question_raw: The locus of a variable point whose distance from (-2, 0) is {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} times its distance from the line {tex}x=-\frac{9}{2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let the point be P(x1, y1).
According to the given condition,
{tex}\sqrt{\left(x_{1}+2\right)^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}=\frac{2}{3}\left(x_{1}+\frac{9}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}+2\right)^{2}+y_{1}^{2}=\frac{4}{9}\left(x_{1}+\frac{9}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 9\left(x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+4 x_{1}+4\right)=4\left(x_{1}^{2}+\frac{81}{4}+9 x_{1}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 x_{1}^{2}+9 y_{1}^{2}=45{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}^{2}}{9}+\frac{y_{1}^{2}}{5}=1{/tex}
Locus of (x1, y1) is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1{/tex}, which is equation of an ellipse.

questions.question: Let the eccentricity of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ be reciprocal to that of the ellipse x^2 + 4y^2 = 4. If the hyperbola passes through a focus of the ellipse, then a focus of the hyperbola is at
questions.question_raw: Let the eccentricity of the hyperbola {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} be reciprocal to that of the ellipse x2 + 4y2 = 4. If the hyperbola passes through a focus of the ellipse, then a focus of the hyperbola is at
questions.explanation:
The equation of the ellipse is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{1}=1{/tex}
Let e be its eccentricity.
Then, {tex}e=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
The foci of the ellipse are S ({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 0) and S'({tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 0).
Eccentricity of the hyperbola {tex}=\frac{1}{e}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
The hyperbola passes through S({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 0).
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{a^{2}}-0{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = 3  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The co-ordinates of the foci are ({tex}\pm{/tex}2, 0).

questions.question: Let F be the focus of the parabola y^2 - 2x + 6y + 11 = 0. The equation of the circle with centre at F and which touches the parabola is
questions.question_raw: Let F be the focus of the parabola y2 - 2x + 6y + 11 = 0. The equation of the circle with centre at F and which touches the parabola is
questions.explanation:
Given parabola is (y + 3)2 = 2(x - 1)
vertex A = (1, -3)
Let X = x - 1, Y = y + 3
and {tex}a=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Focus {tex}=\left(\mathrm{X}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{Y}=0\right){/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \mathrm{F}=\left(x=\frac{3}{2}, y=-3\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The centre of required circle {tex}=\left(\frac{3}{2},-3\right){/tex}
and radius {tex}=|\mathrm{FA}|=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Required equation is {tex}\left(x-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The length of transverse axis of the hyperbola 3x^2 - 4y^2 = 32 is
questions.question_raw: The length of transverse axis of the hyperbola 3x2 - 4y2 = 32 is
questions.explanation:
The given equation can be written as
{tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\frac{32}{3}}-\frac{y^{2}}{8}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}=1{/tex}
Here, {tex}a^{2}=\left(\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow a=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
The length of transverse axis = 2a {tex}=2 \times \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}

questions.question: The set of values of a in the interval $\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$, for which the point (sin$\alpha$, cos$\alpha$) does not lie outside the parabola 2y^2 + x - 2 = 0, is
questions.question_raw: The set of values of a in the interval {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]{/tex}, for which the point (sin{tex}\alpha{/tex}, cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}) does not lie outside the parabola 2y2 + x - 2 = 0, is
questions.explanation: {tex}\left[\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{5 \pi}{6}\right] \cup\left[\pi, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]{/tex}

questions.question: For the ellipse 4(x - 2y + 1)^2 + 9(2x + y + 2)^2 = 180, lengths of major and minor axes are respectively
questions.question_raw: For the ellipse 4(x - 2y + 1)2 + 9(2x + y + 2)2 = 180, lengths of major and minor axes are respectively
questions.explanation:
We have, 4(x - 2y + 1)2 + 9(2x + y + 2)2 = 180
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{(x-2 y+1)^{2}}{45}+\frac{(2 x+y+2)^{2}}{20}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\frac{x-2 y+1}{\sqrt{1+4}}\right)^{2}}{3^{2}}+\frac{\left(\frac{2 x+y+2}{\sqrt{4+1}}\right)^{2}}{2^{2}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{X^{2}}{3^{2}}+\frac{Y^{2}}{2^{2}}=1{/tex}, where
{tex}\mathrm{X}=\frac{x-2 y+1}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{Y}=\frac{2 x+y+2}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Length of the major axis = 2(3) = 6,
Length of the minor axis = 2(2) = 4.

questions.question: Let R represent the smaller region bounded by x^2 + y^2 = 4 and y^2 = 4x. If (a, b) is the largest interval of values of $\alpha$ such that (-2$\alpha$, $\alpha$ + 1) lies in the region R, then
questions.question_raw: Let R represent the smaller region bounded by x2 + y2 = 4 and y2 = 4x. If (a, b) is the largest interval of values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} such that (-2{tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1) lies in the region R, then

options.text: a and b both are negative numbers
a and b both are rational
b is a rational number and a is irrational

questions.explanation:
Point P lies inside the circle.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (-2{tex}\alpha{/tex})2 + ({tex}\alpha{/tex} +1)2 < 4
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 5{tex}\alpha^{2}{/tex} + 2{tex}\alpha{/tex} - 3 < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (5{tex}\alpha{/tex} - 3) ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1) < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-1<\alpha<\frac{3}{5}{/tex} ...(i)
Point P lies inside the parabola.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1)2 < 4 (-2{tex}\alpha{/tex})
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^2{/tex} + 10{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 < 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + 5)2 < 24
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-2 \sqrt{6}<\alpha+5<2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow-5-2 \sqrt{6}<\alpha<-5+2 \sqrt{6}{/tex} ...(ii)
(i) and (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow-1<\alpha<-5+2 \sqrt{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -1, b = 2 {tex}\sqrt{6}{/tex} - 5 = -0.101 (approx)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a and b both are negative numbers.

questions.question: For some $\theta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, if the eccentricity of the hyperbola, x^2 - y^2 sec^2 $\theta$ = 10 is $\sqrt{5}$ times the eccentricity of the ellipse, x^2 sec^2$\theta$ + y^2 = 5, then the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse, is:
questions.question_raw: For some {tex}\theta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, if the eccentricity of the hyperbola, x2 - y2 sec2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 10 is {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} times the eccentricity of the ellipse, x2 sec2{tex}\theta{/tex} + y2 = 5, then the length of the latus rectum of the ellipse, is:
questions.explanation:
Hyperbola : {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{10}-\frac{y^{2}}{10 \cos ^{2} \theta}=1 \Rightarrow e_{1}=\sqrt{1+\cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} and Ellipse : {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{5 \cos ^{2} \theta}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e_{2}=\sqrt{1-\cos ^{2} \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex}
According to the question, {tex}e_{1}=\sqrt{5} e_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 1+\cos ^{2} \theta=5 \sin ^{2} \theta \Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Now length of latus rectum of ellipse {tex}=\frac{2 a^{2}}{b}=\frac{10 \cos ^{2} \theta}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{20}{3 \sqrt{5}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{5}}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The distance between the directrices of the hyperbola x = 8 sec $\theta$, y = 8 tan $\theta$ is
questions.question_raw: The distance between the directrices of the hyperbola x = 8 sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = 8 tan {tex}\theta{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have,
x = 8 sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = 8 tan {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{8}{/tex} = sec{tex}\theta{/tex}, {tex}\frac{y}{8}{/tex} = tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x^{2}}{8^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{8^{2}}{/tex}= sec2{tex}\theta{/tex} - tan2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
Here, a = 8, b = 8
{tex}e=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{8^{2}}{8^{2}}}=\sqrt{1+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
The distance between directries {tex}=\frac{2 a}{e}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 8}{\sqrt{2}}=8 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The centre of the circle passing through the point (0, 1) and touching the parabola y = x^2 at the point (2, 4) is:
questions.question_raw: The centre of the circle passing through the point (0, 1) and touching the parabola y = x2 at the point (2, 4) is:
questions.explanation:
The circle passes through A(0, 1) and B(2, 4). So its centre is the point of intersection of perpendicular bisector of AB and normal to the parabola at (2, 4).
Perpendicular bisector of AB:
{tex}y-\frac{5}{2}=-\frac{2}{3}(x-1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 6y = 19 ...(i)
Equation of normal to the parabola at (2, 4) is,
y - 4 = {tex}-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}(x - 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 4y = 18 ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i) and (ii), {tex}x=-\frac{16}{5}, y=\frac{53}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Centre of the circle is {tex}\left(-\frac{16}{5}, \frac{53}{10}\right){/tex}

questions.question: The vertices of the hyperbola 9x^2 - 16y^2 - 36x + 96y - 252 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The vertices of the hyperbola 9x2 - 16y2 - 36x + 96y - 252 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
We have, 9x2 - 16y2 - 36x + 96y - 252 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \frac{(x-2)^{2}}{16}-\frac{(y-3)^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{X^{2}}{16}-\frac{Y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex}
The vertices are (X = {tex}\pm{/tex}a, Y = 0)
i.e.,(x - 2 = {tex}\pm{/tex}4 , y - 3 = 0)
The vertices of the hyperbola are (6, 3) and (-2 ,3).

questions.question: If lx + my + n = 0 is tangent to the parabola x^2 = y, then condition of tangency is
questions.question_raw: If lx + my + n = 0 is tangent to the parabola x2 = y, then condition of tangency is
questions.explanation:
We have, lx + my + n = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-m}{l} y-\frac{n}{l}{/tex} ...(i)
and x2 = y ...(ii)
Now, condition of tangency is {tex}c=\frac{a}{m}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-n}{l}=\frac{1}{4} \times\left(\frac{-l}{m}\right){/tex} ...[From (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} l2 = 4 mn

questions.question: If a hyperbola passes through the point P(10, 16) and it has vertices at ($\pm$6, 0), then the equation of the normal to it at P is:
questions.question_raw: If a hyperbola passes through the point P(10, 16) and it has vertices at ({tex}\pm{/tex}6, 0), then the equation of the normal to it at P is:
questions.explanation:
Let the hyperbola is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex}
If a hyperbola passes through vertices at ({tex}\pm{/tex}6, 0), then
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 6
As hyperbola passes through the point P(10, 16)
{tex}\therefore \frac{100}{36}-\frac{256}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 = 144
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required hyperbola is {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{36}-\frac{v^{2}}{144}=1{/tex}
Equation of normal is {tex}\frac{36 x}{10}+\frac{144 y}{16}{/tex} = 36 + 144
{tex}\therefore{/tex} At P(10, 16) normal is
{tex}\frac{36 x}{10}+\frac{144 y}{16}{/tex} = 36 + 144
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x + 5y = 100

questions.question: If the length of the chord of the circle, x^2 + y^2 = r^2(r > 0) along the line, y - 2x = 3 is r, then r^2 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the length of the chord of the circle, x2 + y2 = r2(r > 0) along the line, y - 2x = 3 is r, then r2 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
In right {tex}\Delta{/tex}RSQ, sin {tex}60^{\circ}=\frac{R S}{r}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow R S=r \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3} r}{2}{/tex}
Now equation of PQ is y - 2x - 3 = 0
{tex}\therefore \frac{\sqrt{3} r}{2}=\frac{|0+0-3|}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{3} r}{2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{5}} \Rightarrow r=\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{5} \Rightarrow r^{2}=\frac{12}{5}{/tex}

questions.question: A line y = mx + 1 intersects the circle (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25 at the points P and Q. If the midpoint of the line segment PQ has x-coordinate $-\frac{3}{5}$, then which one of the following options is correct?
questions.question_raw: A line y = mx + 1 intersects the circle (x - 3)2 + (y + 2)2 = 25 at the points P and Q. If the midpoint of the line segment PQ has x-coordinate {tex}-\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, then which one of the following options is correct?
questions.explanation:
Given : Circle (x - 3)2 + (y + 2)2 = 25, with centre C(3, -2) and radius 5 is intersected by a line y = mx + 1 at P & Q such that co-ordinates of mid point R of PQ is {tex}-\frac{3}{5}{/tex}. Since x-coordinates of point R is {tex}-\frac{3}{5}{/tex} and point R lies on the line y = mx + 1, therefore y-coordinate of R will be {tex}\frac{3 m}{5}+1{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore R\left(-\frac{3}{5}, \frac{-3 m}{5}+1\right){/tex}

Since R is the midpoint of PQ, therefore CR {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\frac{-3 m}{5}+1+2}{-\frac{3}{5}-3} \times m{/tex} = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 - 5m + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 2, 3

questions.question: Locus of the image of the point (2, 3) in the line (2x - 3y + 4) + k(x - 2y + 3) = 0, k $\in$ R, is a:
questions.question_raw: Locus of the image of the point (2, 3) in the line (2x - 3y + 4) + k(x - 2y + 3) = 0, k {tex}\in{/tex} R, is a:
questions.explanation:
Intersection point of 2x - 3y + 4 = 0 and x - 2y + 3 = 0 is (1, 2)

Let the image of 4 (2, 3) is {tex}B(\alpha, \beta){/tex}
Since, P is the fixed point for given family of lines
So, PB = PA
{tex}(\alpha-1)^{2}+(\beta-2)^{2}{/tex} = (2 - 1)2 + (3 - 2)2
{tex}(\alpha-1)^{2}+(\beta-2)^{2}{/tex} = 1 + 1 = 2
(x - 1)2 + (y - 2)2 {tex}=(\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
Compare with
(x - a)2 + (y - b)2 = r2
Therefore, the given locus is a circle with centre (1, 2) and radius {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}.

questions.question: A square is inscribed in the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 4y + 3 = 0. Its sides are parallel to the coordinate axes. The one vertex of the square is:
questions.question_raw: A square is inscribed in the circle x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y + 3 = 0. Its sides are parallel to the coordinate axes. The one vertex of the square is:
questions.explanation:
Given circle is x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y + 3 = 0. Its centre (1, -2). Lines through centre (1, -2) and parallel to axes are x = 1 and y = -2.

Let the side of the square be 2k.
Then sides of square are x = 1 - k and x = 1 + k
and y = -2 - k and y = -2 + k
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Co-ordinates of P, Q, R, S are (1 + k, -2 + k), (1 - k, -2 + k), (1 - k, -2 - k), (1 + k, -2 - k) respectively.
Also, P(1 + k, -2 + k) lies on the given circle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 + k)2 + (-2 + k)2 - 2 (1 + k) + 4 (-2 + k) + 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2k2 = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1 or -1
If k = 1, then P(2, -1), Q (0, -1), R(0, -3), 5(2, -3)
If k = -1, then P(0, -3), Q(2, -3), R(2, -1), 5(0, -1)

questions.question: Let P be a point on the parabola, y^2 = 12x and N be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from P on the axis of the parabola. A line is now drawn through the mid-point M of PN, parallel to its axis which meets the parabola at Q. If the y-intercept of the line NQ is $\frac{4}{3}$, then:
questions.question_raw: Let P be a point on the parabola, y2 = 12x and N be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from P on the axis of the parabola. A line is now drawn through the mid-point M of PN, parallel to its axis which meets the parabola at Q. If the y-intercept of the line NQ is {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}, then:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} y2 = 12x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 3
Let P(at2, 2at)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N(at2, 0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M(at2, at)
{tex}\because{/tex} Equation of QM is y = at
So, y2 = 4ax {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{a t^{2}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow Q\left(\frac{a t^{2}}{4}, a t\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation of QN is {tex}y=\frac{-4}{3 t}{/tex}(x - at2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} QN passes through {tex}\left(0, \frac{4}{3}\right){/tex}, then
 {tex}\frac{4}{3}=-\frac{4}{3 t}\left(-a t^{2}\right) \Rightarrow a t=1 \Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Now, {tex}M Q=\frac{3}{4} a t^{2}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: The area (in sq. units) of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the parabola y^2 = 8x, with one of its vertices on the vertex of this parabola, is:
questions.question_raw: The area (in sq. units) of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the parabola y2 = 8x, with one of its vertices on the vertex of this parabola, is:
questions.explanation:
Let A = (2t2, 4t) and B = (2t2, -4t)

For equilateral triangle ({tex}\angle{/tex}AOM = 30o)
{tex}\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{4 t}{2 t^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 t}{2 t^{2}} \Rightarrow t=2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Area {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 8(2 \sqrt{3}) \cdot 2 \cdot 24=192 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If one end of a focal chord AB of the parabola y^2 = 8x is at $A\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},-2\right)$, then the equation of the tangent to it at B is:
questions.question_raw: If one end of a focal chord AB of the parabola y2 = 8x is at {tex}A\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},-2\right){/tex}, then the equation of the tangent to it at B is:
questions.explanation:
Let parabola y2 = 8x at point {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2},-2\right){/tex} is (2t2, 4t)
{tex}\Rightarrow t=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
Parameter of other end of focal chord is 2
So, coordinates of B is (8, 8)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation of tangent at B is 8y - 4(x + 8) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y - x = 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 2y + 8 = 0

questions.question: Let P be the point on the parabola, y^2 = 8x which is at a minimum distance from the centre C of the circle, x^2 + (y + 6)^2 = 1. Then the equation of the circle, passing through C and having its centre at P is:
questions.question_raw: Let P be the point on the parabola, y2 = 8x which is at a minimum distance from the centre C of the circle, x2 + (y + 6)2 = 1. Then the equation of the circle, passing through C and having its centre at P is:
questions.explanation:
Minimum distance {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} perpendicular distance
Eqn of normal at p(2t2, 4t)
y = -tx + 4t + 2t3
It passes through C(0, -6)
t3 + 2t + 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = -1

Centre of new circle = P(2t2, 4t) = P(2, -4)
Radius = PC {tex}=\sqrt{(2-0)^{2}+(-4+6)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of circle is:
(x - 2)2 + (y + 4) {tex}=(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 4x + 8y + 12 = 0

questions.question: Consider a circle with its centre lying on the focus of the parabola y^2 = 2px such that it touches the directrix of the parabola. Then a point of intersection of the circle and parabola is:
questions.question_raw: Consider a circle with its centre lying on the focus of the parabola y2 = 2px such that it touches the directrix of the parabola. Then a point of intersection of the circle and parabola is:
questions.explanation:
The focus of parabola y2 = 2px is {tex}\left(\frac{p}{2}, 0\right){/tex} and directrix x = -p/2

In the figure, we have supposed that p > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Centre of circle is {tex}\left(\frac{p}{2}, 0\right){/tex} and radius {tex}=\frac{p}{2}+\frac{p}{2}=p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The equation of circle is {tex}\left(x-\frac{p}{2}\right)^{2}+y^{2}=p^{2}{/tex}
For points of intersection of y2 = 2px ...(i)
and 4x2 + 4y2 - 4px - 3p2 = 0 ...(ii)
can be obtained by solving (i) and (ii) as follows
4x2 + 8px - 4px - 3p2 - 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2x + 3p) (2x - p ) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{-3 p}{2}, \frac{p}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = -3p2 (not possible), p2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm{/tex} p
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required points are ({tex}\frac p2{/tex}, p) and ({tex}\frac p2{/tex}, -p)

questions.question: Area (in sq. units) of the region outside $\frac{|x|}{2}+\frac{|y|}{3}=1$ and inside the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ is:
questions.question_raw: Area (in sq. units) of the region outside {tex}\frac{|x|}{2}+\frac{|y|}{3}=1{/tex} and inside the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of ellipse {tex}=\pi a b=\pi \times 2 \times 3=6 \pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area of the ellipse - 4 (Area of triangle OPQ)
{tex}=6 \pi-4\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 3\right){/tex}
= 6{tex}\pi{/tex} - 12 = 6({tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) sq. units

questions.question: The minimum area of triangle formed by the tangent to the $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ & coordinate axes is:
questions.question_raw: The minimum area of triangle formed by the tangent to the {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} & coordinate axes is:
questions.explanation:
Any tangent to the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1{/tex} at
P(a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) is {tex}\frac{x \cos \theta}{a}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{b}=1{/tex}

It meets co-ordinate axes at A (a sec {tex}\theta{/tex} ,0) and B (0, b cosec {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}OAB = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} a sec {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} b cosec {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a b}{\sin 2 \theta}{/tex}
For area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}OAB to be minimum, sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} should be maximum i.e., 1.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}OAB = ab sq. units.

questions.question: The locus of the orthocentre of the triangle formed by the lines (1 + p)x - py + p(1 + p) = 0, (1 + q)x - qy + q(1 + q) = 0, and y = 0, where p $\ne$ q, is
questions.question_raw: The locus of the orthocentre of the triangle formed by the lines (1 + p)x - py + p(1 + p) = 0, (1 + q)x - qy + q(1 + q) = 0, and y = 0, where p {tex}\ne{/tex} q, is
questions.explanation:
The triangle is formed by the lines
AB : (1 + p)x - py + p(1 + p) = 0
AC : (1 + q)x - qy + q (1 + q) = 0
BC : y = 0

So that the vertices of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC are
A(pq, (p + 1)(q + 1)), B(-p ,0) and C(-q, 0)
Let H(h, k) be the orthocentre of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC. Then as
AH {tex}\perp{/tex} BC and passes through A(pq, (p + 1)(p + 1))
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of AH is x = pq
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = pq ...(i)
{tex}\because{/tex} BH is perpendicular to AC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} m1m2 = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k-0}{h+p} \times \frac{1+q}{q}=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{p q+p} \times \frac{1+q}{q}=-1{/tex} [using (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = -pq ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we observe that h + k = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Locus of (h, k) is x + y = 0 , which is a straight line.

questions.question: Each of the four inequalities given below defines a region in the xy plane. One of these four regions does not have the following property. For any two points (x_1, y_1) and (x_2, y_2) in the region, the point $\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right)$ is also in the region. The inequality defining this region is
questions.question_raw: Each of the four inequalities given below defines a region in the xy plane. One of these four regions does not have the following property. For any two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in the region, the point {tex}\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right){/tex} is also in the region. The inequality defining this region is
questions.explanation:
1. x2 + 2y2 {tex}\le{/tex} 1represents the interior region of an ellipse where on taking any two points the midpoint of that segment will also lie inside that ellipse.
  2. Max {|x|, |y|} {tex}\le{/tex} 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x| {tex}\le{/tex} 1, |y| < 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 {tex}\le{/tex} x {tex}\le{/tex} 1 and -1 {tex}\le{/tex} y {tex}\le{/tex} 1
    which represents the interior region of a square with its sides x = {tex}\pm{/tex}1 and y = {tex}\pm{/tex}1 in which for any two points, their midpoint also lies inside the region.
  3. x - y2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 1 represents the exterior region of hyperbola in which if we take two points (2, 0) and (-2, 0) then their midpoint (0, 0) does not lie in the same region (as shown in the figure).
  4. y2 {tex}\le{/tex} x represents an interior region of the parabola in whichÂ any two points and their midpoint also lie inside the region.

questions.question:
The ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1$ with foci A and B is shown below in the figure. C is a point on the ellipse.
If the radius of the inscribed circle is 1, then area of $\Delta$ABC is
questions.question_raw:
The ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{4}=1{/tex} with foci A and B is shown below in the figure. C is a point on the ellipse.

If the radius of the inscribed circle is 1, then area of {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is
questions.explanation:
a2 = 9, b2 = 4
{tex}e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}=1-\frac{4}{9}=\frac{5}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2e2 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |AB| = {tex}2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
and |BC| + |CA| = 6
If r is the radius of inscribed circle, then
area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\frac{1}{2}r{/tex}[|AB| + |BC| + |CA|]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC = {tex}=\frac{1}{2}(1)[2 \sqrt{5}+6]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC {tex}=3+\sqrt{5}{/tex}
