questions.question: The area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}$ = 1 is:
questions.question_raw: The area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex} = 1 is:
questions.explanation:
Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}{/tex}Â = 1 as shown in the figure below:

The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. AÂ =Â 4xy which gives A2Â = 16x2y2Â =Â s (say)
Therefore, s =Â {tex}16 x^{2}\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}\right) \cdot b^{2}=\frac{16 b^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(a^{2} x^{2}-x^{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}=\frac{16 b^{2}}{a^{2}} \cdot\left[2 a^{2} x-4 x^{3}\right]{/tex}
Again,Â {tex}\frac{d s}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \text { and } y=\frac{b}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Now,Â {tex}\frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}=\frac{16 b^{2}}{a^{2}}\left[2 a^{2}-12 x^{2}\right]{/tex}
At x =Â {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d^{2} s}{d x^{2}}=\frac{16 b^{2}}{a^{2}}\left[2 a^{2}-6 a^{2}\right]{/tex}Â =Â {tex}\frac{16 b^{2}}{a^{2}}\left(-4 a^{2}\right)<0{/tex}
Thus at x =Â {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}, y=\frac{b}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}, s is the maximum
Hence the area A is maximum.
Maximum areaÂ = 4xy =Â {tex}4 \times\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} \times\frac{b}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}Â = 2ab sq. units

questions.question: Tangents drawn from origin to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2ax - 2by + b^2 = 0 are perpendicular to each other, if
questions.question_raw: Tangents drawn from origin to the circle x2 + y2 - 2ax - 2by + b2 = 0 are perpendicular to each other, if
questions.explanation: Tangents from origin to the circle will be perpendicular, if the circle touches both axes i.e., a = b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 = b2.

questions.question: The equation of the circle of radius 5 and touching the coordinate axes in third quadrant, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle of radius 5 and touching the coordinate axes in third quadrant, is
questions.explanation:
Since, the circle touches the co-ordinate axes in third quadrant.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The coordinates of the centre are (-5, -5) and radius is 5.
The equation of the circle is
(x + 5)2 + (y + 5)2 = 52

questions.question: Circles x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 8y - 4 = 0
questions.question_raw: Circles x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y = 0 and x2 + y2 - 8y - 4 = 0

options.text: cuts each other at two points

questions.explanation:
The centres and radius of given circles are
C1(1, 2), C2(0, 4), r1 = {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} and r2 = {tex}2\sqrt{5}{/tex}
Now, C1C2 = {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex} = |r2 -  r1|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Circles touch each other internally.

questions.question: The point P(10, 7) lies outside the circle x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 2y - 20 = 0. The greatest distance of P from the circle is
questions.question_raw: The point P(10, 7) lies outside the circle x2 + y2 - 4x - 2y - 20 = 0. The greatest distance of P from the circle is
questions.explanation:
Here, r = {tex}\sqrt{4+1+20}{/tex} = 5 and C {tex}\equiv{/tex} (2, 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CP {tex}=\sqrt{(10-2)^{2}+(7-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 10
Required maximum distance = 10 + 5 = 15

questions.question: If the circle x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 2y + k = 0 bisects the circumference of the circle x^2 + y^2 + 2x - 6y - 15 = 0, then k =
questions.question_raw: If the circle x2 + y2 + 6x - 2y + k = 0 bisects the circumference of the circle x2 + y2 + 2x - 6y - 15 = 0, then k =
questions.explanation:
The equation of the common chord of the given circles is
4x + 4y + (k + 15) = 0 ...(i)
Since, x2 + y2 + 6x - 2y + k = 0 bisects the circumference of x2 + y2 + 2x - 6y - 15 = 0.
Therefore, (i) is a diameter of the circle x2 + y2 + 2x - 6y - 15 = 0
whose centre is (-1, 3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4 (-1) + 4(3) + k + 15 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -23

questions.question: If a circle pass through (4, 0) and (0, 2) and center at y-axis then find the radius of the circle.
questions.question_raw: If a circle pass through (4, 0) and (0, 2) and center at y-axis then find the radius of the circle.
questions.explanation:
5 units
Equation of circle with center at x-axis (a, 0) and radius r units is

{tex} (x-a)^2+(y)^2=r^2 {/tex}
{tex} =>(2-a)^2+(0)^2=r^2 {/tex}

And {tex}(0-a)^2+(4)^2=r^2{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow>(a-2)^2=a^2+4^2=>(-2)(2 a-2)=16=>a-1=-4=>a=-3{/tex}

So, {tex}r^2=(2+3)^2=5^2{/tex}
{tex}r=5{/tex} units.

questions.question: If y = 2x is a chord of the circle x^2 + y^2 - 10x = 0, then the equation of the circle which has this chord as a diameter, is
questions.question_raw: If y = 2x is a chord of the circle x2 + y2 - 10x = 0, then the equation of the circle which has this chord as a diameter, is
questions.explanation:
Family of circles passing through the points of intersection of circle and chord is x2 + y2 - 10x + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(y - 2x) = 0.
Its centre is {tex}\left\{(5+\lambda),-\frac{\lambda}{2}\right\}{/tex}
It lies on y - 2x = 03
{tex}\therefore -\frac{\lambda}{2}-10-2 \lambda=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-5 \lambda=20 \Rightarrow \lambda=-4{/tex}
Hence, the equation of circle is x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y = 0

questions.question: If the line 3x + 4y - 1 = 0 touches the circle (x - 1)^2 + (y - 2)^2 = r^2, then the value of r will be
questions.question_raw: If the line 3x + 4y - 1 = 0 touches the circle (x - 1)2 + (y - 2)2 = r2, then the value of r will be
questions.explanation:
If the line 3x + 4y - 1 = 0 touches the circle (x - 1)2 + (y- 2)2 = r2, then the perpendicular distance from centre of circle on line is equal to the radius of circle.
{tex}\therefore \left|\frac{3+8-1}{5}\right|=r{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 2

questions.question: The equation of the circle passing through (1, 2) and the points of intersection of the circles x^2 + y^2 - 8x - 6y + 21 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 15 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle passing through (1, 2) and the points of intersection of the circles x2 + y2 - 8x - 6y + 21 = 0 and x2 + y2 - 2x - 15 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Equation of the required circle passing through the point of intersection of the given circles is
x2 + y2 - 8x - 6y + 21 + k(x2 + y2 - 2x - 15) = 0
Also, it passes through (1, 2).
{tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Equation of required circle is
x2 + y2 - 8x - 6y + 21 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(x2 + y2 - 2x - 15) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 6x - 4y + 9 = 0

questions.question: If the line y cos $\alpha$ = x sin $\alpha$ + a cos $\alpha$ be a tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = a^2, then
questions.question_raw: If the line y cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} = x sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} + a cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} be a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = a2, then
questions.explanation:
If the line x sin {tex}\alpha{/tex} - y cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} + a cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 is a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = a2, then
{tex}\left|\frac{\sin \alpha(0)-\cos \alpha(0)+a \cos \alpha}{\sqrt{\sin ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha}}\right|=a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}| = a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cos2{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1

questions.question: The points of intersection of the line 4x - 3y - 10 = 0 and the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 4y - 20 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The points of intersection of the line 4x - 3y - 10 = 0 and the circle x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y - 20 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
We have, 4x - 3y - 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{3 y+10}{4}{/tex}
Substituting the value of x in
x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y - 20 = 0, we get
{tex}\left(\frac{3 y+10}{4}\right)^{2}{/tex} + y2 - 2{tex}\left(\frac{3 y+10}{4}\right){/tex} + 4y - 20 = 0
25(y2 + 4y - 12) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 or -6
y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 and
y = -6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2
The points are (-2, -6) and (4, 2)

questions.question: The circle x^2 + y^2 = 4x + 8y + 5 intersects the line 3x - 4y = m at two distinct points, if
questions.question_raw: The circle x2 + y2 = 4x + 8y + 5 intersects the line 3x - 4y = m at two distinct points, if
questions.explanation:
Centre of circle is (2, 4) and radius is {tex}\sqrt{4+16+5}{/tex} = 5
Since, the line intersects the circle at two points.
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{6-16-m}{\sqrt{9+16}}\right|<5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |m + 10| < 25 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -25 < m + 10 < 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -35 < m < 15

questions.question: The line lx + my + n = 0 will be a tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = a^2 if
questions.question_raw: The line lx + my + n = 0 will be a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = a2 if
questions.explanation:
Line y = mx + c is a tangent, if c = {tex}\pm a \sqrt{1+m^{2}}{/tex}.
Now lx + my + n = 0 or {tex}y=-\frac{l}{m} x-\frac{n}{m}{/tex} is a tangent, if
{tex}-\frac{n}{m}=\pm a \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{l}{m}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
i.e., if n2 = a2(m2 + l2)

questions.question: If 2x - 4y = 9 and 6x -12y + 7 = 0 are the tangents of same circle, then its radius will be
questions.question_raw: If 2x - 4y = 9 and 6x -12y + 7 = 0 are the tangents of same circle, then its radius will be
questions.explanation:
We have,
2x - 4y = 9 and 6x - 12y = -7
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2x - 4y {tex}=\frac{-7}{3}{/tex}
Distance between two parallel lines
{tex}=\left|\frac{c_{1}-c_{2}}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=\left|\frac{9-\left(\frac{-7}{3}\right)}{\sqrt{(2)^{2}+(-4)^{2}}}\right|=\left|\frac{9+\frac{7}{3}}{\sqrt{4+16}}\right|=\left|\frac{\frac{34}{3}}{\sqrt{20}}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\left|\frac{34}{\sqrt{180}}\right|{/tex}
Since the tangents are parallel, therefore the distance between these two tangents will be their diameter
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Diameter {tex}=\frac{34}{\sqrt{180}}=\frac{17}{3 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius {tex}=\frac{17}{6 \sqrt{5}}{/tex}

questions.question: The equations of tangents to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 22x - 4y + 25 = 0 which are perpendicular to the line 5x + 12y + 8 = 0 are
questions.question_raw: The equations of tangents to the circle x2 + y2 - 22x - 4y + 25 = 0 which are perpendicular to the line 5x + 12y + 8 = 0 are
questions.explanation:
Centre of the given circle is (11, 2) and radius = {tex}\sqrt{121+4-25}{/tex} = 10
Equation of line perpendicular to
5x + 12y + 8 = 0 is 12x - 5y + k = 0.
Now, it is a tangent to the circle, if distance of a line from the centre of circle is equal to the radius of the circle.
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{12(11)-5(2)+k}{\sqrt{144+25}}\right|=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 8 or -252
Hence, the equations of tangents are 12x - 5y + 8 = 0 and 12x - 5y = 252

questions.question: The centres of a set of circles, each of radius 3, lie on the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25. The locus of any point in the set is
questions.question_raw: The centres of a set of circles, each of radius 3, lie on the circle x2 + y2 = 25. The locus of any point in the set is
questions.explanation:
Let h, k) be any point in the set, then equation of circle is (x - h)2 + (y - k)2 = 9
But (h, k) lies on x2 + y2 = 25, then h2 + k2 = 25
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 5 - 3 {tex}\le{/tex} Distance between the two circles {tex}\le{/tex} 5 + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \leq \sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}} \leq 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 {tex}\le{/tex} (h2 + k2) {tex}\le{/tex} 64
The locus of (h, k) is 4 {tex}\le{/tex} (x2 + y2) {tex}\le{/tex} 64

questions.question: The normal to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 3x - 6y - 10 = 0 at the point (-3, 4) is
questions.question_raw: The normal to the circle x2 + y2 - 3x - 6y - 10 = 0 at the point (-3, 4) is
questions.explanation:
The equation of normal to the circle
x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 at (x1, y1) is
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}+g}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}+f}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x+3}{-3-\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{y-4}{4-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 9y - 30 = 0

questions.question: The two points A and B in a plane are such that for all points P lying on a circle satisfy $\frac{\mathrm{PA}}{\mathrm{PB}}=k$, then k will not be equal to
questions.question_raw: The two points A and B in a plane are such that for all points P lying on a circle satisfy {tex}\frac{\mathrm{PA}}{\mathrm{PB}}=k{/tex}, then k will not be equal to
questions.explanation:
Place the coordinate axes such that the origin is the midpoint of segment AB and the x-axis is along with AB.
Let A = (a, 0), B = (-a, 0) and P = {tex}(\alpha, \beta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{PA}^{2}}{\mathrm{~PB}^{2}}=k^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (a - a)2 + {tex}\beta^{2}{/tex} = k2\(a + a)2 + {tex}\beta^{2}{/tex}]
The locus is
(x2 + y2)(1 - k2) - 2a(1 + k2)x + (1 - k2)a2 = 0
This is a circle for k {tex}\neq{/tex} 1.
Remark: In coordinate geometry, the choice of frame of reference is important. Here the choice of origin has simplified the calculation.

questions.question: The equation of the circle having centre (1, -2) and passing through the point of intersection of lines 3x + y = 14, 2x + 5y = 18 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle having centre (1, -2) and passing through the point of intersection of lines 3x + y = 14, 2x + 5y = 18 is

The point of intersection of 3x + y - 14 = 0 and 2x + 5y - 18 = 0 is (4, 2).
Centre of the circle is (1, -2).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} radius = {tex}\sqrt{(4-1)^{2}+(2+2)^{2}}=5{/tex}
The equation of the circle is
(x - 1)2 + (y + 2)2 = 52
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 2x + 4y - 20 = 0

questions.question: The area of the circle whose centre is at (1, 2) and which passes through the point (4, 6) is

questions.question: The equation of the circle which passes through (1, 0) and (0, 1) and has its radius as small as possible, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle which passes through (1, 0) and (0, 1) and has its radius as small as possible, is
questions.explanation:
Radius of the required circle is least when (1, 0) and (0, 1) are ends of a diameter.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Least radius {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and the centre {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
Required equation is
{tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - x - y = 0

questions.question: The equation of a circle with origin as centre and passing through the vertices of an equilateral triangle whose median is of length 3a is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a circle with origin as centre and passing through the vertices of an equilateral triangle whose median is of length 3a is
questions.explanation:
Since the triangle is equilateral.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The centroid of the triangle is the same as the circumcentre.
Radius of the circumcircle = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} (median) = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}(3a) = 2a
Hence, the equation of the circumcircle whose centre is at (0, 0) and radius 2a is x2 + y2 = (2a)2.

questions.question: The square of the length of the tangent from (3, -4) on the circle x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 6y + 3 = 0 is

questions.question: The equation of a tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 25 passing through (-2, 11) is
questions.question_raw: The equation of a tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = 25 passing through (-2, 11) is
questions.explanation:
Let the equation of the required tangent be y - 11 = m(x + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mx - y + 2m + 11 = 0 ...(i)
If (i) touches the circle x2 + y2 = 25, then the length of the perpendicular from the centre is equal to the radius
{tex}\therefore \frac{2 m+11}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}=\pm 5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 21m2 - 44m - 96 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow m=\frac{24}{7},-\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
Substituting the values of m in (i), the equations of the tangents are 4x + 3y = 25 and 24x - 7y + 125 = 0.

questions.question: Given the circles x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 5 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 6x - 2y + 6 = 0. Let P be a point $(\alpha, \beta)$ such that the lengths of the tangents from P to both the circles are equal, then

questions.question: The normal drawn at P (-1, 2) on the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 2y - 3 = 0 meets the circle at another point Q. Then the coordinates of Q are
questions.question_raw: The normal drawn at P (-1, 2) on the circle x2 + y2 - 2x - 2y - 3 = 0 meets the circle at another point Q. Then the coordinates of Q are

questions.question: If P and Q are the points of intersection of the circles x^2 + y^2 + 3x + 7y + 2p - 5 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 2y - p^2 = 0, then there is a circle passing through P, Q and (1, 1) for
questions.question_raw: If P and Q are the points of intersection of the circles x2 + y2 + 3x + 7y + 2p - 5 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 2x + 2y - p2 = 0, then there is a circle passing through P, Q and (1, 1) for

options.text: all except one value of p
all except two values of p

questions.explanation:
Equation of circle passing through the intersection of given circles is
(x2 + y2 + 3x + 7y + 2p - 5) + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(x + 5y + 2p - 5 + p2) = 0
Since it passes through (1, 1).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (7 + 2p) + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(1 + p)2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{7+2 p}{(1+p)^{2}}{/tex}
If p = -1, {tex}\lambda{/tex} does not exist.

questions.question: The equation of the circle whose two diameters have equations 2x + 3y = 3 and 16x - y = 4 and which passes through (4, 6), is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle whose two diameters have equations 2x + 3y = 3 and 16x - y = 4 and which passes through (4, 6), is
questions.explanation:
Solving 2x + 3y = 3 and 16x - y = 4, we get the centre = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{10}, \frac{4}{5}\right){/tex}.
The circle passes through (4, 6)
{tex}\Rightarrow r^{2}=\left(4-\frac{3}{10}\right)^{2}+\left(6-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{37}{10}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{26}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{4073}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The equation of the circle is
{tex}\left(x-\frac{3}{10}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{4}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{4073}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5(x2 + y2) - 3x - 8y = 200

The equation of the circle is
(x - 1)2 + (y + 1)2 = 52
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 2x + 2y - 23 = 0

questions.question: The locus of a point which moves so that the ratio of the length of the tangents to the circles x^2 + y^2 + 4x + 3 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 5 = 0 is 2 : 3 is
questions.question_raw: The locus of a point which moves so that the ratio of the length of the tangents to the circles x2 + y2 + 4x + 3 = 0 and x2 + y2 - 6x + 5 = 0 is 2 : 3 is
questions.explanation:
Let the point be (x1, y1)
According to the given condition,
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+4 x_{1}+3}}{\sqrt{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}-6 x_{1}+5}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+4 x_{1}+3}{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}-6 x_{1}+5}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 9 x_{1}^{2}+9 y_{1}^{2}{/tex} + 36x1 + 27 = {tex}4 x_{1}^{2}+4 y_{1}^{2}{/tex} - 24x1 + 20
{tex}\Rightarrow 5 x_{1}^{2}+5 y_{1}^{2}{/tex} + 60x1 + 7 = 0
Hence, the locus is 5x2 + 5y2 + 60x + 7 = 0

questions.question: Equations to the circles which touch the lines 3x - 4y + 1 = 0, 4x + 3y - 7 = 0 and pass through (2, 3) are
questions.question_raw: Equations to the circles which touch the lines 3x - 4y + 1 = 0, 4x + 3y - 7 = 0 and pass through (2, 3) are
questions.explanation:
Radius {tex}=\sqrt{(h-2)^{2}+(k-3)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (h - 1)2 + (k - 1)2 = 2[(h - 2)2 + (k - 3)2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 - 6h - 10k + 24 = 0 ...(i)
Let the slope of CP = m. The line CP makes 45° with L1 and L2. Then {tex}\left|\frac{\frac{3}{4}-m}{1+\frac{3 m}{4}}\right|=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = {tex}-\frac{1}{7}{/tex} or 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 7 ...{tex}\left[\because m \neq-\frac{1}{7}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C lies on the line y = 7x - 6.
Take C = (t, 7t - 6)
Substituting in (i), we get 5t2 - 16t + 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = 2 or {tex}\frac{5}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = (2, 8) or {tex}\left(\frac{6}{5}, \frac{12}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Respective radii = 5, 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required equations are (x - 2)2 + (y - 8)2 = 25 or (5x - 6)2 + (5y - 12)2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 + (y - 8)2 = 25 or 5x2 + 5y2 - 12x - 24y + 31 = 0

questions.question:
A circle S cuts three circles
x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 2y + 4 = 0
x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0
and x^2 + y^2 + 4x + 2y + 1 = 0 orthogonally. Then the radius of S is
questions.question_raw:
A circle S cuts three circles
x2 + y2 - 4x - 2y + 4 = 0
x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0
and x2 + y2 + 4x + 2y + 1 = 0 orthogonally. Then the radius of S is
questions.explanation:
Let S1 {tex}\equiv{/tex} x2 + y2 - 4x - 2y + 4 = 0
S2 {tex}\equiv{/tex} x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y + 1 = 0
S3 {tex}\equiv{/tex} x2 + y2 + 4x + 2y + 1 = 0
S1 - S2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 2y - 3 = 0 ...(i)
S2 - S3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x + 6y = 0 ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get the radical centre C = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \frac{-3}{4}\right){/tex}
Radius = length of the tangent from {tex}\left(\frac{3}{4}, \frac{-3}{4}\right){/tex} to the circle {tex}S_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{29}{8}}{/tex}

questions.question: The co-ordinates of the point from where the tangents are drawn to the circles x^2 + y^2 = 1, x^2 + y^2 + 8x + 15 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 10y + 24 = 0 are of same length, are
questions.question_raw: The co-ordinates of the point from where the tangents are drawn to the circles x2 + y2 = 1, x2 + y2 + 8x + 15 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 10y + 24 = 0 are of same length, are
questions.explanation:
The length of tangents is the same
i.e., {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{S}_{1}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{S}_{2}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{S}_{3}}{/tex}
We get the point from where the tangent is drawn,
by solving the 3 equations for x and y.
i.e., x2 + y2 = 1,
x2 + y2 + 8x + 15 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 10y + 24 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 8x + 16 = 0 and 10y + 25 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 and {tex}y=-\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Hence, the point is {tex}\left(-2,-\frac{5}{2}\right){/tex}

questions.question:
Let $\omega_{1}$ : x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 2y + 4 = 0
$\omega_{1}$ : x^2 + y^2 - 12x - 8y + 36 = 0
be two circles. Consider the following statements.
 The given circles intersect at two points.
 A common tangent has equation 4x + 3y = 16.
 If Q = (4, 0), then area of $\triangle$QC_1C_2 (in sq. units) = 5, where C_1 and C_2 are centres of $\omega_{1}$ and $\omega_{2}$.
Which of the statements are true?
questions.question_raw:
Let {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 4x - 2y + 4 = 0
{tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 12x - 8y + 36 = 0
be two circles. Consider the following statements.

  1. The given circles intersect at two points.
  2. A common tangent has equation 4x + 3y = 16.
  3. If Q = (4, 0), then area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}QC1C2 (in sq. units) = 5, where C1 and C2 are centres of {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex}.

Which of the statements are true?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : (x - 2)2 + (y - 1)2 = 1 ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C1 = (2, 1), radius = 1
{tex}\omega_{2}{/tex} : (x - 6)2 + (y - 4)2 = 16 ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C2 = (6, 4), radius = 4
IC1 C2| = {tex}\sqrt{(6-2)^{2}+(4-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 5 = sum of the radii
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Two circles touch externally.

The common tangent is the radical axis.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The equation of the common tangent is
8x + 6y = 32 ...[Subtracting (ii) from (i)]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4x + 3y = 16 ...(iii)
Q lies on common tangent.
Equation of C1 C2 is
3x - 4y - 2 = 0 ...(iv)
Solving (iii) and (iv), we get N = {tex}\left(\frac{14}{5}, \frac{8}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}|\mathrm{NQ} \mid=\sqrt{\left(\frac{14}{5}-4\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{8}{5}-0\right)^{2}}=2{/tex}
Area of {tex}\triangle {/tex}QC1C2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(5)(2) = 5

questions.question: A pair of adjacent vertices of a rectangle are (2, 4) and (2, 10). The line 2x + y = 21 is a diameter of circumscribing circle of the rectangle. The area (in sq. units) of rectangle is
questions.question_raw: A pair of adjacent vertices of a rectangle are (2, 4) and (2, 10). The line 2x + y = 21 is a diameter of circumscribing circle of the rectangle. The area (in sq. units) of rectangle is
questions.explanation:
Two vertices A and D are on line x = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The sides of the rectangle are parallel to the coordinate axes.
The midpoint M of AD = (2, 7)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point P (the centre) has ordinate 7.
P = (h, 7)
Point P lies on 2x + y = 21
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h + 7 = 21 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P = (7, 7) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |PM| = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |AB| = 10
Required Area = (10) (6) = 60

questions.question: Two circles intersect at A(2, 3) and B. The slope of their radical axis is $\frac{3}{4}$. BP and BQ are diameters of two circles. If the equation of PQ is ax + by + c = 0, then |a| + |b| + |c| is
questions.question_raw: Two circles intersect at A(2, 3) and B. The slope of their radical axis is {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}. BP and BQ are diameters of two circles. If the equation of PQ is ax + by + c = 0, then |a| + |b| + |c| is
questions.explanation:
AB is the radical axis of two circles.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Slope of AB {tex}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAP = {tex}\angle{/tex}QAB {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB {tex}\perp{/tex} PQ
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Slope of PQ {tex}=-\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
Point A lies on PQ as {tex}\angle{/tex}PAQ is a straight angle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of PQ passing through A (2, 3) is 4x + 3y - 17 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 4, b = 3, c = -17 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a| + |b| + |c| = 24

questions.question: L_1 : 2x + 3y - 6 = 0 and L_2 : kx + 4y - 12 = 0. If the points of intersection of L_1 and L_2 with coordinate axes are concyclic, then the value of k is
questions.question_raw: L1 : 2x + 3y - 6 = 0 and L2 : kx + 4y - 12 = 0. If the points of intersection of L1 and L2 with coordinate axes are concyclic, then the value of k is
questions.explanation:
A = (3, 0), B = (0, 2), C = {tex}\left(\frac{12}{k}, 0\right){/tex}, D = (0, 3)
Since A, B, C, D are concyclic.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Power of O w.r.t. the circle {tex}\omega{/tex}
= OA{tex}\cdot{/tex}OC = OB{tex}\cdot{/tex}OD
{tex}\Rightarrow(3)\left(\frac{12}{k}\right){/tex} = (2)(3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 6

questions.question: A common tangent to the circles x^2 + y^2 = 4 and (x - 3)^2 + y^2 = 1 is
questions.question_raw: A common tangent to the circles x2 + y2 = 4 and (x - 3)2 + y2 = 1 is
questions.explanation:
The centres of two circles are at O(0, 0) and (3, 0) and their radii are 2 and 1 unit respectively.
Clearly, OC = 2 + 1 i.e. distance between centres is equal to the sum of the radii of the circles.
So, two circles touch each other externally and one of the common tangents is given by S1 - S2 = 0 i.e., 6x - 12 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
The point P of the intersection of direct common tangents divides OC externally in the ratio 2 : 1. So, the coordinates of P are (6, 0).
The equation of a line through (6, 0) is
y = m(x - 6)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mx - y - 6m = 0 ...(i)
If it touches the circle x2 + y2 = 4, then
{tex}\left|\frac{0-0-6 m}{\sqrt{m^{2}+1}}\right|{/tex} = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m2 + 1 = 9m2
{tex}\Rightarrow m=\pm \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Hence, the equations of direct common tangents are
{tex}\pm \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} x-y \mp \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}=0{/tex} ...[From(i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \pm x-2 \sqrt{2} y \mp 6=0{/tex}

questions.question: $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are two circles passing through the points (0, a), (0, -a) and each touches the line y = mx + c. If $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ cut each other orthogonally, then
questions.question_raw: {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} are two circles passing through the points (0, a), (0, -a) and each touches the line y = mx + c. If {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} cut each other orthogonally, then
questions.explanation:
Let the centres of the circles be (h1, 0) and (h2, 0) and their radii be r1 and r2.
h1 and h2 satisfy h2 + a2 = r2
Also length of the perpendicular from the centre to the tangent = radius
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|m h+c|}{\sqrt{1+m^{2}}}=r{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (mh + c)2 = r2(1 + m2)
= (h2 + a2) (1 + m2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 - 2mhc + a2(1 + m2) - c2 = 0 ...(i)
h1 and h2 are the roots of equation (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h1 + h2 = 2mc and h1 h2 = a2(1 + m2) - c2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (h1 - h2)2 = 4m2c2 - 4 [a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} PQ2 = 4m2c2 - 4[a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}{/tex} = 4m2c2 - 4[a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow\left(h_{1}^{2}+a^{2}\right)+\left(h_{2}^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex}
= 4m2c2 - 4 [a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (h1 + h2)2 - 2h1h2 + 2a2
= 4m2c2 - 4 [a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4w2c2 - 2 [a2(1 + m2) - c2] + 2a2
= 4m2c2 - 4[a2(1 + m2) - c2]
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2[a2(1 + m2) - c2] + 2a2 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2a2 + a2m2 = c2 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} a2(2 + m2) = c2

questions.question: A and B are diametrically opposite points on a circle of radius r with centre at Q. If the tangents at A and B meet the tangent at P, at points C and D respectively, then PC$\cdot$PD is
questions.question_raw: A and B are diametrically opposite points on a circle of radius r with centre at Q. If the tangents at A and B meet the tangent at P, at points C and D respectively, then PC{tex}\cdot{/tex}PD is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQD and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BQD are congruent.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PQD = {tex}\angle{/tex}BQD = {tex}\beta{/tex} (say)
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PQC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}AQC are congruent.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}PQC = {tex}\angle{/tex}AQC = {tex}\alpha{/tex} (say)
Since {tex}\angle{/tex}AQB is a straight angle.
{tex}\therefore\alpha+\alpha+\beta+\beta=\pi \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Also, PQ {tex}\perp{/tex} CP {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CQP + {tex}\angle{/tex}QCP {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha+\angle \mathrm{QCP}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QCP = {tex}\beta{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\angle{/tex}PDQ = {tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}PQC and {tex}\Delta{/tex}DPQ are similar.
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{PC}}{\mathrm{QP}}=\frac{\mathrm{QP}}{\mathrm{PD}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PC, PD = (QP)2 = r2

questions.question: The line l_1 : a_1x + b_1y + c_1 = 0 intersects the circle $\omega_{1}$ : x^2 + y^2 + A_1x + B_1y + C_1 = 0 at P and Q. The line l_2 : a_2x + b_2y + c_2 = 0 intersects the circle $\omega_{2}$: x^2 + y^2 + A_2x + B_2y + C_2 = 0 at R and S. If the points P, Q, R, S are concyclic and the circles are not concentric, then
questions.question_raw: The line l1 : a1x + b1y + c1 = 0 intersects the circle {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : x2 + y2 + A1x + B1y + C1 = 0 at P and Q. The line l2 : a2x + b2y + c2 = 0 intersects the circle {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex}: x2 + y2 + A2x + B2y + C2 = 0 at R and S. If the points P, Q, R, S are concyclic and the circles are not concentric, then
questions.explanation:
Let the circle {tex}\omega{/tex} pass-through points P, Q, R, S.
The line l1 intersects the circle {tex}\omega_1{/tex}.
x2 + y2 + A1x + B1y + C1 + k1(a1x + b1y + c1) = 0 ...(i)
Also, the line l2 intersects the circle {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 + y2 + A2x + B2y + C2 + k2(a2x + b2y + c2) = 0 ...(ii)
Since, (i) and (ii) represent the same circle {tex}\omega{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A1 + k1a1 = A2 + k2a2, B1 + k1b1Â = B2 + k2b2, C1 + k1C1 = C2 + k2c2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} k1a1 - k2a2 + A1 - A2 = 0
k1b1 - k2b2 + B1 - B2 = 0
A1c1 - k2c2 + C1 - C2 = 0
The condition for consistency gives
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \mathrm{A}_{1}-\mathrm{A}_{2} & \mathrm{~B}_{1}-\mathrm{B}_{2} & \mathrm{C}_{1}-\mathrm{C}_{2} \\ a_{1} & b_{1} & c_{1} \\ a_{2} & b_{2} & c_{2} \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}

questions.question: The sum of the minimum distance and the maximum distance from the point (4, -3) to the circle x^2 + y^2 + 4x - 10y - 7 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The sum of the minimum distance and the maximum distance from the point (4, -3) to the circle x2 + y2 + 4x - 10y - 7 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Centre of the given circle = C(-2, 5)
Radius of the circle CN = CT {tex}=\sqrt{g^{2}+f^{2}-c}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{2^{2}+5^{2}+7}=\sqrt{36}{/tex} = 6
Now, PC {tex}=\sqrt{6^{2}+8^{2}}=\sqrt{100}{/tex} = 10

Join the external point (4, -3) to the centre of the circle (-2, 5).
Then PT is the minimum distance, from external point P to the circle and PN is the maximum distance.
Minimum distance = |PT| = |PC| - |CT| = 10 - 6 = 4
Maximum distance = |PN| = |PC| + |CN| = 10 + 6 = 16
So, sum of minimum and maximum distance = 16 + 4 = 20

questions.question: A circle $\omega$ touches the line y = x at a point P whose distance from the origin is $4 \sqrt{2}$. The point (10, -2) lies inside circle $\omega$. The length of the chord cut off by circle $\omega$ on the line x + y = 0 is $6 \sqrt{2}$. If the equation of the circle $\omega$ is x^2 + y^2 + ax + by + c = 0, then G.C.D. of (|ab|, c) is
questions.question_raw: A circle {tex}\omega{/tex} touches the line y = x at a point P whose distance from the origin is {tex}4 \sqrt{2}{/tex}. The point (10, -2) lies inside circle {tex}\omega{/tex}. The length of the chord cut off by circle {tex}\omega{/tex} on the line x + y = 0 is {tex}6 \sqrt{2}{/tex}. If the equation of the circle {tex}\omega{/tex} is x2 + y2 + ax + by + c = 0, then G.C.D. of (|ab|, c) is
questions.explanation:
Lines y = x and x + y = 0 are perpendicular to each other.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OACB is a rectangle.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AC = {tex}4 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAC
r2 = PA2 + CA2 = {tex}(3 \sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex} + {tex}(4 \sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex} = 50
Point B lies on the line y = x and |OB| = {tex}4 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = (4, 4)
BC {tex}\perp{/tex} to line y = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of BC = -1 = tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and sin{tex}\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}.
Express point C in parametric form to get
(h, k) = (4 + ({tex}-\sqrt{50}{/tex}) cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, 4 + ({tex}-\sqrt{50}{/tex}) sin {tex}\theta{/tex})
= (4 + 5, 4 - 5) = (9, -1)
Equation of circle is (x - 9)2 + (y + 1)2 = 50
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 18x + 2y + 32 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â a = -18, b = 2, c = 32
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} G.C.D. (36, 32) = 4
Remark:
The statement: â€˜The point (10, -2) lies inside circle {tex}\omega'{/tex}Â is used to fix the position of the circle and rejecting other possible positions.

questions.question_raw: Let the combined equation of a pair of tangents to a circle drawn from the origin O be xy - y2 = (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}) (x2 - xy). If the radius of the circle is 3 units and centre is in the first quadrant, then the length OA (where A is one of the points of contact) is
questions.explanation:
xy - y2 = (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}) (x2 - xy)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y(x - y) = (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}) x (x - y)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - y) [(2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex})x - y] = 0
The tangents are given by y = x and y = (2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex})x.

The slopes of the tangents are 1 and 2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AOM = 45Â° and {tex}\angle{/tex}BOM = 75Â°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BOA = 30Â° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}COA = 15Â°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan 15Â° {tex}=\frac{|\mathrm{CA}|}{|\mathrm{OA}|}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{OA}|=\frac{3}{\tan 15^{\circ}}=\frac{3}{2-\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3(2+\sqrt{3)}}{4-3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |OA| = 3(2 + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex})

questions.question: The circle x^2 + y^2 - 4x - 4y + 4 = 0 is inscribed in a triangle that has two of its sides along the coordinate axes. If the locus of the circumcentre of the triangle is x + y - xy + k$\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ = 0, then k =
questions.question_raw: The circle x2 + y2 - 4x - 4y + 4 = 0 is inscribed in a triangle that has two of its sides along the coordinate axes. If the locus of the circumcentre of the triangle is x + y - xy + k{tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 0, then k =
questions.explanation:
x2 + y2 - 4x - 4y + 4 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 2)2 + (y - 2)2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} centre = (2, 2), radius = 2

Let OAB be a variable triangle circumscribing the circle.
Let A = (a, 0), B = (0, b)
area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}OAB = area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}OSA + area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ASB + area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}BSO
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} a b=\frac{1}{2}\left[(a)(2)+2 \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}+(2)(b)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a b=2\left(a+b+\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}\right){/tex} ...(i)
Let M = (p, q) be the circumcentre.
{tex}\Rightarrow p=\frac{a}{2}, q=\frac{b}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2p, b = 2q
Substituting the values of a and b in (i), we get
4pq = 2[2(p + q) + 2{tex}\sqrt{p^{2}+q^{2}}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (p, q) satisfies xy = x + y + {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â x + y - xy + {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1

questions.question: A stick of length l rests against the floor and a wall of a room. If the stick begins to slide on the floor, then the locus of its middle point is
questions.question_raw: A stick of length l rests against the floor and a wall of a room. If the stick begins to slide on the floor, then the locus of its middle point is
questions.explanation:
Let P(h, k) be the middle point of stick AB.
Then, P(h, k) {tex}=\left(\frac{a+0}{2}, \frac{0+b}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{b}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow h=\frac{a}{2}, k=\frac{b}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2h, b = 2k
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}AOB, OA2 + OB2 = AB2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = l2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2h)2 + (2k)2 = l2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 = {tex}\frac{l^{2}}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The locus of middle point P(h, k) is x2 + y2 = {tex}\frac{l^{2}}{4}{/tex} which is the equation of a circle.

questions.question: Find the equation of circle which pass through (5, 9) and center at (2, 5).
questions.question_raw: Find the equation of circle which pass through (5, 9) and center at (2, 5).
questions.explanation:
Equation of circle with center at {tex}(a, b){/tex} and radius {tex}r{/tex} units is
{tex} (x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2 {/tex}
{tex} (5-2)^2+(9-5)^2=r^2=>r^2=3^2+4^2=>r=5 . {/tex}
So, equation of circle is {tex}(x-2)^2+(y-5)^2=5^2=>x^2+y^2-4 x-10 y+4=0{/tex}.

questions.question: If the straight line ax + by = 2; a, b $\ne$ 0 touches the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x = 3 and is normal to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 4y = 6, then the values of a and b are respectively
questions.question_raw: If the straight line ax + by = 2; a, b {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 touches the circle x2 + y2 - 2x = 3 and is normal to the circle x2 + y2 - 4y = 6, then the values of a and b are respectively
questions.explanation:
Given equations of circles are x2 + y2 - 2x - 3 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} centre is (1, 0) and radius is 2 and x2 + y2 - 4y - 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre is (0, 2) and radius is {tex}\sqrt{10}{/tex}
Since, line ax + by = 2 touches the first circle
{tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{a(1)+b(0)-2}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}\right|{/tex} = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow(a-2)=2 \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex} ...(i)
Also the given line is normal to the second circle. Hence, it will pass through the centre of the second circle.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a(0) + b(2) = 2 or 2b = 2 or b = 1
Putting this value in equation (i), we get
a - 2 = 2{tex}\sqrt{a^{2}+1^{2}}{/tex} or (a - 2)2 = 4(a2 + 1)
or a2 + 4 - 4a = 4a2 +4 or 3a2 + 4a = 0
or a(3a + 4) = 0 or a = 0, {tex}-\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Values of a and b are {tex}\left(-\frac{4}{3}, 1\right){/tex} respectively according to the given choices.

questions.question: The equation of the circle whose centre is (3, -1) and which cuts off a chord of length 6 on the line 2x - 5y + 18 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle whose centre is (3, -1) and which cuts off a chord of length 6 on the line 2x - 5y + 18 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Let AB be the chord cut off by the circle on the line 2x - 5y + 18 = 0.
Let CD be the perpendicular drawn from centre (3, -1) to AB.
{tex}|C D|=\left|\frac{2(3)-5(-1)+18}{\sqrt{2^{2}+(-5)^{2}}}\right|=\sqrt{29}{/tex} and |AD| = 3
(CA)2 = (AD)2 + (CD)2 = 32 + {tex}(\sqrt{29})^{2}{/tex} = 38
The equation of the circle is
(x - 3)2 + (y + 1)2 = 38

questions.question: The equation of the circle which touches x-axis at (3, 0) and passes through (1, 4) is given by

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} centre of the circle is (3, k).
Now, CA2 = CB2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3 - 3)2 + (k - 0)2
= (3 - 1)2 + (k - 4)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â k2 = 4 + k2 - 8k + 16
{tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Required equation is
{tex}(x-3)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Â x2 + y2 - 6x - 5y + 9 = 0

questions.question: Find the radius of the circle with equation x^2+y^2-4x-10y+4=0.
questions.question_raw: Find the radius of the circle with equation x2+y2-4x-10y+4=0.
questions.explanation:
Comparing the equation with general form {tex}x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0{/tex}, we get
{tex} 2 g=-4 \Rightarrow g=-2 {/tex}
{tex} 2 f=-10 \Rightarrow f=-5 {/tex}
{tex} c=4 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Radius }=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-c}=\sqrt{4+25-4}=5 . {/tex}

questions.question: The point (1, 4) lies inside the circle $\omega$ having equation x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 8y + k = 0, where k is an arbitrary constant. The circle $\omega$ neither touches the coordinate axes nor cuts them. The possible values of k are between
questions.question_raw: The point (1, 4) lies inside the circle {tex}\omega{/tex} having equation x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y + k = 0, where k is an arbitrary constant. The circle {tex}\omega{/tex} neither touches the coordinate axes nor cuts them. The possible values of k are between
questions.explanation:
Radius {tex}=\sqrt{9+16-k}=\sqrt{25-k}{/tex}
radius > |PQ| {tex}\Leftrightarrow \sqrt{25-k}>2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 25 - k > 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k < 21 ...(i)
Also, radius < 3 {tex}\Leftrightarrow \sqrt{25-k}<3{/tex}Â {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}> 25 - k < 9
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k > 16 ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get 16 < k < 21

questions.question: AB and CD are perpendicular chords of a circle of radius R meeting at point E. Then the expression EA^2 + EB^2 + EC^2 + ED^2 equals
questions.question_raw: AB and CD are perpendicular chords of a circle of radius R meeting at point E. Then the expression EA2 + EB2 + EC2 + ED2 equals
questions.explanation:
Place E at the origin and chords AB and CD along y -axis and x -axis respectively.
Let A = (0, y1), B = (0, y2), C = (x1, 0), D = (x2, 0)
Then the required expression {tex}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+y_{2}^{2}{/tex}
Let the equation of the circle be
x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 ...(i)
Substituting y = 0 in (i), we get
x2 + 2gx + c = 0.
The roots of the equation are abscissae of points C and D.
x1 + x2 = -2g and x1x2 = c
Substituting x = 0 in (i), we get
y2 + 2fy + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 + y2 = -2f and y1y2 = c
{tex}\therefore x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}+y_{2}^{2}{/tex} = (x1 + x2)2 + (y1 + y2)2 - 2x1x2 - 2y1y2
= 4g2 + 4f2 - 4c
= 4(R2)

questions.question: Area of the equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle x^2 + y^2 - 7x + 9y + 5 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: Area of the equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle x2 + y2 - 7x + 9y + 5 = 0 is

Side of the triangle = AB = 2AD = 2rcos 30Â°
{tex}=2 r \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\sqrt{3} r{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}(side)2 {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(\sqrt{3} r)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \times 3 \times \frac{110}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{165}{8} \sqrt{3}{/tex} square units

questions.question: The equations of the circles that touch the lines x + y = $2 \sqrt{2}$, x + y = $-2 \sqrt{2}$ and the circle x^2 + y^2 = 1 are x^2 + y^2 $\pm$ ax $\mp$ by + c = 0, then a^2 + b^2 - c is
questions.question_raw: The equations of the circles that touch the lines x + y = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}, x + y = {tex}-2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} and the circle x2 + y2 = 1 are x2 + y2 {tex}\pm{/tex} ax {tex}\mp{/tex} by + c = 0, then a2 + b2 - c is
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}\omega{/tex} be the circle given by
x2 + y2 + ax - by + c = 0.

Let P be the centre of the circle {tex}\omega{/tex}, which lies on line y = -x.
Let P = (h, -h).
|OM| = radius of the circle {tex}\omega{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius {tex}=\frac{|0+0-2 \sqrt{2}|}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 2
|OP| = |OQ| + |QP|
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{h^{2}+h^{2}}{/tex} = 1 + 2 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow h=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Centre of the circle {tex}\omega=\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
The equation of the circle is
{tex}\left(x-\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(y+\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}=4{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 3{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} (x - y) + 5 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 - c = 18 + 18 - 5 = 31

questions.question: Let L_1 be a straight line passing through the origin and L_2 be the straight line x + y = 1. If the intercepts made by the circle x^2 + y^2 - x + 3y = 0 on L_1 and L_2 are equal, then which of the following equations can represent L_1?
questions.question_raw: Let L1 be a straight line passing through the origin and L2 be the straight line x + y = 1. If the intercepts made by the circle x2 + y2 - x + 3y = 0 on L1 and L2 are equal, then which of the following equations can represent L1?
questions.explanation:
QT = RN
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PQ = PT
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left|\frac{m}{2}+\frac{3}{2}\right|}{\sqrt{1+m^{2}}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
Squaring and simplifying,
7m2 - 6m - 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (7m + 1)(m - 1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m = 1, {tex}-\frac{1}{7}{/tex}
So possible equations of L1 are x - y = 0 or x + 7y = 0

questions.question: A variable tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 at P cuts the coordinate axes at A and B. The path of midpoint M of AB is given by
questions.question_raw: A variable tangent to the circle x2 + y2 = r2 at P cuts the coordinate axes at A and B. The path of midpoint M of AB is given by
questions.explanation:
The equation of the tangent at P ({tex}\theta{/tex}) is x cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + y sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = r
Substituting y = 0, we get A = (r sec {tex}\theta{/tex}, 0)
Substituting x = 0, we get B = (0, r cosec {tex}\theta{/tex})
Let M = (h, k), then
{tex}h=\frac{r}{2} \sec \theta, k=\frac{r}{2} \operatorname{cosec} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{r}{2 h}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{r}{2 k}\right)^{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} M (h, k) satisfies
{tex}r^{2}\left(\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{y^{2}}\right)=4{/tex}

questions.question: A tangent PT is drawn to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4 at the point P$(\sqrt{3}, 1)$. A straight line L, perpendicular to PT is a tangent to the circle (x - 3)^2 + y^2 = 1. A possible equation of L is
questions.question_raw: A tangent PT is drawn to the circle x2 + y2 = 4 at the point P{tex}(\sqrt{3}, 1){/tex}. A straight line L, perpendicular to PT is a tangent to the circle (x - 3)2 + y2 = 1. A possible equation of L is
questions.explanation:
The equation of tangent at {tex}P(\sqrt{3}, 1){/tex} to the circle x2 + y2 = 4 is {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}x + y = 4.
Its slope is {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The slope of a line perpendicular to PT is {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
The equation of tangent of slope {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} to the circle (x- 3)2 + (y - 0)2 = 12 is x -Â {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}y + c = 0
|CQ| = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|3+c|}{\sqrt{1+3}}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + c = {tex}\pm{/tex}2
The required equations are x - {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}y = 1
or x - {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}y = 5

questions.question: The area of triangle formed by the tangent, normal drawn at (1, $\sqrt 3$) to the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4 and the positive x-axis, is
questions.question_raw: The area of triangle formed by the tangent, normal drawn at (1, {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex}) to the circle x2 + y2 = 4 and the positive x-axis, is
questions.explanation:
The equations of the tangents and normal to x2 + y2 = 4 at (1, {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}) are x + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}y - 4 = 0 and y = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}x respectively.

Hence, the required area {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \sqrt{3}=2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: The equation of the circle $\omega_{1}$ which touches the circle $\omega$ : x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 6y + 17 = 0 externally and which has the lines x^2 - 3xy - 3x + 9y = 0 as normals, is x^2 + y^2 + ax + by + c = 0. The value of $\left|\frac{a b}{c}\right|$ is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} which touches the circle {tex}\omega{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 6x + 6y + 17 = 0 externally and which has the lines x2 - 3xy - 3x + 9y = 0 as normals, is x2 + y2 + ax + by + c = 0. The value of {tex}\left|\frac{a b}{c}\right|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
x2 - 3xy - 3x + 9y = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x - 3y)(x - 3) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The normals are x = 3, x = 3y and their point of intersection is the centre of the circle {tex}\omega_1{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C1 of circle {tex}\omega_1{/tex} = (3, 1)
{tex}\omega{/tex} : (x - 3)2 + (y + 3)2 = 1, centre C2 = (3, -3)

|C1C2| = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Radius of circle {tex}\omega_1{/tex} = |C1C2| - 1 = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of {tex}\omega_1{/tex} is
(x - 3)2 + (y - 1)2 = 9
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - 6x - 2y + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = -6, b = -2, c = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow\left|\frac{a b}{c}\right|{/tex} = 12

questions.question: Tangents drawn from the point (4, 3) to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y = 0 are inclined at an angle
questions.question_raw: Tangents drawn from the point (4, 3) to the circle x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y = 0 are inclined at an angle
questions.explanation:
Centre of the circle C = (1, 2)
Radius = |CT| = {tex}\sqrt{5}{/tex}
Let P = (4, 3)
{tex}|\mathrm{PC}|=\sqrt{(4-1)^{2}+(3-2)^{2}}=\sqrt{10}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{PT}|=\sqrt{10-5}=\sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \triangle{/tex}PCT is an isosceles triangle
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Angle between tangents from P = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The tangents at B(1, 7) and D(4, -2) to the circle $\omega$ : x^2 + y^2 - 2x - 4y - 20 = 0 meet at C. If the centre of the circle is A, then the area of $\square$ABCD (in sq. units) is
questions.question_raw: The tangents at B(1, 7) and D(4, -2) to the circle {tex}\omega{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 2x - 4y - 20 = 0 meet at C. If the centre of the circle is A, then the area of {tex}\square{/tex}ABCD (in sq. units) is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\omega{/tex} : (x - 1)2 + (y - 2)2 = (5)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre A = (1, 2), radius = 5
Slope of AD {tex}=\frac{2-(-2)}{1-4}=-\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
Slope of AB is not defined and equation of AB is given by x = 1.
Equation of CD is
3x - 4y - 20 = 0 ...(i)
Equation of BC is y = 7 ...(ii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get C = (16, 7)
Area of {tex}\square{/tex}ABCD = 2 (area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC)
{tex}=2\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 15\right){/tex} = 75

questions.question: From any point on the circle x^2 + y^2 = a^2 tangents are drawn to the circle x^2 + y^2 = a^2sin^2 $\alpha$, the angle between them is
questions.question_raw: From any point on the circle x2 + y2 = a2 tangents are drawn to the circle x2 + y2 = a2sin2 {tex}\alpha{/tex}, the angle between them is
questions.explanation:
Given two circles are concentric.
Let {tex}\angle \mathrm{OPQ}=\theta{/tex}
QP = a cos{tex}\alpha{/tex}
tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a \sin \alpha}{a \cos \alpha}{/tex} = tan {tex}\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Angle between tangents = {tex}\angle \mathrm{QPR}=2 \alpha{/tex}

questions.question: The acute angle between the circles $\omega_{1}$ : x^2 + y^2 - 4x + 6y + 11 = 0 and $\omega_{2}$ : x^2 + y^2 - 2x + 8y + 13 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The acute angle between the circles {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 4x + 6y + 11 = 0 and {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 2x + 8y + 13 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} : (x - 2)2 + (y + 3)2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C1 = (2, -3), radius = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\omega_{2}{/tex} : (x - 1)2 + (y + 4)2 = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C2 = (1, -4), radius = 2
Observe that C2 lies on {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex}

Let the circles {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex} intersect at P and Q.
Angle between the circles
= angle between the tangents at the point of intersection
= angle between the normals
PC2 = radius of {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex} = normal at P to {tex}\omega_{2}{/tex}
PC1 = radius of {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex} = normal at P to {tex}\omega_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required angle {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\angle{/tex}C2 PC1
Also |C1C2| {tex}=\sqrt{(2-1)^{2}+(4-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
and |PC1| = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} and |PC2| = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta{/tex}PC1C2 is a right angled triangle, where {tex}\angle{/tex}C1 = 90Â°
{tex}\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=1 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: A chord AB drawn from the point A(0, 3) on circle x^2 + 4x +(y - 3)^2 = 0 meets to M in such a way that AM = 2AB, then the locus of point M will be
questions.question_raw: A chord AB drawn from the point A(0, 3) on circle x2 + 4x +(y - 3)2 = 0 meets to M in such a way that AM = 2AB, then the locus of point M will be
questions.explanation:
Let M be (h, k).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Point B is {tex}\left(\frac{h}{2}, \frac{k+3}{2}\right){/tex}
It lies on the given circle.

{tex}\therefore \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2}+4\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)+\left(\frac{k+3}{2}-3\right)^{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h^{2}}{4}+\frac{8 h}{4}+\frac{(k-3)^{2}}{4}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h2 + 8h + k2 - 6k + 9 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + 8x - 6y + 9 = 0, which is a circle.

questions.question: If OA and OB be the tangents to the circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x - 8y + 21 = 0 drawn from the origin O, then |AB| =
questions.question_raw: If OA and OB be the tangents to the circle x2 + y2 - 6x - 8y + 21 = 0 drawn from the origin O, then |AB| =
questions.explanation:
Here, the equation of AB (chord of contact) is
0 + 0 - 3(x + 0) - 4(y + 0) + 21 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + 4y - 21 = 0 ...(i)

Now, CM = perpendicular distance from (3, 4) to line (i) is
{tex}\frac{3 \times 3+4 \times 4-21}{\sqrt{9+16}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}
and AM {tex}=\sqrt{\mathrm{AC}^{2}-\mathrm{CM}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{4-\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{2}{5} \sqrt{21}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AB = 2AM {tex}=\frac{4}{5} \sqrt{21}{/tex}

questions.question: In $\triangle$ABC, AB = 3, BC = 4, CA = 5. Semicircles are drawn outwardly on AB and BC as diameters. PQ is a common tangent to two semicircles. The length of PQ is
questions.question_raw: In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC, AB = 3, BC = 4, CA = 5. Semicircles are drawn outwardly on AB and BC as diameters. PQ is a common tangent to two semicircles. The length of PQ is
questions.explanation:
The dimensions of the sides of the triangle suggest that the triangle is a right triangle. Place the triangle so that B is at origin, A on the y-axis and C on the x-axis.
|PQI = IP'Q'I and |Q'R| = |RQ| - |QQ'|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |Q'R| = |RQ| - |PP'| {tex}=2-\frac{3}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
and |P'R| {tex}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+(2)^{2}}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
|P'Q'| {tex}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{P}^{\prime} \mathrm{R}\right)^{2}-\left(\mathrm{RQ}^{\prime}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{24}{4}}=\sqrt{6}{/tex}

questions.question: Two circles touch each other externally and their radii are integers R_1 and R_2, R_1 > R_2. If the length of a common tangent is $2 \sqrt{35}$, then the number of pairs (R_1, R_2) is
questions.question_raw: Two circles touch each other externally and their radii are integers R1 and R2, R1 > R2. If the length of a common tangent is {tex}2 \sqrt{35}{/tex}, then the number of pairs (R1, R2) is
questions.explanation:
|QM| = {tex}2 \sqrt{35}{/tex}, |PM| = R1 - R2, |PQ| = R1 + R2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (R1 + R2)2 - (R1 - R2)2 = 4(35)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4R1R2 = 4(35)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R1R2 = 35
The required pairs are (35, 1), (7, 5).

questions.question: If circles x^2 + y^2 + 2ax + c = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 2by + c = 0 touch each other, then
questions.question_raw: If circles x2 + y2 + 2ax + c = 0 and x2 + y2 + 2by + c = 0 touch each other, then
questions.explanation:
Distance between the centres = Sum of the radii
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}-c}+\sqrt{b^{2}-c}{/tex}
Squaring on both sides, we get
{tex}c=\sqrt{\left(a^{2}-c\right)\left(b^{2}-c\right)}{/tex}
Again squaring, we get
(a2 + b2) c = a2b2
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}=\frac{1}{c}{/tex}

questions.question: If p and q represent the lengths of a direct common tangent and a transverse common tangent respectively to the circles $\omega_1$ : x^2 + y^2 + 14x - 4y + 28 = 0 and $\omega_2$ : x^2 + y^2 - 14x + 4y - 28 = 0, then $\frac{p}{q}$ is
questions.question_raw: If p and q represent the lengths of a direct common tangent and a transverse common tangent respectively to the circles {tex}\omega_1{/tex} : x2 + y2 + 14x - 4y + 28 = 0 and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} : x2 + y2 - 14x + 4y - 28 = 0, then {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\omega_1{/tex} : (x + 7)2+ (y - 2)2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C1 = (-7, 2) radius = 5
{tex}\omega_2{/tex} : (x - 7)2 + (y + 2)2 = 81
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Centre C2 = (7, -2), radius = 9
|C1C2| {tex}=\sqrt{(7+7)^{2}+(-2-2)^{2}}=\sqrt{212}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |C1 C2| > sum of the radii
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Circles are apart.

Length of transverse common tangent = |AB|
= |C1M|
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}\right)^{2}-16}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{212-16}=\sqrt{196}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 14

Length of transverse common tangent = |PN|
= |C2C1'|
{tex}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}\right)^{2}-(9+5)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{212-196}=\sqrt{16}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{p}{q}=\frac{14}{4}=\frac{7}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: a, b > 0. The length of the common chord of the circles (x - a)^2 + y^2 = a^2 and x^2 + (y - b)^2 = b^2 is
questions.question_raw: a, b > 0. The length of the common chord of the circles (x - a)2 + y2 = a2 and x2 + (y - b)2 = b2 is
questions.explanation:
Let |OM| = l
Express area of triangle OPQ in two ways and equate
{tex}\frac{1}{2} a b=\frac{1}{2} l \sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow l=\frac{a b}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}{/tex}
Length of common chord = 2l {tex}=\frac{2 a b}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: Two circles intersect at A(2, 3) and B. The slope of their radical axis is $\frac{3}{4}$. BP and BQ are the diameters of the circles. If the slope of PQ is $\frac{a}{b}$, then |a + b| is
questions.question_raw: Two circles intersect at A(2, 3) and B. The slope of their radical axis is {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}. BP and BQ are the diameters of the circles. If the slope of PQ is {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex}, then |a + b| is
questions.explanation:
AB is the radical axis.
Slope of AB {tex}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of PQ = {tex}-\frac{4}{3}=\frac{a}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a + b| = |-4 + 3| = 1

questions.question: Let $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ be two circles given by $\omega_1$ : x^2 + y^2 + 2x + 3y + 1 = 0 and $\omega_2$ : x^2 + y^2 + 4x + 3y + 2 = 0. The equation of the circle on the common chord of $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ as a diameter is x^2 + y^2 + ax + by + c = 0. Then abc is
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} be two circles given by {tex}\omega_1{/tex} : x2 + y2 + 2x + 3y + 1 = 0 and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} : x2 + y2 + 4x + 3y + 2 = 0. The equation of the circle on the common chord of {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} as a diameter is x2 + y2 + ax + by + c = 0. Then abc is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\omega_1{/tex} : x2 + y2 + 2x + 3y + 1 = 0 ...(i)
{tex}\omega_2{/tex} : x2 + y2 + 4x + 3y + 2 = 0 ...(ii)

Equation of radical axis of circles {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} is 2x + 1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Putting {tex}x=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} in (i), we get y2 + 3y + {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 4y2 + 12y + 1 = 0 ...(iii)
Let {tex}\mathrm{A}=\left(-\frac{1}{2}, y_{1}\right), \mathrm{B}=\left(-\frac{1}{2}, y_{2}\right),{/tex}
then (iii) gives
y1 + y2 = {tex}-\frac{12}{4}{/tex} = -3 and y1y2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y1 - y2)2 = (y1 + y2)2 - 4y1y2 = 9 - 4{tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex} = 8
|AB| = diameter of the circle
|AB| = |y1 - y2| = {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} radius = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
and centre M{tex}=\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, \frac{y_{1}+y_{2}}{2}\right)=\left(-\frac{1}{2},-\frac{3}{2}\right){/tex}
Equation of the circle is {tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}=2{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + x + 3y + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 0
a = 1, b = 3, {tex}c=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a b c=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question: The line l : x + 2y = 10 touches a circle $\omega$ at (2, 4). The equation of circle $\omega$ if it passes through the point (1, -3) is x^2 + y^2 + ax + by + c = 0. Then, 20(a + b) - 3c equals
questions.question_raw: The line l : x + 2y = 10 touches a circle {tex}\omega{/tex} at (2, 4). The equation of circle {tex}\omega{/tex} if it passes through the point (1, -3) is x2 + y2 + ax + by + c = 0. Then, 20(a + b) - 3c equals
questions.explanation:
Equation of point circle P(2, 4) is
(x - 2)2 + (y - 4)2 = 0
Circle {tex}\omega{/tex} passes through the point of intersection of P and line l.
{tex}\omega{/tex} : (x - 2)2 + (y - 4)2 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} (x + 2y - 10) = 0 ...(i)
Since, {tex}\omega{/tex} passes through point Q(1, -3).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Substituting x = 1, y = -3 in (i), we get
1 + 49 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(1 - 6 - 10) = 0: {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{10}{3}{/tex}
Substituting {tex}\lambda=\frac{10}{3}{/tex} in (i), we get
3(x - 2)2 + 3(y - 4)2 + 10(x + 2y - 10) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 3x2 + 3y2 - 2 x - 4y - 40 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}-\frac{2}{3} x-\frac{4}{3} y-\frac{40}{3}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow a=-\frac{2}{3}, b=-\frac{4}{3}, c=-\frac{40}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20(a + b) - 3c = 20(-2) + 40 = 0

questions.question: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral, where AB, BC, CD are represented by x + y - 1 = 0, 2x + y + 1 = 0 and x - 3y + 5 = 0 respectively. If DA passes through (2, 1), then the equation of DA is
questions.question_raw: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral, where AB, BC, CD are represented by x + y - 1 = 0, 2x + y + 1 = 0 and x - 3y + 5 = 0 respectively. If DA passes through (2, 1), then the equation of DA is
questions.explanation:
Circle through A, B, C, D is given by
(mx - y + c) (2x + y + 1) + {tex}\lambda{/tex}(x + y - 1){tex}\cdot{/tex}Â (x - 3y + 5) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} coefficient of x2 = coefficient of y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2m + {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1 - 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{-(1+2 m)}{4}{/tex} ...(i)
Also, coefficient of (xy) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} m - 2 - 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{m-2}{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{-(1+2 m)}{4}=\frac{m-2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2m - 4 = -1 - 2m
{tex}\Leftrightarrow m=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
The equation of DA passing through (2, 1) is 3x - 4y - 2 = 0

questions.question: If two circles pass through A(1, 0), B(2, -1) and the y axis is a common tangent, then the possible radii of the circles are
questions.question_raw: If two circles pass through A(1, 0), B(2, -1) and the y axis is a common tangent, then the possible radii of the circles are
questions.explanation:
Slope of AB {tex}=\frac{0+1}{1-2}{/tex} = -1
Equation of AB is x + y - 1 = 0 ...(v = 0)
Equation of a circle with AB as a diameter is
(x - 2) (x - 1) + y(y + 1) = 0 ...(u = 0)
Required circles pass through the points of intersection of u = 0 and v = 0, and are given by u + kv = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x2 + (k - 3)x + y2 + (1 + k)y + 2 - k = 0
Since, y axis is a tangent to both the circles.
Therefore, x coordinate of the centre = radius
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{3-k}{2}\right)^{2}=k-2+\left(\frac{k-3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{k+1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} k2 + 6k - 7 = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (k + 1)(k - 7) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1, 7
When k = -1,Â radius {tex}=\sqrt{-3+\left(\frac{-1-3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{-1+1}{2}\right)^{2}}=1{/tex}
When k = 7,Â radius {tex}=\sqrt{5+\left(\frac{7-3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{7+1}{2}\right)^{2}}=5{/tex}

questions.question: Tangents drawn from a point P to meet the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 at A and B. If $\angle$APB = 60Â°, then the locus of point P is
questions.question_raw: Tangents drawn from a point P to meet the circle x2 + y2 = r2 at A and B. If {tex}\angle{/tex}APB = 60°, then the locus of point P is
questions.explanation:
{tex}\triangle{/tex}PAO and {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBO are congruent.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OPB = {tex}\angle{/tex}OPA = 30Â°
{tex}\frac{|\mathrm{OA}|}{|\mathrm{OP}|}{/tex}Â = sin 30oÂ {tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{OP}|=\frac{r}{\frac{1}{2}}=2 r{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}=2 r{/tex}Â {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 4r2

questions.question: A circle is inscribed in an equilateral triangle of side a, the area of any square inscribed in the circle is
questions.question_raw: A circle is inscribed in an equilateral triangle of side a, the area of any square inscribed in the circle is
questions.explanation:
Let r be the radius. Then {tex}3\left(\frac{1}{2} r a\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{a}{2 \sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} diameter = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
Let x be the side length of the square.
Then,
x2 + x2 = {tex}\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{6}{/tex}

questions.question: A variable circle $\omega $ having a fixed radius r passes through the origin and cuts the axes in A, B. If OP $\perp$ AB, then point P traces
questions.question_raw: A variable circle {tex}\omega {/tex} having a fixed radius r passes through the origin and cuts the axes in A, B. If OP {tex}\perp{/tex} AB, then point P traces
questions.explanation:
Let A = (a, 0), B = (0, b), then
a2 + b2 = 4r2 ...(i)
and {tex}\frac{x_{1}}{a}+\frac{y_{1}}{b}=1{/tex} ...(ii)
OP {tex}\perp{/tex} AB {tex}\Rightarrow\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)\left(\frac{y_{1}}{x_{1}}\right)=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow b=\frac{a x_{1}}{y_{1}}{/tex} ...(iii)
From (ii) and (iii), we get,
{tex}\frac{x_{1}}{a}+\frac{v_{1}^{2}}{a x_{1}}=1 \Leftrightarrow \frac{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}{x_{1}}=a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b=\frac{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}{y_{1}}{/tex}
Substituting a, b in (i), we get
{tex}\left(\frac{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}{x_{1}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}}{y_{1}}\right)^{2}=4 r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}\right)^{3}=4 r^{2} x_{1}^{2} y_{1}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P traces a curve (x2 + y2)3 = 4r2 x2 y2

questions.question: A variable circle $\omega$ passes through a fixed point A(p, q) and is tangent to x-axis. The point diametrically opposite to A lies on
questions.question_raw: A variable circle {tex}\omega{/tex} passes through a fixed point A(p, q) and is tangent to x-axis. The point diametrically opposite to A lies on
questions.explanation:
Let point B(x1, y1) be opposite to point A.
Equation of the circle is
(x - x1) (x - p) + (y - y1) (y - q) = 0
Put y = 0 (as x axis is a tangent to the circle)
x2 - (x1 + p)x + (px1 + qy1) = 0
{tex}\triangle{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x1 + p)2 - 4(px1 + qy1) = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x12 + p2 - 2px1) - 4qy1 = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (x1 - p)2 = 4qy1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1, y1) satisfies (x - p)2 = 4qy

questions.question: Let the line segment joining the centres of the circles x^2 - 2x + y^2 = 0 and x^2 + y^2 + 4x + 8y + 16 = 0 intersect the circles at P and Q respectively. Then the equation of the circle with PQ as its diameter is
questions.question_raw: Let the line segment joining the centres of the circles x2 - 2x + y2 = 0 and x2 + y2 + 4x + 8y + 16 = 0 intersect the circles at P and Q respectively. Then the equation of the circle with PQ as its diameter is
questions.explanation:
The centres of two circles are C1(1, 0) and C2(-2, -4) and their radii are 1 and 2 units respectively.
Let C be the centre of the required circle.
Then, CP = CQ = 1.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} CC1 = 2 and CC2 = 3.
Clearly, C divides C1 C2 in the ratio 2 : 3.
Therefore, coordinates of C are
{tex}\left(\frac{-4+3}{2+3}, \frac{-8+0}{2+3}\right)=\left(-\frac{1}{5},-\frac{8}{5}\right){/tex}.
Hence, the equation of the required circle is
{tex}\left(x+\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(y+\frac{8}{5}\right)^{2}=1^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x2 + 5y2 + 2x + 16y + 8 = 0

questions.question: The area of the triangle formed by joining the origin to the points of intersection of the line $x \sqrt{5}$ + 2y = $3 \sqrt{5}$ and circle x^2 + y^2 = 10 is
questions.question_raw: The area of the triangle formed by joining the origin to the points of intersection of the line {tex}x \sqrt{5}{/tex} + 2y = {tex}3 \sqrt{5}{/tex} and circle x2 + y2 = 10 is
questions.explanation:
Length of perpendicular from origin to the line {tex}x \sqrt{5}+2 y=3 \sqrt{5}{/tex} is

{tex}\mathrm{OL}=\left|\frac{3 \sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{5})^{2}+2^{2}}}\right|=\frac{3 \sqrt{5}}{3}=\sqrt{5}{/tex}
Radius of the given circle {tex}=\sqrt{10}{/tex} = |OQ| = |OP|
|PQ| = 2 QL {tex}=2 \sqrt{\mathrm{OQ}^{2}-\mathrm{OL}^{2}}=2 \sqrt{10-5}=2 \sqrt{5}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}OPQ {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{PQ} \times \mathrm{OL}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{5} \times \sqrt{5}{/tex}Â = 5

questions.question: ABCD is a square, the length of whose side is a. Taking AB and AD as the coordinate axes, the equation of the circle passing through the vertices of the square is
questions.question_raw: ABCD is a square, the length of whose side is a. Taking AB and AD as the coordinate axes, the equation of the circle passing through the vertices of the square is
questions.explanation:
According to the figure, A(0, 0), B(a, 0), C(a, a) and D(0, a).
The centre is {tex}\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right){/tex}.
The equation of the circle is {tex}\left(x-\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{a^{2}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 - ax - ay = 0

questions.question: The point $(1,4)$ lie ________ the circle $x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y+2=0$.
questions.question_raw: The point {tex}(1,4){/tex} lie ________ the circle {tex}x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y+2=0{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
outside circle
Circle has equation {tex}x^2+y^2-2 x-4 y+2=0{/tex}
. {tex}1^2+4^2-2 * 1-4 * 4+2=1+16-2-16+2=1>0{/tex} so, point is outside the circle.

questions.question: A circle $\omega$ is given by x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0. AB is a variable chord of $\omega$ subtending a right angle at the origin O. If OP $\perp$ AB, then P traces
questions.question_raw: A circle {tex}\omega{/tex} is given by x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0. AB is a variable chord of {tex}\omega{/tex} subtending a right angle at the origin O. If OP {tex}\perp{/tex} AB, then P traces
questions.explanation:
Let P = (x1, y1)
PA and PB are signed distances.
PA . PB = -OP2
{tex}=-\left(x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} ...(i)
Also, power of P = PA {tex}\cdot{/tex} PB
{tex}=x_{1}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}{/tex} + 2gx1 + 2fy1 + c ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 x_{1}^{2}+2 y_{1}^{2}{/tex} + 2gx1 + 2fy1 + c = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P satisfies x2 + y2 + gx + fy + {tex}\frac{c}{2}{/tex} = 0,
which represents a circle.

questions.question: A point P moves such that the sum of the squares of its distances from the sides of a given square is a constant. Then point P moves on
questions.question_raw: A point P moves such that the sum of the squares of its distances from the sides of a given square is a constant. Then point P moves on

options.text: a circle with centre as the centre of the square
a circle passing through the centre of the square
a circle with centre as one of the vertices of the square

questions.explanation:
Let â€˜aâ€™ be the side length of the square and the moving point P be (h, k).
Let the square be placed such that one vertex is at the origin and two adjacent sides lie along the coordinate axes. Then according to the given condition,
h2 + (h - a)2 + k2 + (k - a)2 = constant = c2 (say)
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} h2 + k2 - a(h + k) + a2 -Â  {tex}\frac{c^{2}}{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (h, k) satisfies x2 + y2 - xa - ya + a2 -{tex}\frac{c^{2}}{2}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Leftrightarrow\left(x-\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(y-\frac{a}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{c^{2}-a^{2}}{2}{/tex} ...(i)
Equation (i) represents a circle with centre at {tex}\left(\frac{a}{2}, \frac{a}{2}\right){/tex}, which is same as the centre of the square.

questions.question: The equation of the circle passing through the origin and cutting intercepts of length 3 and 4 units from the positive axes, is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the circle passing through the origin and cutting intercepts of length 3 and 4 units from the positive axes, is
questions.explanation:
Given, |OA| = 3 and |OB| = 4
{tex}\therefore \Rightarrow{/tex} |OL| = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} and |CL| = 2
OC2 = {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} + 22 {tex}=\frac{25}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\mathrm{OC}|=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}

The centre of the circle is {tex}\left(\frac{3}{2}, 2\right){/tex} and radius = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}.
The equation of the circle is

x2 + y2 - 3x - 4y = 0

questions.question: From a variable point P, tangents are drawn one each to the two circles x^2 + y^2 = a^2, x^2 + y^2 = b^2, a > b. If the tangents are mutually perpendicular, then P describes a
questions.question_raw: From a variable point P, tangents are drawn one each to the two circles x2 + y2 = a2, x2 + y2 = b2, a > b. If the tangents are mutually perpendicular, then P describes a
questions.explanation:
Let P = (h, k)
Let the perpendicular tangents be PB and PA with slopes m and {tex}-\frac{1}{m}{/tex} respectively.
The equation of PB is y - k = m(x - h)
The perpendicular distance of PB from origin O = |OB|
{tex}\Leftrightarrow b=\frac{|m h-k|}{\sqrt{1+m^{2}}}{/tex}Â {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (mh - k)2 = b2(1 + m2) ...(i)
Similarly, OA {tex}\perp{/tex} PA gives {tex}a=\frac{|m k+h|}{\sqrt{1+m^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (mk + h)2 = (1 + m2)a2 ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
(1 + m2) (h2 + k2) = (1 + m2) (a2 + b2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(h, k) satisfies x2 + y2 = a2 + b2 which represents a circle with radius {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Let A = (-1, 0) and D = (0, -1). Two points B and C are such that points A, B, C, D are concyclic. Given that AB and CD are parallel and AB has equation x - y + 1 = 0.
If B = (p, 2) and C = (r, 5), then r - s - p is
questions.question_raw:
Let A = (-1, 0) and D = (0, -1). Two points B and C are such that points A, B, C, D are concyclic. Given that AB and CD are parallel and AB has equation x - y + 1 = 0.
If B = (p, 2) and C = (r, 5), then r - s - p is
questions.explanation:
Equation of AD : x + y + 1 = 0 ...(i)
and equation of AB : x - y + 1 = 0 . ..(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB {tex}\perp{/tex} AD
Points A, B, C, D concyclic and AB || DC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cyclic quadrilateral ABCD is a rectangle
Point B(p, 2) satisfies (ii) the equation of AB
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 1
Equation of CD : x - y - 1 = 0 ...(iii)
Point C satisfies (iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r - s = 1 ...(iv)
Slope of BC = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{s-2}{r-1}=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r + s = 3 ...(v)
Solving (iv) and (v), we get r = 2, s = 1
Required expression = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0

questions.question: The normals drawn at P(t_1) and Q(t_2) to y^2 = 4ax meet the curve again at a point. The tangents at P and Q meet at R. As P and Q vary, the circumcentre of $\Delta$PQR describes
questions.question_raw: The normals drawn at P(t1) and Q(t2) to y2 = 4ax meet the curve again at a point. The tangents at P and Q meet at R. As P and Q vary, the circumcentre of {tex}\Delta{/tex}PQR describes

options.text: a line parallel to the y - axis
a line parallel to the x - axis

questions.explanation:
P = ({tex}a t_{1}^{2}{/tex}, 2at1), Q = ({tex}a t_{2}^{2}{/tex}, 2at2)
Then R = (at1t2, a(t1 + t2))
The normals drawn at P and Q meet the curve at S. Let S = ({tex}a t_{3}^{2}{/tex}, 2at3),
Then t3 = -t - {tex}\frac{2}{t}{/tex} where t = t1 or t2
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} t2 + t.t3 + 2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t1 + t2 = -t3 and t1t2 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R = (2a, -at3)
Clearly {tex}\square{/tex}PROS is a cyclic quadrilateral,
{tex}\angle{/tex}RPS = {tex}\angle{/tex}RQS = 90Â°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RS is the diameter of the circumcircle of {tex}\square{/tex}PRQS (This circle is same as the circumcircle of {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQR.)
Let the circumcentre be C(h, k).
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Circumcentre C = Midpoint of RS
{tex}\Leftrightarrow(h, k)=\left(\frac{2 a+a t_{3}^{2}}{2}, \frac{-a t_{3}+2 a t_{3}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h = 2a + {tex}a t_{3}^{2}{/tex}, 2k = at3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2h = 2a + {tex}a\left(\frac{2 k}{a}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2k2 = a(h - a)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The circumcentre C moves on a parabola given by 2y2 = a(x - a)

questions.question: Consider the parabola y^2 = 4ax. If the normal at a point P on the parabola meets the curve again at a point Q and the least distance of point Q from the tangent at the vertex of the parabola is 16, then a is
questions.question_raw: Consider the parabola y2 = 4ax. If the normal at a point P on the parabola meets the curve again at a point Q and the least distance of point Q from the tangent at the vertex of the parabola is 16, then a is
questions.explanation:
Let P = (at2, 2at)
The normal at P meets the curve again at Q, then
{tex}\mathrm{Q}=\left(a\left(-t-\frac{2}{t}\right)^{2}, 2 a\left(-t-\frac{2}{t}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}=\left(a\left(t^{2}+\frac{4}{t^{2}}+4\right),-2 a\left(t+\frac{2}{t}\right)\right){/tex}
The distance of Q from the tangent at the vertex
= The abscissa of point Q
{tex}=a\left(t^{2}+\frac{4}{t^{2}}+4\right){/tex}
{tex}=a\left[\left(t-\frac{2}{t}\right)^{2}+4+4\right] \geq 8 a{/tex}
Minimum distance = 8a = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 2

questions.question: The equation of the parabola whose focus is the point (0, 0) and the tangent at the vertex is x - y + 1 = 0 is
questions.question_raw: The equation of the parabola whose focus is the point (0, 0) and the tangent at the vertex is x - y + 1 = 0 is
questions.explanation:
Let focus be S(0, 0) and A is the vertex of parabola. Take any point Z such that AS = AZ.
Given tangent at vertex is x - y + 1 = 0.
Since directrix is parallel to the tangent at the vertex.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Equation of directrix is x - y + {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0, where
{tex}\lambda{/tex} is constant.
{tex}\because{/tex} A is the midpoint of SZ,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SZ = 2.SA
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{|0-0+\lambda|}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}=2 \times \frac{|0-0+1|}{\sqrt{1^{2}+(-1)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda|{/tex} = 2 i.e. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2
Directrix in this case always lies in IInd quadrant
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda{/tex} = 2
Hence, equation of directrix is x - y + 2 = 0
Now, P be any point on parabola
{tex}\therefore{/tex} SP = PM {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} SP2 = PM2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0)2 + (y - 0)2 {tex}=\left(\frac{|x-y+2|}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 + 2xy - 4x + 4y - 4 = 0
